Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity. It can be negative, resulting in a decrease in speed, or positive, resulting in an increase in speed. A particle can have zero velocity but non-zero acceleration at an instant.

The kinematic equations of motion are applicable for one-dimensional motion with constant acceleration. The equations involve algebraic quantities that can be positive or negative, and the values must be substituted with proper signs.
The definitions of instantaneous velocity and acceleration are exact and always correct. However, the kinematic equations are true only for motion with constant acceleration.
A body can be considered a point object if its size is negligible compared to the distance it travels. This approximation is useful for simplifying complex motion problems.
Position-time graphs can be used to analyze motion and determine properties such as distance, speed, and time. The graph can provide information about the relative positions and speeds of objects.

Retardation is the opposite of acceleration, and it can be uniform. The retardation of an object can be calculated using the equation of motion, and it can be used to determine the time it takes for an object to stop.

When a projectile is thrown upwards, its initial velocity determines its maximum height and time of flight. The direction of the velocity and acceleration can be determined using the equation of motion.

Instantaneous velocity is the velocity of an object at a specific instant in time. It is a vector quantity, with both magnitude and direction. The instantaneous velocity of an object can be different from its average velocity over a given time period.

Average speed is defined as the total path length divided by the time interval, while average velocity is the displacement divided by the time interval. The two quantities can be different, with average speed being greater than or equal to the magnitude of average velocity.
An object can have non-zero acceleration at an instant when its velocity is zero. This occurs when the object is changing direction, such as at the highest point of a projectile's motion.
A speed-time graph can be used to represent the motion of an object over time. The graph can show the object's speed, acceleration, and changes in direction.

concepts.concept_title: Magnitude of Displacement and Path Length
concepts.description: The magnitude of displacement is the shortest distance between an object's initial and final positions, while the path length is the total distance traveled by the object. The two quantities can be different, with the path length being greater than or equal to the magnitude of displacement.
Instantaneous speed and velocity are always equal in magnitude, as they both represent the rate of change of an object's position at a specific instant in time.

Relative motion is the motion of an object as observed from a different frame of reference. This concept is crucial in understanding the motion of particles and objects in various situations. The relative speed of two objects is the speed at which they are moving with respect to each other.

Graphs can be used to analyze the motion of particles and objects. Different types of graphs, such as x-t plots and v-t plots, provide valuable information about the motion of particles, including their position, velocity, and acceleration.

Average speed is the total distance traveled by an object divided by the time taken, while average velocity is the displacement of an object divided by the time taken. The sign of average velocity depends on the direction of motion.

Simple harmonic motion is a type of periodic motion where the acceleration of an object is proportional to its displacement from the mean position. The signs of position, velocity, and acceleration variables can be determined from the x-t plot of the motion.

Average acceleration is the change in velocity of an object divided by the time taken. The average acceleration can be determined from the v-t plot of the motion, and its sign depends on the direction of motion.
Quantities with magnitude only, specified completely by a single number and unit. Examples include distance, mass, temperature, and time.
Quantities with both magnitude and direction. Vectors can be added, subtracted, and multiplied, and are used to describe physical quantities like velocity and acceleration.
A vector can be represented by an arrow in space, with the length of the arrow representing the magnitude and the direction of the arrow representing the direction of the vector.
A vector that represents the change in position of an object from one point to another, with the tail at the initial point and the tip at the final point.
A vector quantity has both magnitude and direction, and it obeys the triangle law of addition or parallelogram law of addition. Vectors can be represented by bold face type or an arrow over a letter.
Displacement vector is a vector quantity that represents the straight line joining the initial and final positions of an object, and it does not depend on the actual path undertaken by the object.

Two vectors are equal if they have the same magnitude and the same direction. Equality of vectors can be checked by shifting one vector parallel to itself until its tail coincides with the other vector.
Position vector is a vector that represents the position of an object in a plane, with respect to a chosen origin. It is represented by a straight line joining the origin to the object.
Vectors can be represented by bold face type or an arrow over a letter. The magnitude of a vector is often called its absolute value, indicated by |v| = v.
Vectors that can be displaced parallel to themselves without changing their magnitude or direction. They have no fixed line of application.
Vectors for which the location or line of application is important. They have a fixed line of application and cannot be displaced parallel to themselves.
When a vector is multiplied by a scalar, its magnitude is scaled by the scalar, and its direction remains the same if the scalar is positive. If the scalar is negative, the direction is reversed.

A graphical method of adding vectors, where the tail of the second vector is placed at the head of the first vector, and the resultant vector is drawn from the tail of the first vector to the head of the second vector.
When two vectors are added, the resultant vector is the third side of a triangle formed by the two vectors, with the magnitude and direction of the resultant vector being the sum of the two vectors.
A vector with zero magnitude and no direction, often represented by the symbol 0. It has no physical significance but is used as a reference point in vector operations.

The commutative law of vector addition states that the order of adding two vectors does not change the result. Mathematically, this is expressed as A + B = B + A. This means that the resultant vector is the same regardless of the order in which the vectors are added.

The associative law of vector addition states that when three vectors are added, the result is the same regardless of which two vectors are added first. Mathematically, this is expressed as (A + B) + C = A + (B + C). This means that the order in which the vectors are grouped does not affect the final result.

A null vector, also known as a zero vector, is a vector with zero magnitude and no direction. It is denoted by 0 and has the property that A + 0 = A and λ0 = 0, where λ is a scalar. The null vector is the result of adding two equal and opposite vectors or multiplying a vector by zero.

The subtraction of vectors can be defined in terms of the addition of vectors. The difference of two vectors A and B is defined as A - B = A + (-B), where -B is the negative of vector B. This means that subtracting vector B from vector A is equivalent to adding the negative of vector B to vector A.

The parallelogram method is a graphical method of adding two vectors. It involves drawing the two vectors with their tails at a common origin, then drawing a line from the head of one vector parallel to the other vector, and another line from the head of the other vector parallel to the first vector. The resultant vector is the diagonal of the parallelogram formed by these lines.

The triangle method is a graphical method of adding two vectors. It involves drawing the two vectors head to tail, then drawing a line from the tail of the first vector to the head of the second vector. The resultant vector is this line.
The process of adding two or more vectors to obtain a resultant vector. Vectors can be added geometrically or algebraically, and the resultant vector is the sum of the individual vectors.
The process of breaking down a vector into its component vectors along a set of axes or directions. This can be done using unit vectors or by multiplying the vector by real numbers.
Vectors with a magnitude of 1 and a specific direction, used to specify direction only. Unit vectors along the x-, y-, and z-axes are denoted by i, j, and k, respectively.
A system of axes used to specify the position of a point in space. The x-, y-, and z-axes are mutually perpendicular, and unit vectors along these axes are used to resolve vectors into components.
The components of a vector along a set of axes or directions. These components can be positive, negative, or zero, depending on the angle between the vector and the axis.
A vector can be represented by its magnitude and direction, or by its components along the x and y axes. The vector can be written as A = |A|nˆ, where nˆ is a unit vector along A.

A vector can be resolved into its components along the x and y axes by drawing lines from the head of the vector perpendicular to the axes. The components can be found using the equations A = Axcosθ and A = Aysinθ.
Vectors can be added in two dimensions by finding the sum of their components along the x and y axes. The resultant vector can be found using the equations R = Ax + Bx and R = Ay + By.

Vectors can be added in three dimensions by finding the sum of their components along the x, y, and z axes. The resultant vector can be found using the equations Rx = Ax + Bx, Ry = Ay + By, and Rz = Az + Bz.

A position vector is a vector that describes the position of an object in space. It can be written as r = xi + yj + zk, where x, y, and z are the components of the position vector along the x, y, and z axes.

concepts.concept_title: Magnitude of a Vector in Three Dimensions
concepts.description: The magnitude of a vector in three dimensions can be found using the equation A = √(Ax^2 + Ay^2 + Az^2), where Ax, Ay, and Az are the components of the vector along the x, y, and z axes.
The graphical method of vector addition is a technique used to add vectors by combining their respective components. It helps in visualizing the vectors and the resultant vector.

concepts.concept_title: Law of Cosines for Vector Addition
concepts.description: The Law of Cosines states that the magnitude of the resultant vector R is given by R = √(A² + B² + 2AB cos θ), where A and B are the magnitudes of the vectors and θ is the angle between them.

concepts.concept_title: Law of Sines for Vector Addition
concepts.description: The Law of Sines states that the ratio of the magnitude of a vector to the sine of its angle is equal to the ratio of the magnitude of the resultant vector to the sine of its angle.
The position vector r of a particle is a vector that points from the origin to the particle's location. The displacement ∆r is the change in position vector, given by ∆r = r′ - r.
The parallelogram method of vector addition is a technique used to add two vectors by constructing a parallelogram with the vectors as its sides.
Average velocity of an object is the ratio of displacement and corresponding time interval. It is a vector quantity and its direction is the same as that of displacement.
Instantaneous velocity is the limiting value of average velocity as the time interval approaches zero. It is a vector quantity and its direction is tangential to the path of the object.
Velocity can be expressed in component form, with x and y components. The magnitude of velocity is given by the square root of the sum of the squares of its components.
Acceleration is the limiting value of average acceleration as the time interval approaches zero. It is a vector quantity and can be expressed in component form.
Acceleration can be expressed in component form, with x and y components. The magnitude of acceleration is given by the square root of the sum of the squares of its components.

Average acceleration of an object is defined as the change in velocity divided by the time interval. It can be expressed as a = ∆v / ∆t, where ∆v is the change in velocity and ∆t is the time interval.
Instantaneous acceleration is the limit of average acceleration as the time interval approaches zero. It is the rate of change of velocity at a particular instant.
Velocities in different directions can be added using the triangle law of vector addition. The resultant velocity is the vector sum of the individual velocities.

concepts.concept_title: Motion in a Plane with Constant Acceleration
concepts.description: When an object moves in a plane with constant acceleration, its velocity changes at a constant rate. The velocity at any time can be found using the equation v = v0 + at, where v0 is the initial velocity and a is the acceleration.
Velocity and acceleration vectors can have any angle between 0° and 180°. The direction of the acceleration vector is the same as the direction of the change in velocity.

concepts.concept_title: Equation of Motion in Two Dimensions
concepts.description: The equation of motion in two dimensions can be represented as r = r0 + vt + (1/2)at^2, where r is the position vector, r0 is the initial position, v is the initial velocity, a is the acceleration, and t is time. This equation can be broken down into component forms for x and y directions.

concepts.concept_title: Independence of Horizontal and Vertical Motions
concepts.description: The motion of a projectile can be considered as the result of two separate, simultaneously occurring components of motions: one along a horizontal direction without acceleration and the other along the vertical direction with constant acceleration due to gravity.

The equation for projectile motion can be expressed as x = x0 + v0x*t and y = y0 + v0y*t - (1/2)*g*t^2, where x0 and y0 are the initial positions, v0x and v0y are the initial velocities in the x and y directions, g is the acceleration due to gravity, and t is time.

The velocity of an object in two dimensions can be found by taking the derivative of the position vector with respect to time, resulting in v = dx/dt * i + dy/dt * j, where i and j are unit vectors in the x and y directions.

The speed of an object in two dimensions can be calculated using the magnitude of the velocity vector, given by the equation speed = sqrt(vx^2 + vy^2), where vx and vy are the components of velocity in the x and y directions.

concepts.concept_title: Equation of Path of a Projectile
concepts.description: The equation of the path of a projectile is given by y = (tan θ)x - gx^2 / (2(v cosθ)^2), which represents a parabola. This equation is derived by eliminating time between the expressions for x and y.
The time taken by a projectile to reach its maximum height is given by t_m = v sinθ / g, where v is the initial velocity, θ is the angle of projection, and g is the acceleration due to gravity.

The total time during which a projectile is in flight, also known as the time of flight, is given by T = 2(v sinθ) / g, where v is the initial velocity, θ is the angle of projection, and g is the acceleration due to gravity.
The maximum height reached by a projectile is given by h = (v^2 sin^2θ) / (2g), where v is the initial velocity, θ is the angle of projection, and g is the acceleration due to gravity.

The horizontal distance travelled by a projectile from its initial position to the point where it hits the ground is called the horizontal range, given by R = (v^2 sin2θ) / g, where v is the initial velocity, θ is the angle of projection, and g is the acceleration due to gravity.
The components of the initial velocity of a projectile are given by v_x = v cosθ and v_y = v sinθ, where v is the initial velocity and θ is the angle of projection.

concepts.concept_title: Components of Velocity at Time t
concepts.description: The components of the velocity of a projectile at time t are given by v_x = v cosθ and v_y = v sinθ - gt, where v is the initial velocity, θ is the angle of projection, g is the acceleration due to gravity, and t is time.

The motion of an object under the influence of gravity, where the object is launched with an initial velocity at an angle to the horizontal. The range, maximum height, and time of flight can be calculated using equations of motion.

concepts.concept_title: Equations of Motion for Projectile Motion
concepts.description: Equations that describe the motion of a projectile, including the range, maximum height, and time of flight. These equations are used to solve problems involving projectiles.
The horizontal distance traveled by a projectile, which depends on the initial velocity, angle of launch, and acceleration due to gravity.
The maximum vertical distance reached by a projectile, which depends on the initial velocity, angle of launch, and acceleration due to gravity.

concepts.concept_title: Time of Flight of a Projectile
concepts.description: The time it takes for a projectile to complete its motion, which depends on the initial velocity, angle of launch, and acceleration due to gravity.
The motion of an object in a circular path at a constant speed, where the velocity is changing continuously in direction.

Angular displacement is the angle through which an object moves in a circular path. It is measured in radians. The angular displacement is denoted by Δθ and is defined as the ratio of the arc length to the radius of the circle.

Angular speed is the rate of change of angular displacement. It is measured in radians per second. The angular speed is denoted by ω and is defined as the ratio of the angular displacement to the time taken.

Linear speed is the speed of an object moving in a circular path. It is measured in meters per second. The linear speed is denoted by v and is defined as the product of the angular speed and the radius of the circle.

Centripetal acceleration is the acceleration of an object moving in a circular path. It is measured in meters per second squared. The centripetal acceleration is denoted by a_c and is defined as the product of the angular speed squared and the radius of the circle.

Uniform circular motion is the motion of an object moving in a circular path with a constant speed. The acceleration of the object is directed towards the center of the circle and is constant in magnitude.
An external force is needed to move a stationary body or stop a moving body. However, it is unclear if an external force is required to maintain uniform motion.
Aristotle believed that an external agency is always required to maintain motion. Galileo disproved this by considering a frictionless environment.
Galileo studied motion of objects on an inclined plane, where moving down accelerates, moving up retards, and horizontal motion is uniform.
A body at rest remains at rest and a body in uniform motion continues to move with uniform velocity unless acted upon by an external force.

concepts.concept_title: Newton's First Law of Motion (Law of Inertia)
concepts.description: A body at rest or in uniform motion will continue in its state unless acted upon by an external force, illustrating the law of inertia.
The force acting on a body is equal to the rate of change of its momentum.
For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.
The total momentum of a closed system remains constant over time.
A body or system is said to be in equilibrium when the net force acting on it is zero.

Galileo experimented with a ball on a double inclined plane to understand motion. He observed that the final height of the ball is nearly the same as its initial height in an ideal situation with no friction.

An external force is required to keep a body in motion, as observed in a moving toy car. The opposing forces such as friction always present in the natural world explain why forces by external agencies are necessary.

In an idealised situation, where forces like friction are absent, a body's motion does not cease. The child applying a force to a toy car in uniform motion would be unnecessary if there were no friction.

The law of inertia states that a moving toy car comes to rest because the external force of friction opposes its motion. In the absence of friction, the child would not be required to apply any force to keep the toy car in uniform motion.

concepts.concept_title: Galileo's Inference of the Law of Inertia
concepts.description: Galileo inferred that an object will continue to move with constant velocity unless acted upon by an external force. The law of inertia was inferred by Galileo from his experiments.
Every body continues to be in its state of rest or of uniform motion in a straight line unless compelled by some external force to act otherwise.

concepts.concept_title: Types of Forces in Ancient Indian Science
concepts.description: In ancient Indian science, force was thought to be of different kinds: continuous pressure, impact, persistent tendency to move in a straight line, and transmitted force.
The concept of vega, or tendency to move in a straight line, was thought to be opposed by contact with objects, including atmosphere.
key_terms: Tendency to Move in a Straight Line

concepts.description: A wave results from the transmission of vibrations, while a current is a motion of particles under gravity and fluidity.
The concept of instantaneous motion, or tatkaliki gati, was introduced by Bhaskara and anticipated the modern notion of instantaneous velocity.
Ancient Indian scientists knew that the position of a particle in space can be indicated by distance measured along three axes, and introduced units of length and time for measurement.
Galileo arrived at a new insight on motion, stating that the state of rest and the state of uniform linear motion are equivalent.
key_terms: Equivalence of State of Rest and Uniform Linear Motion

concepts.description: A body at rest or in uniform motion will remain so unless an external force is applied.
A body at rest or in uniform motion will remain so unless a net external force is applied.
If the net external force on a body is zero, its acceleration is zero.
A body in uniform motion will continue to move with a uniform velocity unless disrupted.
A body accelerates only if a net external force is applied.

concepts.concept_title: Friction as the Only External Force
concepts.description: The only external force acting on an object in certain situations is the force of friction.

concepts.concept_title: First Law - Zero Net Force implies Stationary Object
concepts.description: According to the first law, a stationary object at rest will remain at rest if the net external force acting on it is zero. This means that any stationary object, including a book on a table, will remain stationary if the net force acting on it is zero.
In the case of an object at rest on a horizontal surface, the normal force (R) acting on the object must be equal and opposite to its weight (W). This means that R = W.
An external force is required to accelerate an object from rest. This external force can cause an object to accelerate, but is not required to maintain a uniform velocity.
According to the first law, an object at rest will remain at rest unless an external force is applied. This concept is related to inertia, as objects tend to resist changes in their motion.
Frictional force can cause objects to accelerate or decelerate, depending on its direction and magnitude. Friction can also resist changes in motion when objects are moving at uniform velocity.

Gravitational force, or weight, acts on objects, causing them to accelerate or decelerate depending on the presence of other forces. In the absence of external forces, an object's weight will cause it to accelerate towards the center of the Earth.
The first law refers to the simple case when the net external force on a body is zero. The law states that the acceleration of the body is zero.
The second law of motion relates the net external force to the acceleration of the body. It states that the greater the rate of change of momentum, the greater is the force.
The momentum of a body is defined as the product of its mass and velocity. It is a vector quantity.
The rate of change of momentum is a measure of how fast the change in momentum is brought about. It is related to the applied force.
The mass of a body is an important parameter that determines the effect of force on its motion. A greater force is needed to stop a heavy body than a light body in the same time.
For a given mass, the greater the speed, the greater is the opposing force needed to stop the body in a certain time.
Momentum is a product of mass and velocity. The change in momentum is directly proportional to the applied force and time interval, but can be instantaneous.
Momentum is a vector quantity with both magnitude and direction, not always in the same direction as the force or initial momentum.
The force is directly proportional to the product of mass and acceleration, and is equal to the rate of change of momentum.
F = ma is the vector equation of the second law, stating that the force experienced by a body is equal to the product of its mass and acceleration.
The unit of force can be defined using the second law, and is known as the newton, which is the force that causes an acceleration of 1 m/s^2 to a mass of 1 kg.
If the net force acting on an object is zero, then its acceleration is also zero, and it will remain at rest or in a state of uniform motion according to the first law.
The force acting on a body is related to its acceleration at a given instant.
When a force is not parallel to the velocity of a body, it changes only the component of velocity along the direction of force.

concepts.concept_title: Second Law of Motion for a Single Point Particle
concepts.description: The second law of motion is applicable to a single point particle, where the force F in the law is the net external force and a is the acceleration of the particle.

concepts.concept_title: Second Law of Motion for a Rigid Body or System of Particles
concepts.description: The second law of motion is also applicable to a rigid body or a system of particles, where the force F refers to the total external force on the system and a is the acceleration of the system as a whole.

concepts.concept_title: Local Relation of Second Law of Motion
concepts.description: The second law of motion is a local relation, where the force F at a point in space at a certain instant is related to the acceleration a at that point at that instant.
A large force acting for a very short duration produces a finite change in momentum of the body.
A large force acting for a short time to produce a finite change in momentum is called an impulsive force.
The product of force and time, which remains a measurable quantity, is called impulse.
Forces always occur in pairs, and the force on body A by B is equal and opposite to the force on body B by A.

concepts.concept_title: Action and Reaction in Newton's Third Law
concepts.description: The terms action and reaction in the third law mean nothing else but 'force', and there is no cause-effect relation implied.
Impulse = Change in momentum, and this relation is a direct consequence of Newton's second law.
Forces always occur in pairs, equal but opposite in direction, acting on different bodies.
Impulse is the total change in momentum of an object, or the force exerted on an object over a given time period.
The change in an object's momentum is equal to the average force applied to the object, multiplied by the time over which it is applied.
The force exerted by a surface on an object is perpendicular (normal) to the surface, and opposite in direction to the object's velocity.
An impulse can be broken down into its x- and y-components, which are perpendicular to each other.
When an object collides with a surface, the impulse it experiences is perpendicular to the surface, and is normal to the surface's direction.
Impulse is the change in momentum vector and impulse and force are in the same direction.
The total momentum of an isolated system of interacting particles is conserved.
The impulse on a particle is equal to the change in its momentum.
The x-component and y-component of impulse can be calculated separately.

concepts.concept_title: Second Law of Motion and Impulse
concepts.description: The impulse exerted on a particle is equal to the change in its momentum.
The law of conservation of momentum applies to collisions in a two-body system.
For three forces in equilibrium, the resultant of any two forces is equal and opposite to the third force.
Equilibrium occurs when the net external force on a particle is zero, meaning it's either at rest or in uniform motion.
The first law states that a particle in equilibrium is either at rest or in uniform motion.

concepts.concept_title: Second Law of Motion for Equilibrium
concepts.description: When two forces act on a particle, equilibrium requires the forces to be equal and opposite.
The vector sum of three concurrent forces in equilibrium is zero.
Normal forces are the components of contact forces perpendicular to contact surfaces, while frictional forces are parallel to contact surfaces.
The third law states that forces always occur in pairs, equal and opposite in magnitude, when two objects interact.
Moment of a force is a measure of the tendency of the force to rotate an object.
Torque and moment are related concepts that describe rotational motion.
The total momentum of an isolated system is conserved and remains constant.
The total kinetic energy of an isolated system remains constant, assuming no external work is done.

Viscous forces are contact forces that oppose motion due to friction and can be represented by the equation F = -bv, where F is the force of viscosity, b is the coefficient of viscosity, and v is the velocity of the object.

Contact forces are forces that arise due to the contact between two objects, and can be further divided into normal forces, frictional forces (static and kinetic), and other forces such as tension and spring force.

Tension in a string is a contact force that arises due to the stretching of the string, and can be represented by the equation F = -kx, where F is the restoring force, k is the force constant, and x is the displacement from the unstretched state.

The force due to a spring is a contact force that arises due to the compression or extension of the spring, and can be represented by the equation F = -kx, where F is the restoring force, k is the force constant, and x is the displacement from the unstretched state.

Static friction is a type of frictional force that opposes the motion of an object when it is at rest, and can be represented by the equation f = μsN, where f is the static frictional force, μs is the coefficient of static friction, and N is the normal force.

Electrical forces are non-contact forces that arise due to the interaction between charged particles, and can be represented by Coulomb's law, F = k * q1 * q2 / r^2, where F is the force of electrostatic interaction, k is Coulomb's constant, q1 and q2 are the charges, and r is the distance between the charges.

Impending motion is the motion that would take place under an applied force if friction were absent. The limiting value of static friction (f) is independent of the area of contact and varies with the normal force approximately as f = µN.
The coefficient of static friction (µ) is a constant of proportionality that depends only on the nature of the surfaces in contact.
The law of static friction states that the maximum frictional force (f) is equal to the product of the coefficient of static friction and the normal force: f = µN.
Frictional force is the force that opposes impending motion between surfaces in contact. The maximum value of static frictional force is denoted by f.
When external force exceeds the maximum limit of static friction, the body begins to slide on the surface, and the frictional force opposing motion is called kinetic friction.
The coefficient of kinetic friction depends only on the surfaces in contact.
The law of kinetic friction states that the frictional force opposing motion is equal to the product of the coefficient of kinetic friction and the normal force: f = µN.

concepts.concept_title: Force of friction and static friction
concepts.description: Friction is a contact force that opposes impending or actual relative motion between two surfaces. Static friction is the maximum force of friction, which prevents the surfaces from moving relative to each other.
Kinetic friction is a type of friction that occurs when two surfaces are already in motion relative to each other.

concepts.concept_title: Maximum value of kinetic friction and its relation to maximum value of static friction
concepts.description: The maximum value of kinetic friction is equal to the maximum value of static friction.
The normal force is a force exerted perpendicular to the surface of contact between two objects.

concepts.concept_title: Weight (mg) and its components along inclined plane
concepts.description: When a body is placed on an inclined plane, its weight is resolved into two components: mg sinθ (parallel to the inclined plane) and mg cosθ (perpendicular to the inclined plane).

concepts.concept_title: Angle of repose (θ) and coefficient of static friction (μ)
concepts.description: The angle of repose (θ) is related to the coefficient of static friction (μ) by the equation tanθ = μ.
When a block is placed on an inclined plane, the self-adjusting frictional force (f) increases as the angle of inclination increases until it reaches its maximum value at an angle θ.

concepts.concept_title: Condition for impending relative motion (θ > θ)
concepts.description: Impending relative motion occurs when the angle of inclination (θ) is greater than the angle of repose (θ).

concepts.concept_title: Maximum value of static frictional force (f max) and its relation to normal force (N)
concepts.description: The maximum value of static frictional force (f max) is given by f max = μN.

concepts.concept_title: Equation for Acceleration due to Friction
concepts.description: The equation for acceleration due to friction is given as ma = f ≤ µ N, where f ≤ µ m g.

concepts.concept_title: Motion of a Block on an Inclined Plane
concepts.description: A block on an inclined plane will begin to slide when the force of gravity acting on it equals the force of static friction.
Rolling friction is caused by the deformation of surfaces in contact, resulting in a finite area of contact.
Kinetic friction can be reduced using lubricants, ball bearings, or air cushions between moving parts.

concepts.concept_title: Importance of Friction in Daily Life
concepts.description: Friction is important for quick stopping of relative motion, walking, and other daily activities.

concepts.concept_title: Coefficient of Friction for Static Friction
concepts.description: The coefficient of static friction is 0.04 in the given example.
Friction between tyres and road is much smaller than static or sliding friction, and provides the necessary external force to accelerate a car on an ordinary road.

concepts.concept_title: Circumstances Where Static Friction Provides Centripetal Acceleration
concepts.description: Static friction provides the centripetal acceleration for a body moving in a circular path with a uniform speed.

concepts.concept_title: Equation of Centripetal Force: f = m v^2 / R
concepts.description: The centripetal force is given by f = m v^2 / R, where m is the mass of the body, v is the speed, and R is the radius of the circular path.

concepts.concept_title: Maximum Speed of Circular Motion: v = √(R g)
concepts.description: The maximum speed of a body moving in a circular path is given by v = √(R g), where R is the radius of the path and g is the acceleration due to gravity.

concepts.concept_title: Centripetal Force on a Banked Road
concepts.description: On a banked road, the centripetal force is provided by the component of the contact force between the road and the car tyres, along the surface.

concepts.concept_title: Equation of Normal Reaction and Friction on a Banked Road
concepts.description: On a banked road, the equations N cos θ = mg + m N sin θ and N sin θ + µN cos θ = mv^2 / R represent the normal reaction and frictional force.

concepts.concept_title: Friction on a Banked Road: f = m N / s
concepts.description: The frictional force on a banked road is given by f = m N / s, where m is the mass of the car, N is the normal reaction, and s is the coefficient of friction.

concepts.concept_title: Optimum speed on a banked road
concepts.description: The optimum speed on a banked road is given by v = (R g tan θ)1/2, where R is the radius of the bank, g is the acceleration due to gravity, and θ is the angle of banking.

concepts.concept_title: Maximum permissible speed on a banked road
concepts.description: The maximum permissible speed on a banked road is given by v = (R g s) / (1-µ tanθ), where R is the radius of the bank, g is the acceleration due to gravity, µ is the coefficient of friction, and θ is the angle of banking.

concepts.concept_title: Condition for no slipping on a banked road
concepts.description: For no slipping on a banked road, the normal reaction's component must be greater than or equal to the centripetal force needed.

concepts.concept_title: Normal force on a banked road
concepts.description: The normal force on a banked road is given by N = mg cosθ - µ mg sinθ.

concepts.concept_title: Centripetal force on a circular turn
concepts.description: The centripetal force needed for a circular turn is given by Fc = (m v^2) / R.

concepts.concept_title: Equation of motion for a circular turn
concepts.description: The equation of motion for a circular turn is F = m a, where F is the net force acting on the object and a is the acceleration.

concepts.concept_title: Condition for a cyclist not to slip on an unbanked road
concepts.description: For a cyclist not to slip on an unbanked road, the condition is v^2 <= µ R g.
A diagram showing a system and all the forces acting on it by the remaining part of the system.
The process of selecting a convenient part of the assembly of bodies as one system.

concepts.concept_title: Drawing FBD with Given or Known Forces
concepts.description: Identifying and including in the free-body diagram information about forces whose magnitudes and directions are given or certain.
The process of using Newton's third law to determine the forces between two interacting bodies.
The process of determining unknown forces on a system using laws of motion.
Forces occurring in pairs where the force exerted by one body on another is equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.
Every body continues to be in its state of rest or of uniform motion in a straight line, unless compelled by some external force to act otherwise.
Aristotle believed that a force is necessary to keep a body in uniform motion, but this view was later proved to be incorrect.
Galileo extrapolated the law of inertia from his observations on motion of bodies on inclined planes.
Momentum (p) of a body is the product of its mass (m) and velocity (v): p = mv.
The rate of change of momentum of a body is proportional to the applied force and takes place in the direction in which the force acts.
The SI unit of force is newton: 1 N = 1 kg m s-2.
Newton's second law is a vector equation.
Newton's second law is applicable to a particle, and also to a body or a system.

concepts.concept_title: First Law Consistency with Second Law
concepts.description: Newton's second law is consistent with the first law (F = 0 implies a = 0).
An action-reaction pair consists of mutual forces which are always equal and opposite between two bodies.
Free-body diagrams allow you to clearly define your system and consider all forces on the system due to objects that are not part of the system itself.
Force applied to a system is proportional to its mass and acceleration, and is determined by the total external force and acceleration of the system.
The product of force and time, which equals the change in momentum.
For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction, where forces occur between pairs of bodies and action and reaction are simultaneous.
The total momentum of an isolated system remains conserved, following from the second and third laws of motion.
The force opposing relative motion between surfaces in contact, consisting of static and kinetic friction, both depending on the coefficient of friction.
Force applied to a body doesn't necessarily be in the direction of motion and can be in any direction, being parallel to acceleration.
A force at a given point and time determines the acceleration at that point at that time, and is not influenced by past motion.
The stone has only the vertical force of gravity when the effects of the surrounding air are neglected

concepts.concept_title: Net Force in Second Law of Motion
concepts.description: The net force due to all external agencies acting on an object is described by the second law of motion F = ma

concepts.concept_title: Centripetal Force as a Material Force
concepts.description: The centripetal force is simply a name given to the force that provides inward radial acceleration in circular motion, which should be identified as a material force like tension or gravity
Static friction has a limit of f ≤ µ N, where µ is the coefficient of friction

concepts.concept_title: Equality of Normal Reaction and Weight
concepts.description: The equality of normal reaction and weight (mg) has no connection with the third law, and is only applicable when the body is in equilibrium

concepts.concept_title: Action and Reaction in Third Law of Motion
concepts.description: Action and reaction in the third law of motion are simultaneous mutual forces between a pair of bodies, acting on different bodies

concepts.concept_title: Reduction of Forces to Force on A by B
concepts.description: Every force and its equivalent terms should be reduced to the phrase 'force on A by B' for clarity

concepts.concept_title: Applicability of Second Law of Motion
concepts.description: The second law of motion is applicable to both inanimate and animate objects, requiring an external force to accelerate
The objective concept of force in physics should not be confused with the subjective concept of the 'feeling of force'

concepts.concept_title: Net Force Acting on a Stationary Object
concepts.description: The net force acting on a stationary object is zero.

concepts.concept_title: Net Force Acting on a Moving Object with No Friction
concepts.description: For a moving object with no friction, the net force acting on it will be zero if it is moving at a constant velocity.

concepts.concept_title: Force of Tension in a Circular Motion
concepts.description: The net force on an object moving in a circle is directed towards the center and is provided by the force of tension in the string.

concepts.concept_title: Retarding Force and Time to Stop
concepts.description: The time it takes for an object to stop under the influence of a constant retarding force is given by the equation t = 2v^2/mF.
A constant force applied to an object will change its velocity, with the magnitude of the change in velocity proportional to the force and the time over which it is applied.

When two forces act on an object perpendicular to each other, the net force acting on it can be calculated using the Pythagorean theorem, and the acceleration can be determined using the net force and mass.
The average retarding force on an object is given by the equation F = -Δp/Δt, where Δp is the change in momentum and Δt is the time over which the force is applied.
The initial thrust (force) of a rocket is given by the equation F = ma, where m is the mass and a is the initial acceleration.

concepts.concept_title: Force and Position in a Uniformly Accelerated Motion
concepts.description: The position of an object in a uniformly accelerated motion can be determined using the equation s = ut + (1/2)at^2, where u is the initial velocity, a is the acceleration, and t is the time.

concepts.concept_title: Force and Acceleration in a Rotational Motion
concepts.description: The acceleration of an object in a rotational motion is determined by the force acting on it and its mass, with the equation a = F/m.

concepts.concept_title: Force and Change in Position in a Rotational Motion with String Cut
concepts.description: When a string is cut in a rotational motion, the force acting on the object is no longer directed towards the center, resulting in a change in its position.

The weight of an object is given by the equation W = mg, where m is the mass and g is the acceleration due to gravity, and its acceleration in a stationary elevator is zero, but in a moving elevator, it is determined by the acceleration of the elevator.
Position-time graph of a particle is a plot of position vs time. The force on the particle can be determined from the slope of the graph.

Impulse on an object is the product of force and time over which it acts. When two objects collide, the impulse imparted by one object on the other is the same as the impulse imparted by the other object on the first object.
When two objects are connected by a string and a force is applied to one of them, the tension in the string can be determined by the force applied and the mass of the other object.

concepts.concept_title: Acceleration of Masses Connected by a String
concepts.description: When two masses are connected by a string and released, the acceleration of the masses can be determined by the force applied and the mass of the string.
When a projectile is fired by a gun, the recoil speed of the gun can be determined by the momentum of the projectile and the mass of the gun.
When an object is deflected by an angle, the impulse imparted on it can be determined by its initial speed, mass, and the angle of deflection.
When an object is moving in a circular path, the tension in the string can be determined by the centripetal force required to keep it in the circular path.
When a projectile is fired, its trajectory can be described by the path it follows after the string breaks.

Why a horse cannot pull a cart and run in empty space, why passengers are thrown forward when a speeding bus stops suddenly, why it is easier to pull a lawn mower than to push it, and why a cricketer moves his hands backwards while holding a catch.

concepts.concept_title: Work as a definite physical quantity in Physics
concepts.description: In physics, the word 'Work' is defined precisely, meaning the transfer of energy from one object to another through a force applied over a distance.

concepts.concept_title: Energy as the capacity to do work
concepts.description: Energy is the capacity to do work, and it is related to the ability of an object to transfer energy from one place to another.

concepts.concept_title: Power as the rate at which work is done
concepts.description: Power is defined as the rate at which work is done or the rate at which energy is transferred from one object to another.
The scalar product of two vectors A and B is defined as A x B cos θ, where θ is the angle between the two vectors.
The scalar product of two vectors is a scalar quantity and does not have a direction.
The dot product of two vectors A and B is given by A.B = A B cos θ, where θ is the angle between the vectors. It is the product of the magnitudes of A and B and the cosine of the angle between them.
The scalar product of two vectors A and B is equal to the product of the magnitude of A and the component of B along A.
The dot product of two vectors is commutative, meaning A.B = B.A.
The dot product of a vector with a sum of two other vectors is equal to the sum of the dot products of the vector with each of the other vectors, A.(B + C) = A.B + A.C.
The dot product of a vector with a scalar times another vector is equal to the scalar times the dot product of the two vectors, A.(λ B) = λ (A.B).
Unit vectors i, j, and k have a magnitude of 1 and satisfy specific dot product relationships, such as i⋅i = j⋅j = k⋅k = 1.
The work done by a force on an object is equal to the force times the displacement of the object, F.d = F d cosθ.
The work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy, ∆K = W.
The kinetic energy of an object is half the product of its mass and the square of its speed, K.E. = (1/2) m v^2.
Kinetic energy is the energy an object possesses due to its motion, denoted by K. Work done by a force is defined as the product of the force and displacement it acts upon.
The change in kinetic energy of a particle is equal to the work done on it by the net force.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done by a Variable Force
concepts.description: Work done by a variable force over a displacement can be calculated using the work-energy theorem.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done by a Constant Force
concepts.description: Work done by a constant force can be calculated using the formula W = Fd cosθ.

concepts.concept_title: Conditions for No Work to be Done
concepts.description: No work is done if the displacement is zero, the force is zero, or the force and displacement are perpendicular to each other.

concepts.concept_title: Examples of Work Done by Different Forces
concepts.description: Examples of work done by different forces, such as gravitational force and frictional force, are discussed to illustrate the concept of work.
Work is done when a force applied to an object causes a displacement. Displacement and force must be in the same direction for work to be done.

concepts.concept_title: Work and energy have the same dimensions
concepts.description: Both work and energy have the dimensions of ML^2T^-2.

concepts.concept_title: Joule is the SI unit of work and energy
concepts.description: The SI unit of work and energy is the joule (J).

concepts.concept_title: Kinetic energy is a scalar quantity
concepts.description: Kinetic energy is a measure of the work an object can do due to its motion, and is a scalar quantity.

concepts.concept_title: Kinetic energy is a measure of work done
concepts.description: The kinetic energy of an object is a measure of the work it can do due to its motion.

concepts.concept_title: Work done is equal to area under a curve
concepts.description: The work done by a force can be calculated as the area under a curve representing the force-displacement graph.

concepts.concept_title: Work can be positive or negative
concepts.description: Work done by a force can be either positive or negative depending on the angle between the force and displacement.

concepts.concept_title: Work done by friction is negative
concepts.description: When a frictional force opposes displacement, the work done by the frictional force is negative.

concepts.concept_title: Kinetic energy increases with velocity squared
concepts.description: The kinetic energy of an object increases with the square of its velocity.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done by a Variable Force
concepts.description: Work done by a variable force is equal to the limit of the sum of the product of force and displacement when Δx tends to zero.

concepts.concept_title: Definite Integral of Force Over Displacement
concepts.description: The work done by a variable force can be expressed as a definite integral of force over displacement.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done by a Varying Force in One Dimension
concepts.description: The work done by a varying force in one dimension is equal to the area enclosed by the force-displacement graph.

concepts.concept_title: Work-Energy Theorem for a Variable Force
concepts.description: The work-energy theorem states that the change in kinetic energy of an object is equal to the work done on it by the forces acting on it.

concepts.concept_title: Time Rate of Change of Kinetic Energy
concepts.description: The time rate of change of kinetic energy of an object is equal to the force applied to it times the velocity of the object.

concepts.concept_title: Integration of Work Done by a Variable Force
concepts.description: The work done by a variable force can be calculated by integrating the force over the displacement.
The work done by a frictional force is equal to the negative of the area under the force-displacement graph.

concepts.concept_title: Equation for Work Done by a Variable Force
concepts.description: The work done by a variable force is given by the equation W = ∫ F dx.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done by a Variable Force
concepts.description: The work done by a variable force is equal to the area under the force-distance graph.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done by a Constant Force
concepts.description: The work done by a constant force is given by the product of the force and the displacement in the direction of the force.
Newton's second law relates the force acting on an object to its acceleration.
The gravitational force on an object is equal to the mass of the object multiplied by the acceleration due to gravity.
The potential energy of an object is the stored energy due to its position or configuration.
Energy can be converted from one form to another but it cannot be created or destroyed.
The work done on an object is equal to the change in its kinetic energy.
The gravitational potential energy of an object is equal to its mass multiplied by the acceleration due to gravity and the height of the object.
The gravitational potential at a point is the negative of the gravitational potential energy per unit mass at that point.

concepts.concept_title: Gravitational Potential Energy at Height h
concepts.description: Gravitational potential energy of an object at height h is given by the equation Ug = mgh.
Potential energy is the capacity for action or possibility of action that manifests itself as kinetic energy when external constraints are removed.
The variation of g with height is discussed in Chapter 7 on Gravitation.
A force F(x) is conservative if it can be derived from a scalar quantity V(x).

concepts.concept_title: Relation between Work and Potential Energy
concepts.description: The work done by a conservative force depends only on the initial and final positions.
The dimensions of potential energy are ML^2T^-2.

The total mechanical energy of a system is conserved if the forces, doing work on it, are conservative. This implies that the change in kinetic energy is equal to the negative of the change in potential energy.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done by a Conservative Force
concepts.description: The work done by a conservative force is given by the negative change in potential energy. This is represented by the equation F(x)Δx = -ΔV.

concepts.concept_title: Relation Between Kinetic and Potential Energy
concepts.description: The change in kinetic energy is equal to the negative of the change in potential energy, i.e., ΔK = -ΔV. This implies that the total mechanical energy is conserved.

concepts.concept_title: Equation of Conservation of Mechanical Energy
concepts.description: The equation ∆(K + V) = 0 represents the conservation of mechanical energy, where K is the kinetic energy and V is the potential energy.

concepts.concept_title: Potential Energy due to a Conservative Force
concepts.description: The potential energy due to a conservative force can be defined such that -ΔV = F(x)Δx. This equation implies that the potential energy is a state function.

concepts.concept_title: Equations of Motion for a Ball Dropping from a Cliff
concepts.description: The equations v = 2gH and v = sqrt(2g(H-h)) represent the motion of a ball dropping from a cliff. These equations illustrate the conservation of mechanical energy.
The total energy of an isolated system remains constant over time. It is conserved.
Kinetic energy is the energy of motion, while potential energy is the stored energy due to position.

concepts.concept_title: Energy at Different Points of Pendulum's Trajectory
concepts.description: The energy at different points of a pendulum's trajectory depends on its position and velocity.
When the string becomes slack at the topmost point of a pendulum's trajectory, it experiences horizontal motion.
When the string is cut at the topmost point of a pendulum's trajectory, it exhibits projectile motion.
The work done by a spring force depends on its displacement from the equilibrium position.
The force exerted by a spring is directly proportional to its displacement from the equilibrium position.
The work done by a spring depends on the spring constant and displacement.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done by the Spring Force
concepts.description: The work done by a spring force is given by W = kx^2/2, where k is the spring constant and x is the displacement. The work done is independent of the path and depends only on the initial and final positions.
A conservative force is a force for which the work done is independent of the path and depends only on the initial and final positions. The spring force is an example of a conservative force.
The potential energy of a spring is given by V(x) = kx^2/2. The potential energy is zero when the block and spring system is in the equilibrium position.

The total mechanical energy of a system remains constant if there are no external forces doing work on it. The kinetic energy and potential energy are complementary and one increases as the other decreases.
Hooke's law states that the force exerted by a spring is proportional to its displacement from the equilibrium position. F = -kx.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done in a Cyclic Process
concepts.description: The work done in a cyclic process is zero since the initial and final positions are the same.

concepts.concept_title: Conservation of energy with conservative forces
concepts.description: The principle of conservation of energy applies to systems with conservative forces, where the total mechanical energy remains constant.

concepts.concept_title: Non-conservative forces modify the conservation of energy
concepts.description: Non-conservative forces like friction require the use of the work-energy theorem instead of the conservation of mechanical energy.

concepts.concept_title: Zero potential energy is arbitrary and depends on convenience
concepts.description: The zero of potential energy is set according to convenience, and once fixed, it must be consistently adhered to throughout a discussion.

concepts.concept_title: Spring-mass system with friction and conservative forces
concepts.description: In a spring-mass system with both conservative and non-conservative forces, the total work done by the forces equals the change in total mechanical energy.

concepts.concept_title: Work done by a force depends on direction
concepts.description: A force's work done on an object depends on the direction of motion, leading to the consideration of non-conservative forces in certain situations.

concepts.concept_title: Example: Maximum compression of a spring with friction
concepts.description: The maximum compression of a spring with friction can be calculated by equating the total work done by forces to the change in total mechanical energy.
Power is defined as the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred.
Average power is defined as the ratio of work done to the total time taken.
Instantaneous power is defined as the limiting value of average power as time interval approaches zero.

concepts.concept_title: Instantaneous Power in terms of Force and velocity
concepts.description: Instantaneous power is also expressed as the product of the force and the instantaneous velocity.
The dimensions of power are [M L^2 T^-3].
The unit of power is called a watt (W) and is defined as 1 J s^-1.
Horse power (hp) is a unit of power and is equal to 746 watts.

concepts.concept_title: Collision and Conservation of Momentum and Energy
concepts.description: The laws of momentum and energy conservation are applied to the collision of two objects.
The total linear momentum of a system remains constant in all collisions, meaning the initial momentum is equal to the final momentum.
The force exerted on an object causes a change in its momentum; the total impulse on an object is equal to the change in its momentum.
Newton's third law states that when two objects interact, the forces exerted on one object by the other are always equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.
In an elastic collision, the kinetic energy is conserved and the deformation is fully relieved. The initial kinetic energy is equal to the final kinetic energy.
In a completely inelastic collision, the two particles move together after the collision and some of the kinetic energy is lost.

concepts.concept_title: Conservation of Kinetic Energy in Elastic Collision
concepts.description: In an elastic collision, the total kinetic energy of the system is conserved. The initial kinetic energy is equal to the final kinetic energy.
In an inelastic collision, some of the initial kinetic energy is transformed into other forms of energy, such as heat and sound.
The collision time Δt is the time duration for which the two objects interact with each other during the collision.

concepts.concept_title: Mass and Velocity Equations in Elastic Collision
concepts.description: The mass and velocity equations in an elastic collision are given by Eqs. (5.23) to (5.27), which relate the masses and velocities of the two objects before and after the collision.
A collision where the objects stick together after collision, resulting in a loss of kinetic energy.

concepts.concept_title: Conservation of Momentum in One Dimension
concepts.description: The total momentum before collision is equal to the total momentum after collision in one dimension.
An angle between two objects in a collision which does not represent the actual impact angle, but rather a convenient simplification.
A collision where one mass is much greater than the other, resulting in the lighter mass being reversed in direction.
The transfer of momentum from one object to another during a collision.
Collisions where the objects move in two dimensions, resulting in the need to consider the conservation of momentum in three directions.
The conservation of the vertical component of momentum in a two-dimensional collision.
A collision where the objects bounce off each other without losing any energy.

concepts.concept_title: Fractional Kinetic Energy Loss in Elastic Collision
concepts.description: The calculation of the energy lost by an object during an elastic collision.
A material used to slow down neutrons in a nuclear reaction, such as heavy water or graphite.
A head-on collision occurs when two bodies travel along the same straight line, while a glancing collision occurs at an angle.
A one-dimensional collision occurs when the initial velocities and final velocities of both bodies are along the same straight line.
Momentum is conserved in collisions, as per the law of conservation of momentum.
In an elastic collision, kinetic energy is conserved, while in an inelastic collision, kinetic energy is not conserved.
Scattering occurs when two particles interact with each other and change their paths, depending on their initial velocities and the type of interaction.
A force is conservative if the work done by it on an object is path-independent and depends only on the end points.
The potential energy function V(x) is defined such that the force at any point x is the negative of the derivative of V(x) with respect to x.
The total mechanical energy of a body remains constant if the only forces acting on it are conservative.
Gravitational potential energy of a particle of mass m at a height x is given by V(x) = m g x. g is the acceleration due to gravity.
Elastic potential energy of a spring of force constant k and extension x is given by V ( x ) = 1 k x2. The elastic potential energy depends on the force constant and the extension of the spring.
The scalar product of two vectors A and B is given by A.B = AB cos θ, where θ is the angle between A and B.
Work done is a scalar quantity. It can be positive or negative. The work done by a specific force on a body over a certain displacement can be calculated.
The work-energy theorem relates the work done on a body to its change in kinetic energy. It is a scalar form of Newton's second law.
For conservative forces, the potential energy of a body is always undetermined up to a constant. The point where the potential energy is zero is a matter of choice.
Not all forces encountered in mechanics have an associated potential energy. For example, work done by friction over a closed path is not zero.
In a collision, the total linear momentum is always conserved, but the kinetic energy may not be conserved.
Work done by a force on a body is not instantaneous but an average over a displacement. At every instant, the force does not necessarily act in the direction of motion.

concepts.concept_title: Sign of Work Done by a Force
concepts.description: The sign of work done by a force depends on the nature of the force and the direction of displacement. Positive work is done when force and displacement are in the same direction or when the force is external and the displacement is against the force.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done by a Constant Force
concepts.description: The work done by a constant force is the product of the force and the displacement of the body in the direction of the force.
Frictional force does negative work on an object moving in the same direction as the force of friction.
Gravitational force always does positive work on an object moving in a gravitational field.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done by an Applied Force
concepts.description: The work done by an applied force depends on the nature of the force and the displacement of the body.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done by the Resistive Force of Air
concepts.description: The resistive force of air does negative work on an object moving through the air.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done by a Variable Force
concepts.description: The work done by a variable force is the integral of the force with respect to displacement.
The total energy of a system is conserved over time unless external forces are involved.
The potential energy function is a graphical representation of the potential energy of a particle in a one-dimensional system.
The turnback point is the point at which a particle executes simple harmonic motion and returns to its original position.
The gravitational potential energy of an object is proportional to its height above the ground.
The total mechanical energy of a closed system is conserved over time.
The work-energy theorem states that the work done on a body is equal to the change in its kinetic energy.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done by a Conservative Force
concepts.description: When a conservative force does positive work on a body, its potential energy increases.

concepts.concept_title: Decrease in Potential Energy due to Friction
concepts.description: Work done by a body against friction always results in a loss of its potential energy.

concepts.concept_title: Change in Total Momentum of a Mass System
concepts.description: The rate of change of total momentum of a many-particle system is proportional to the external force.

concepts.concept_title: Conservation of Momentum and Energy in Elastic Collision
concepts.description: In an elastic collision of two bodies, the momentum and energy of each body is conserved.

concepts.concept_title: Conservation of Energy in a System
concepts.description: Total energy of a system is always conserved, no matter what internal and external forces on the body are present.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done in Motion Along a Closed Loop
concepts.description: Work done in the motion of a body over a closed loop is zero for every force in nature.

concepts.concept_title: Final Kinetic Energy in Inelastic Collision
concepts.description: In an inelastic collision, the final kinetic energy is always less than the initial kinetic energy of the system.

concepts.concept_title: Conservation of Kinetic Energy in Elastic Collision
concepts.description: In an elastic collision of two billiard balls, the total kinetic energy is conserved only after the collision.

concepts.concept_title: Conservation of Linear Momentum in Elastic Collision
concepts.description: In an elastic collision, the total linear momentum is conserved.

concepts.concept_title: Conservation of Energy in Inelastic Collision
concepts.description: In an inelastic collision, the total energy of the system is not conserved.

concepts.concept_title: Dependence of Potential Energy on Separation Distance
concepts.description: If the potential energy of two billiard balls depends only on the separation distance between their centers, the collision is elastic.
The power delivered to a body is proportional to the acceleration of the body.

concepts.concept_title: Displacement of a Body Under Constant Power
concepts.description: The displacement of a body moving under the influence of a source of constant power is proportional to the square root of time.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done by a Constant Force
concepts.description: The work done by a constant force on a body is equal to the product of the force and the displacement of the body in the direction of the force.

concepts.concept_title: Kinetic Energy of a Charged Particle
concepts.description: The kinetic energy of a charged particle is given by the product of its mass and the square of its speed.
The mass of an object can be converted into energy, and vice versa, according to Einstein's famous equation E=mc^2.

concepts.concept_title: Terminal Velocity and Work Done by Gravitational Force
concepts.description: An object in free fall experiences a uniform speed at half its original height until it hits the ground, and the work done by gravity in the second half of the journey is zero.
Momentum is conserved in a collision between two objects if the total momentum before the collision is equal to the total momentum after the collision.
In an elastic collision, the total kinetic energy is conserved, while in an inelastic collision, the total kinetic energy is not conserved.
The efficiency of a pump is the ratio of the work done by the pump to the electrical energy supplied to it.

concepts.concept_title: Conservation of Momentum in a Pendulum Collision
concepts.description: In an elastic collision between two objects, the total momentum is conserved, and the velocity of the colliding object is transferred completely to the stationary object.
When a pendulum is released from a horizontal position, some of the energy is lost due to air resistance, and the speed of the pendulum at the lowermost point is reduced.
The acceleration of a moving object is zero if no external force is acting on it.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done by a Variable Force
concepts.description: The work done by a variable force is the area under the force-distance graph.

concepts.concept_title: Mass of Air Passing Through a Circle
concepts.description: The mass of air passing through a circle is given by the product of the area of the circle and the density of air.
The kinetic energy of air is given by the product of the mass of the air and the square of the velocity of the air.

concepts.concept_title: Electrical Power Produced by a Windmill
concepts.description: The electrical power produced by a windmill is the product of the wind's power and the efficiency of the windmill.
The work done by a person in lifting a mass to a height is given by the product of the force applied and the displacement.
The energy released from fat is given by the product of the mass of fat and the energy released per unit mass.
The useful electrical energy that can be generated from solar power is calculated by considering the percentage of energy conversion and the intensity of solar power incident on a surface.
The power consumption of a family and the energy flux incident on a surface are related by the surface area that needs to be covered.
The percentage of solar energy that can be converted to useful electrical energy is a factor in determining the required surface area.

concepts.concept_title: Area Calculation (Roof of a Typical House)
concepts.description: The area needed to supply a certain power is compared to the area of the roof of a typical house.
The intensity of solar power incident on a surface is given as 200 W/m^2.
Idealised model of a particle used to describe the motion of bodies of finite size, where motion is described in terms of the motion of a system of particles.

concepts.concept_title: Linear Momentum of a System of Particles
concepts.description: Motion of a system of particles where the motion of the system is described in terms of the motion of a central particle, with the centre of mass considered as the reference point.

concepts.concept_title: Centre of Mass and Extended Bodies
concepts.description: Usefulness of the concept of centre of mass in understanding the motion of extended bodies.
A body with a perfectly definite and unchanging shape, where distances between all pairs of particles do not change.

concepts.concept_title: Types of Motion in Rigid Body
concepts.description: Motion of a rigid body, where it can have either translational motion or rotational motion.
Line or fixed axis about which a rigid body rotates.
Motion of a rigid body where all particles move with the same velocity at any instant of time.
A surface with an angle with respect to the horizontal that can cause translational motion of an object.

concepts.concept_title: Velocity of Different Parts of a Sliding Object on an Inclined Plane
concepts.description: The velocity of different parts of an object sliding down an inclined plane is the same at any instant of time.

concepts.concept_title: Rolling Motion of a Solid Cylinder
concepts.description: A type of motion that includes both translational and rotational motion.

concepts.concept_title: Motion of a Rigid Body with Rotational Motion
concepts.description: A rigid body can have both translational and rotational motion simultaneously.
An imaginary line around which a rigid body rotates.

concepts.concept_title: Rotational Motion of a Rigid Body
concepts.description: Motion of a rigid body around a fixed or oscillating axis.
Motion of a particle that describes a circle around a fixed or oscillating center.
A type of rigid body that rotates around a fixed axis while also oscillating.
A rigid body rotates about a fixed axis if one line of the body is fixed.
A particle moving in a circle with center C has radius r and its position is given by r = 0 on the fixed axis.
The point through which the axis of rotation passes is called the center of rotation.
The axis of rotation of a spinning top or top moves around a vertical through its point of contact with the ground, sweeping out a cone.
The axis of rotation of an oscillating table fan or pedestal fan moves with a sidewise movement in a horizontal plane about the vertical through the point of attachment.
In general cases of rotation, one point of the rigid body is fixed.
A rigid body can move with pure translation, a combination of translation and rotation or pure rotation.
Rotational motion is motion about a fixed or moving axis. The two types are rotation and translation.
The centre of mass of a system of particles is the point where the mass of the system can be assumed to be concentrated.

concepts.concept_title: Centre of Mass of a Two-Particle System
concepts.description: For two particles of masses and at positions, the centre of mass is at a position.

concepts.concept_title: Equation for Centre of Mass Position
concepts.description: For n particles of masses and at positions, the centre of mass is at a position.
The centre of mass can be regarded as the mass-weighted mean of the positions of the particles.

concepts.concept_title: Position Vector of the Centre of Mass
concepts.description: The position vector of the centre of mass is the total momentum of a system of particles divided by the total mass of the system.

concepts.concept_title: Coordinate System for Centre of Mass
concepts.description: A suitable coordinate system can be chosen such that the origin is at the centre of mass, making calculations simpler.

concepts.concept_title: Position of Centre of Mass for Particles at Equal Intervals
concepts.description: The position vector of the centre of mass for particles at equal intervals on a straight line is the average of the position vectors of the particles.

concepts.concept_title: Centre of Mass of a Continuous Distribution of Mass
concepts.description: For a continuous distribution of mass, the centre of mass can be found by subdividing the body into smaller elements and summing their contributions.

concepts.concept_title: Approximation for Centre of Mass in a Continuous Distribution
concepts.description: The centre of mass in a continuous distribution of mass can be approximated by considering the body as made up of infinitesimally small elements.

The centre of mass of a body can be found by taking the integral of the mass multiplied by the position of each infinitesimal mass element, and then dividing by the total mass. This is mathematically expressed as ∑∆m →∫ dm = M.
The coordinates of the centre of mass are given by X= M ∫xdm, Y= M ∫ydmand Z= M ∫zdm, where M is the total mass of the body.

concepts.concept_title: Vector Expression of Centre of Mass
concepts.description: The vector expression of the centre of mass is given by R = 1 ∫ rdm, where r is the position vector of the infinitesimal mass element.

concepts.concept_title: Centre of Mass of Homogeneous Bodies
concepts.description: The centre of mass of homogeneous bodies of regular shapes like rings, discs, spheres, rods etc. coincides with their geometric centres.
Using reflection symmetry, it can be shown that the centre of mass of a body lies at its geometric centre.

concepts.concept_title: Centre of Mass of an Equilateral Triangle
concepts.description: The centre of mass of an equilateral triangle can be found by finding the point of concurrence of the medians.

concepts.concept_title: Centre of Mass of an L-Shaped Lamina
concepts.description: The centre of mass of an L-shaped lamina can be found by considering it as a combination of multiple geometric shapes.
The centre of mass of a body can be calculated using the equations X= M ∫xdm, Y= M ∫ydmand Z= M ∫zdm.

concepts.concept_title: Centre of Mass of L-Shaped Lamina
concepts.description: The centre of mass of an L-shaped lamina lies on the line joining the points where the three squares making up the lamina touch each other.

concepts.concept_title: Method to Find Centre of Mass of Triangular Lamina
concepts.description: The centre of mass of a triangular lamina can be found by subdividing it into narrow strips each parallel to the base and finding the midpoint of each strip.

concepts.concept_title: Physical Importance of Centre of Mass
