concepts.description: The force on a system of particles is the vector sum of all external forces acting on the particles, and the acceleration of its centre of mass is the sum of all external forces divided by the total mass of the system.
The centre of mass of a system of particles moves as if all the mass of the system was concentrated at the centre of mass and all the external forces were acting on it.
The acceleration of the centre of mass of a system of particles is the sum of all external forces divided by the total mass of the system.

concepts.concept_title: External Forces on System of Particles
concepts.description: Only external forces contribute to the motion of the centre of mass of a system of particles.

concepts.concept_title: Newton's Third Law for Internal Forces
concepts.description: Internal forces exerted by particles on each other occur in equal and opposite pairs, and their contributions to the sum of forces are zero.
The velocity of the centre of mass of a system of particles is the sum of the velocities of individual particles, weighted by their masses.

concepts.concept_title: External forces on a system of particles
concepts.description: External forces acting on a system of particles can be known only, without needing to consider internal forces.

concepts.concept_title: Equation of motion for the centre of mass
concepts.description: The motion of the centre of mass can be determined without specifying the nature of the system or its internal forces.
Forces on individual particles are equal to the product of mass and acceleration of each particle.
The linear momentum of a particle is defined as the product of its mass and velocity.

concepts.concept_title: Linear momentum of a system of particles
concepts.description: The linear momentum of a system of particles is the vector sum of the linear momenta of each particle.

concepts.concept_title: Newton's second law for a system of particles
concepts.description: The total force acting on a system of particles can be calculated by summing the forces on individual particles.
Linear momentum of a particle is the product of its mass and velocity. It is a measure of the tendency of the particle to keep moving in a straight line.

concepts.concept_title: Law of Conservation of Linear Momentum
concepts.description: The law states that the total linear momentum of a system of particles is constant if the total external force acting on the system is zero.

concepts.concept_title: Centre of Mass of a System of Particles
concepts.description: The centre of mass of a system of particles is the point where the entire mass of the system can be considered to be concentrated.
Internal forces do not contribute to the motion of the centre of mass of a system of particles, and the total linear momentum of the system remains constant.
The linear momentum of a system of particles can be broken down into its components along the x, y, and z axes.

concepts.concept_title: Newton’s Second Law for a system of Particles
concepts.description: Newton’s second law states that the force acting on a system of particles is equal to the rate of change of its linear momentum.

concepts.concept_title: Equation of Conservation of Linear Momentum
concepts.description: The equation represents the law of conservation of linear momentum, stating that the total linear momentum of a system of particles is constant when the total external force is zero.
key_terms: equation of conservation of linear momentum

concepts.description: A frame of reference where the centre of mass is at rest, especially useful when dealing with systems of particles.

concepts.concept_title: Centre of Mass in Uniform Motion
concepts.description: When a system of particles undergoes uniform motion, its centre of mass also moves uniformly.

concepts.concept_title: Circular Motion of Particles about Centre of Mass
concepts.description: When two particles are in a system, their motion can be considered as a combination of uniform motion in a straight line and circular orbits about the centre of mass.
The total linear momentum of a system remains constant if no external forces are acting on it.
The decay of a radioactive particle involves the conservation of linear momentum.
A vector product of two vectors is a vector quantity, and it's essential for the study of rotational motion.
A vector product of two vectors a and b is a vector c such that the magnitude of c is absinθ, where a and b are magnitudes of a and b, and θ is the angle between the two vectors.
The order of vectors in vector product matters, with a × b ≠ b × a, and a × b also equals -b × a.

concepts.concept_title: Behavior of Vector Product Under Reflection
concepts.description: Under reflection, a × b remains unchanged.
Vector product is distributive with respect to vector addition: a × (b + c) = a × b + a × c.
The product of a vector with itself is zero, i.e., a × a = 0.
A unit vector is a vector with a magnitude of 1. It has the same direction as the original vector.
The cross product of two vectors results in a third vector that is perpendicular to both of the original vectors.
The vector triple product is a result of taking the cross product of two vectors with another vector, resulting in a new vector.
Angular velocity is a measure of how fast a particle is rotating around a fixed axis.
The instantaneous angular velocity is the rate of change of the angular displacement with respect to time.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship between Linear and Angular Velocity
concepts.description: The linear velocity of a particle is related to its angular velocity by a vector product.
Instantaneous angular velocity (ω) is the rate of change of angular displacement with respect to time.

concepts.concept_title: Relation between Linear and Angular Velocity
concepts.description: The magnitude of linear velocity (v) of a particle in circular motion is related to the angular velocity (ω) by the equation υ = ωr.

concepts.concept_title: Linear Velocity of a Particle in a Rigid Body
concepts.description: The linear velocity of a particle in a rigid body is given by v = ωr, where r is the perpendicular distance from the fixed axis.

concepts.concept_title: Angular Velocity of a Whole Body
concepts.description: The angular velocity of a whole body is the same for all particles, and is referred to as the angular velocity of the whole body.
Pure rotation is characterised by all parts of the body having the same angular velocity at any instant of time.
The angular velocity is a vector quantity that lies along the axis of rotation, and its direction is along the fixed axis.

concepts.concept_title: Vector Product of Angular Velocity and Position Vector
concepts.description: The vector product ω × r corresponds to the linear velocity of a particle and is perpendicular to both ω and r.
Torque is the rotational analogue of force in linear motion. It is the twisting force that causes an object to rotate.
Angular momentum is a measure of an object's tendency to continue rotating. It is the product of an object's moment of inertia and its angular velocity.
Torque and angular momentum are related by the equation τ = dL/dt, where τ is the torque and L is the angular momentum.
Moment of inertia is the measure of an object's resistance to changes in its rotation. It depends on the object's mass distribution and its distance from the axis of rotation.
Angular velocity is a measure of an object's rate of rotation. It is the rate at which the angular displacement changes.
Angular acceleration is the rate of change of angular velocity. It is analogous to linear acceleration.

concepts.concept_title: Relation between linear and angular velocity
concepts.description: The relationship between linear velocity and angular velocity is given by v = ω × r, where v is the linear velocity, ω is the angular velocity, and r is the radius of rotation.
Moment of force is the rotational analogue of force in linear motion.
Moment of force is defined as the vector product of position vector and force vector.
Moment of force has properties of a vector product such as direction reversal.
Torque and moment of force are interchangeable terms.
Angular acceleration is the time rate of change of angular velocity.
Angular momentum is the rotational analogue of linear momentum.
Angular momentum is defined as the vector product of position vector and linear momentum vector.

Moment of a force is a vector quantity, representing the tendency of a force to cause rotation about a point. It is defined as the product of the magnitude of the force and the perpendicular distance from the point of application to the axis of rotation.

concepts.concept_title: SI Unit of Moment of Force
concepts.description: The SI unit of moment of force is newton metre (N m), which is a measure of the tendency of a force to cause rotation.
Angular momentum is a vector quantity, representing the tendency of a rotating object to maintain its rotation. It is defined as the product of the moment of inertia and the angular velocity.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship between Moment of Force and Angular Momentum
concepts.description: The moment of force is related to the angular momentum by the equation τ = (dr/dt) x p = (r x F), where τ is the torque, r is the radius vector, p is the linear momentum, and F is the force.
Torque is a measure of the rate of change of angular momentum, representing the rotational force that causes a rotating object to accelerate.
When a rotating object is subject to a torque, its angular momentum precesses (rotates around) the axis of the torque.

concepts.concept_title: Total Angular Momentum of a System
concepts.description: The total angular momentum of a system of particles is the vector sum of the angular momenta of individual particles.
The angular momentum of a particle is given by the cross product of its position vector and linear momentum.
The torque on a particle is the cross product of its position vector and the external force acting on it.
If the total external torque on a system is zero, the total angular momentum of the system is conserved.
The components of the total angular momentum vector can be conserved individually if the total angular momentum is conserved.

concepts.concept_title: Rotational Analogue of Linear Momentum Conservation
concepts.description: The conservation of total angular momentum is the rotational analogue of the conservation of total linear momentum.

concepts.concept_title: Torque Resulting from Equal and Opposite Forces
concepts.description: Since forces are equal and opposite, the torque resulting from each action-reaction pair of forces is zero.
The angular momentum of a particle about an arbitrary point is given by l = r × p, where p is the total linear momentum of the particle.
Equation (6.28b) is the rotational analogue of (6.17) for a single particle system, showing the time rate of change of angular momentum equals the external torque.
The total force on a rigid body is zero, according to Eq. (6.30a), leading to the total linear momentum remaining constant.
Equation (6.30b) states that the total torque on a rigid body is zero, leading to the rotational equilibrium condition.
The angular momentum of a body remains constant when the total force and total torque acting on it are both zero.

concepts.concept_title: Independence of Rotational Equilibrium from Origin
concepts.description: If the translational equilibrium condition holds for a rigid body, the rotational equilibrium condition remains valid if the origin is shifted.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of External Forces on a Rigid Body
concepts.description: External forces cause changes in both the translational and rotational states of a rigid body.
The force equations and torque equations for a rigid body are represented by two vector equations.

concepts.concept_title: Translational Equilibrium of a Rigid Body
concepts.description: A rigid body is in translational equilibrium if the net force acting on it is zero.

concepts.concept_title: Rotational Equilibrium of a Rigid Body
concepts.description: A rigid body is in rotational equilibrium if the net torque acting on it is zero.

concepts.concept_title: Mechanical Equilibrium of a Rigid Body
concepts.description: A rigid body is in mechanical equilibrium if both its linear and angular momentum are not changing with time.

concepts.concept_title: Conditions for Equilibrium in a Rigid Body
concepts.description: For mechanical equilibrium, the sum of the force components along two perpendicular axes and the sum of the torque components along a perpendicular axis must be zero.

concepts.concept_title: Equilibrium of a Rigid Body in a Plane
concepts.description: When all forces acting on a rigid body are coplanar, we need only three conditions to be satisfied for mechanical equilibrium.

concepts.concept_title: Comparison with Equilibrium of a Particle
concepts.description: The conditions of equilibrium of a rigid body are similar to those of a particle, with the additional requirement of rotational equilibrium.
A state where the net force acting on a body is zero, resulting in no translational motion.
A couple or torque is a pair of forces that produces rotation without translation, resulting in circular motion.
A state where a body is in translational equilibrium but not in rotational equilibrium, or vice versa.
A measure of the tendency of a force to rotate a body about a pivot point.
A couple is a pair of forces that produces rotation, and its moment is a measure of its tendency to rotate a body.
A fulcrum is a pivot point that allows a lever to rotate freely, and it is useful in transferring forces.
A lever is in mechanical equilibrium when the sum of its moments is zero, and it is a useful tool for lifting loads.
Moments can be either positive or negative, depending on their direction and the sign convention used.
The moment of a couple is the total moment of its two forces, and it does not depend on the pivot point used.
The moment of a couple is the sum of the moments of the two forces making the couple, expressed as a cross product of position vectors and the net force of the couple.
A lever is a simple machine that consists of a rod pivoted at a point along its length, used to amplify the force applied to lift a load. Mechanical Advantage is the ratio of Load Arm to Effort Arm.
The principle of moments states that the moment of a couple is equal to the sum of the moments of the forces making up the couple, and is independent of the point of reference.

The Centre of Gravity (CG) of a body is that point where the total gravitational torque on the body is zero, defined as the point where the sum of the position vectors of the particles of the body with respect to the CG is zero.
The Gravitational Torque on an extended body due to the force of gravity on a particle is given by τ = r × mg.
The total gravitational torque on a body is the sum of the torques due to each particle, and is given by ∑(r × mg) = 0 for the Centre of Gravity (CG).

concepts.concept_title: Centre of Mass and Centre of Gravity
concepts.description: The centre of mass of a body depends on its mass distribution and does not consider gravity, whereas the centre of gravity depends on both mass distribution and gravity.
When the net force acting on a body is zero, the centre of mass remains at rest or moves in a straight line with constant speed.
Torque is a measure of the turning effect of a force on a body. A body is in rotational equilibrium when the net torque acting on it is zero.

concepts.concept_title: Centre of Gravity of a Body
concepts.description: The centre of gravity of a body is the point where the entire weight of the body can be considered to be acting.

concepts.concept_title: Determining the Centre of Gravity Using Multiple Suspension Points
concepts.description: By suspending a body from multiple points and finding the intersection of the vertical lines passing through these points, we can determine the centre of gravity.

concepts.concept_title: Equation of Rotational Equilibrium for a Rigid Body
concepts.description: For rotational equilibrium of a rigid body, the sum of the clockwise moments must be equal to the sum of the anticlockwise moments.
The centre of mass is the point where the entire mass of an object can be considered to be concentrated for the purpose of analyzing its motion.
The reactions at supports of a rigid body in equilibrium can be determined using the principles of statics.
The moment of inertia is a measure of the resistance of a rigid body to changes in its rotational motion.

concepts.concept_title: Kinetic Energy of a Rotating Body
concepts.description: The kinetic energy of a rotating body is the sum of the kinetic energies of all its individual particles.
When a rigid body rotates about a fixed axis, the linear velocity of a particle is given by the product of its distance from the axis and the angular velocity.
For rotational equilibrium, the net torque acting on a rigid body must be zero.

concepts.concept_title: Equations of Motion for a Rotating Body
concepts.description: The equations of motion for a rotating body include the equation for the kinetic energy and the equations for the angular momentum.
Kinetic energy of a rotating body is given by K = Iω^2, analogous to linear motion K = mv^2, where I is the moment of inertia.
The moment of inertia of a body is a measure of its resistance to changes in its rotational motion, and it depends on the distribution of mass about the axis of rotation.
The radius of gyration is a geometric property of a body that describes how the mass of the body is distributed with respect to the axis of rotation.

concepts.concept_title: Analogies between Rotational and Translational Motion
concepts.description: Rotational motion has analogies with linear motion, with the moment of inertia playing a similar role to mass in linear motion.
The moment of inertia of a uniform ring is I = MR^2, while the radius of gyration for a rod is k = L/√12.
Moment of inertia is the measure of rotational inertia of a body about an axis, resisting change in its state of rotational motion.
The dimensions of moment of inertia are ML2, and its SI unit is kg m2.

concepts.concept_title: Moment of Inertia of Pair of Masses
concepts.description: The moment of inertia of a pair of equal masses rotating about an axis through their center of mass is I = Ml^2/4.
key_terms: Moment of Inertia of Pair of Masses

concepts.concept_title: Moment of Inertia of Regular Shaped Bodies
concepts.description: Table 6.1 provides the moment of inertia of various regular shaped bodies about specific axes.
key_terms: Moment of Inertia of Regular Shaped Bodies

concepts.description: A flywheel is a disc with a large moment of inertia, used in machines to resist sudden changes in speed and provide a smooth ride.

concepts.concept_title: Analogy between Rotational Motion and Translational Motion
concepts.description: Rotational motion can be analogously described as a circular motion, with angular velocity playing the same role as linear velocity.
key_terms: Analogy between Rotational Motion and Translational Motion

concepts.description: Angular displacement is the angle through which a particle in a rotating body has moved from its initial position.
Angular velocity is the time rate of change of angular displacement, measured in rad/s.
Angular acceleration is the time rate of change of angular velocity, measured in rad/s^2.

The kinematic equations in rotational motion are analogous to those in linear motion, with angular displacement, angular velocity, and angular acceleration replacing linear displacement, velocity, and acceleration.

concepts.concept_title: Equation of Angular Acceleration in Rotation
concepts.description: The equation for angular acceleration is ω = αt, where ω is the angular velocity, α is the angular acceleration, and t is time.

concepts.concept_title: Unit Conversion between Angular Speed and Revolutions
concepts.description: The unit conversion between angular speed in rad/s and revolutions per minute (rpm) is given by 1 rad/s = 9.549/π rpm.

The equation for angular displacement is θ = θ0 + ωt + αt^2, where θ is the final angular displacement, θ0 is the initial angular displacement, ω is the angular velocity, and α is the angular acceleration.

The equation for angular velocity is ω^2 = ω0^2 + 2α(θ - θ0), where ω is the final angular velocity, ω0 is the initial angular velocity, α is the angular acceleration, θ is the final angular displacement, and θ0 is the initial angular displacement.
Angular speed is the rate of change of angular displacement with respect to time. It is measured in radians per second.
Angular displacement is the change in angular position of an object from its initial to its final position. It is measured in radians.
Angular velocity is the rate of change of angular displacement with respect to time. It is a vector quantity with direction along the axis of rotation.
Torque is a measure of the twisting or rotational force applied to an object. It is a vector quantity with direction along the axis of rotation.
Moment of inertia is a measure of an object's resistance to changes in its rotation. It depends on the mass distribution and size of the object.
Angular acceleration is the rate of change of angular velocity with respect to time. It is a measure of the change in an object's rotation.
Power in rotational motion is the rate at which work is done. It is given by the product of torque and angular velocity.
Work in rotational motion is the product of torque and angular displacement. It is a measure of the energy transferred.
Kinetic energy in rotational motion is given by the square of the angular speed times the moment of inertia.
Comparisons between linear and rotational motions are made with the help of Table 6.2, listing the analogues of the quantities.
The product of an object's moment of inertia and its angular velocity.
The product of the force applied and the perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation.
The product of the torque and the angular displacement.
The rate at which work is done on the body, given by the product of the torque and angular velocity.

concepts.concept_title: Rate of Increase of Kinetic Energy
concepts.description: Equal to the rate at which work is done on the body.
key_terms: Rate of Increase of Kinetic Energy

concepts.description: Equal to the product of the torque and change in angular displacement.
Equal to the total work done on the body.
key_terms: Total Work Done on the Body

concepts.description: Torque is a measure of rotational force that causes an object to rotate.
Moment of inertia is a measure of an object's resistance to changes in its rotation.
Torque produces angular acceleration in a rotating object, similar to how force produces linear acceleration.
Angular momentum is a measure of an object's tendency to keep rotating, depending on its mass, velocity, and distance from the axis of rotation.
When the total external torque on a system is zero, its total angular momentum is conserved.
The work done by a force on an object is equal to the change in its kinetic energy, similar to the work-energy theorem in linear motion.

concepts.concept_title: Angular Momentum of a System of Particles
concepts.description: The total angular momentum of a system of particles is given by the sum of the angular momentum of each particle.
The total angular momentum of a system of particles is given by L = ∑ r i ×p i.

concepts.concept_title: Relation Between Angular Momentum and Moment of Inertia
concepts.description: The angular momentum of a rotating rigid body is related to its moment of inertia and angular velocity by L = Iω.

concepts.concept_title: Angular Momentum of a Rigid Body
concepts.description: The angular momentum of a rigid body is given by L = ∑ r i ×p i, where r i is the perpendicular distance of the i-th particle from the axis of rotation.

concepts.concept_title: Angular Momentum of a Symmetric Rigid Body
concepts.description: The angular momentum of a symmetric rigid body is zero, as the angular momentum of each particle cancels out due to symmetry.
The ladder rule for angular momentum states that L = L + L, where L is the angular momentum of a rotating rigid body and L is the angular momentum of the ith particle.
Torque is related to the time derivative of angular momentum by τ = dL/dt.
The angular momentum of a closed system is conserved, meaning that the total angular momentum remains constant over time.

concepts.concept_title: Principle of Conservation of Angular Momentum
concepts.description: The total angular momentum of a system remains constant when no external torque acts on it.

concepts.concept_title: Moment of Inertia (I) and Its Relation to Angular Speed
concepts.description: I = Iω = constant for a system with no external torque, where I is the moment of inertia and ω is the angular speed.
τ = Iα, where τ is the torque, I is the moment of inertia, and α is the angular acceleration.

concepts.concept_title: Conservation of Angular Momentum in Rotational Motion
concepts.description: The component of angular momentum perpendicular to the axis of rotation remains constant, resulting in a change in the angular speed.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Changing Moment of Inertia on Angular Speed
concepts.description: Stretching arms increases I, resulting in decreased ω, while bringing arms closer to the body decreases I, resulting in increased ω.
Angular velocity is a vector with magnitude ω = dθ/dt and it is directed along the axis of rotation.
The cross product of two vectors a and b is a vector written as a×b, with magnitude |a||b|sinθ and direction by the right-handed screw or the right-hand rule.

concepts.concept_title: Linear Velocity of a Rotating Particle
concepts.description: The linear velocity of a particle of a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis is given by v = ω × r, where r is the position vector of the particle with respect to an origin along the fixed axis.
The centre of mass of a system of n particles is defined as the point whose position vector is ∑mri/M, where M is the total mass of the system.

concepts.concept_title: Velocity of the Centre of Mass
concepts.description: The velocity of the centre of mass of a system of particles is given by V = P/M, where P is the linear momentum of the system.
The angular momentum of a system of n particles about the origin is given by L = Σr × pi, where i ranges from 1 to n.
The torque or moment of force on a system of n particles about the origin is given by τ = Σr × Fi, where i ranges from 1 to n.

concepts.concept_title: Mechanical Equilibrium of a Rigid Body
concepts.description: A rigid body is in mechanical equilibrium if it is in translational equilibrium (ΣF = 0) and rotational equilibrium (Στ = 0).
The moment of inertia of a rigid body about an axis is defined by the formula I = Σmr^2, where r is the perpendicular distance of the ith point of the body from the axis.
Kinetic energy can be separated into two parts: the kinetic energy of a system about its centre of mass (K') and the kinetic energy of the centre of mass

concepts.concept_title: Newton's Second Law for Finite Sized Bodies
concepts.description: Newton's Second Law is extended to finite sized bodies (or systems of particles)
The time rate of change of the total angular momentum is equal to the total external torque
The vanishing of the total external force and the vanishing of the total external torque are independent conditions
The total torque on a system is independent of the origin if the total external force is zero

concepts.concept_title: Centre of Gravity Centre of Mass
concepts.description: The centre of gravity of a body coincides with its centre of mass only if the gravitational field is uniform
The angular momentum and angular velocity are not necessarily parallel vectors
The moment of inertia of a body about a rotation axis depends on its mass distribution
The location of the centre of mass of different objects such as a sphere, cylinder, ring, and cube can be found using symmetry arguments
The approximate location of the centre of mass in a diatomic molecule like HCl can be found using the masses of the atoms
The speed of the centre of mass of a system does not change when internal forces act on the system
The dot product of two vectors and the cross product of two other vectors forms a parallelepiped
The components of the angular momentum of a particle can be found using the position and momentum vectors of the particle
If a particle moves only in the x-y plane, its angular momentum has only a z-component
The total torque on a system is the sum of the torques due to each external force acting on the system

concepts.concept_title: Angular momentum vector of two-particle system
concepts.description: The angular momentum vector of a two-particle system is the same, regardless of the point chosen to measure it from.

concepts.concept_title: Center of gravity of a non-uniform bar
concepts.description: The center of gravity of a non-uniform bar is its weight-less balance point.

concepts.concept_title: Force exerted by the ground on wheels of a car
concepts.description: The force exerted by the level ground on each front wheel and each back wheel of a car can be calculated using the car's center of gravity.
Torques of equal magnitude applied to different objects can result in different angular speeds.
The kinetic energy associated with the rotation of a solid cylinder can be calculated using its angular speed and moment of inertia.

concepts.concept_title: Angular momentum of a rotating object
concepts.description: The angular momentum of an object rotating about its axis can be calculated using its moment of inertia and angular speed.
When the moment of inertia of an object decreases, its angular speed increases, resulting in an increase in rotational kinetic energy.

concepts.concept_title: Angular acceleration of a rotating object
concepts.description: The angular acceleration of an object can be calculated using the torque applied to it and its moment of inertia.

concepts.concept_title: Power required to maintain uniform angular speed
concepts.description: The power required to maintain a rotating object at a uniform angular speed can be calculated using the torque applied to it and its angular speed.

concepts.concept_title: Center of gravity of a flat body with a hole
concepts.description: The center of gravity of a flat body with a circular hole can be found by considering the hole as a weight-less object.

concepts.concept_title: Balance of a meter stick with coins
concepts.description: The balance point of a meter stick can be affected by the addition of weights at different locations.

concepts.concept_title: Moment of inertia of a rotating object
concepts.description: The moment of inertia of an object can be influenced by its shape, size, and mass distribution.

concepts.concept_title: Torque required to maintain uniform angular speed
concepts.description: The torque required to maintain a rotating object at a uniform angular speed can be influenced by the presence of friction.

concepts.concept_title: Power required to accelerate a rotating object
concepts.description: The power required to accelerate a rotating object can be influenced by its moment of inertia, angular acceleration, and friction.

concepts.concept_title: Moment of Inertia of a Diatomic Molecule
concepts.description: Calculates the moment of inertia of a diatomic molecule about an axis through its centre perpendicular to the bond axis.

concepts.concept_title: Moment of Inertia and Total Kinetic Energy Relation
concepts.description: Derives the relation between moment of inertia and total kinetic energy of a moving diatomic molecule.

concepts.concept_title: Kinetic Energy and Angular Velocity Relation
concepts.description: Relates the kinetic energy and angular velocity of a rotating diatomic molecule.
Calculates the average angular velocity of a diatomic molecule based on given conditions.
Galileo recognised that all bodies, irrespective of their masses, accelerate towards the earth with a constant acceleration.
Observations of planets and stars have been a subject of attention since early times, and Kepler's laws describe their motion.

The Italian physicist Galileo recognised that all bodies, irrespective of their masses, attract each other with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
Gravitational potential energy is the energy of an object due to its position in a gravitational field.
Escape speed is the minimum speed required for an object to escape from a celestial body's gravitational pull.
The total energy of an orbiting satellite is the sum of its kinetic energy and gravitational potential energy.
An ellipse is a closed curve formed by stretching a string between two points (focii) and drawing a curve with a pencil while keeping the string taut.
Planets move in elliptical orbits with the Sun at one of the focii.
The line joining a planet to the Sun sweeps equal areas in equal intervals of time.
The square of the time period of revolution of a planet is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis of the ellipse.
Perihelion is the closest point to the Sun, and aphelion is the farthest point in an elliptical orbit.
The semi-major axis is half the distance between the two focii of an ellipse.
The law states that the area swept by a planet's radius vector in a given time is constant. This is a consequence of conservation of angular momentum.
The law states that the force between two objects is proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
The angular momentum of a planet remains constant as it revolves around the Sun due to the conservation of angular momentum, leading to Kepler's second law.

concepts.concept_title: Equation of angular momentum for a central force
concepts.description: The angular momentum of a planet in a central force field is conserved and can be expressed as L = mr^2/dt (m, r, and v being mass, radius, and velocity).

concepts.concept_title: Gravitational force and conservation of momentum
concepts.description: The gravitational force between two objects is attractive and conserves the total momentum of the system, leading to a mutual gravitational attraction.

concepts.concept_title: Gravitational force as a central force
concepts.description: The gravitational force is a central force that acts along the line joining the masses, leading to the conservation of angular momentum.
The universal law of gravitation was experimentally verified by observing the motion of the Moon around the Earth and comparing the predicted acceleration of the Moon with the observed acceleration.

Newton's law states that every body in the universe attracts every other body with a force directly proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
The force F between two point masses m and M is given by F = G * m * M / r^2, where G is the universal gravitational constant and r is the distance between the masses.

concepts.concept_title: Equation of Gravitational Force in Vector Form
concepts.description: The gravitational force F between two point masses m and M can be expressed in vector form as F = -G * m * M / r^2 * r̂, where r̂ is the unit vector from m to M.

concepts.concept_title: Gravitational Force Between Multiple Point Masses
concepts.description: The total force on a point mass m due to multiple point masses M1, M2, M3, etc. is the vector sum of the individual forces exerted by each of these masses.

concepts.concept_title: Gravitational Force on a Point Mass Near a Hollow Spherical Shell
concepts.description: The gravitational force on a point mass m due to a hollow spherical shell of uniform density is the same as if the entire mass of the shell is concentrated at its center.

concepts.concept_title: Gravitational force due to spherical shell
concepts.description: Gravitational force on a point mass inside a spherical shell of uniform density is zero, due to symmetry.

concepts.concept_title: Resultant gravitational force on a point mass inside a spherical shell
concepts.description: Resultant gravitational force on a point mass inside a spherical shell is zero, due to symmetry and principle of superposition.

concepts.concept_title: Calculation of gravitational force on a point mass inside a spherical body
concepts.description: Gravitational force on a point mass inside a spherical body is calculated by integrating the force due to each point mass in the body.

concepts.concept_title: Equivalence of extended object and point mass
concepts.description: Extended object (like the earth) can be treated as a point mass for calculation of gravitational force, if its mass is concentrated at its centre.

concepts.concept_title: Gravitational force inside and outside a spherical body
concepts.description: Gravitational force inside a spherical body is different from that outside the body, due to concentration of mass at the centre.
The restoring torque in a suspended wire is directly proportional to the angle of twist, τ ∝ θ.
G can be measured using the Cavendish's experiment, where the gravitational force between two spherical balls is measured.

concepts.concept_title: Gravitational Force on a Mass at a Distance
concepts.description: The gravitational force on a mass depends on its distance from the center of the Earth, G F ∝ 1/r^2.
The acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the Earth is G M / R^2, g = (G M / R^3) * h.
At a height h above the Earth's surface, the gravitational acceleration g h is given by g h = g (1 - 2h/R) , where g is the surface acceleration due to gravity and R is the Earth's radius.
Cavendish's experiment involves measuring the gravitational acceleration g and the Earth's radius R to estimate the value of G using the formula g = GM/R^2.

concepts.concept_title: Acceleration Due to Gravity Below the Earth's Surface
concepts.description: At a depth d below the Earth's surface, the acceleration due to gravity g d is given by g d = GM/(R - d)^2.

concepts.concept_title: Force Due to a Spherical Shell
concepts.description: A point mass outside a spherical shell experiences no net gravitational force due to the shell.
The potential energy of a point mass at a height h above the Earth's surface is given by W = mgh + W, where m is the mass, g is the surface acceleration due to gravity, and W is a constant.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done in Lifting a Particle
concepts.description: The work done in lifting a particle of mass m from a height h 1 to a height h 2 above the Earth's surface is given by W = mgh2 - mgh1.
The gravitational potential energy is the energy stored in a body due to its position and is defined as the work done in displacing the body from its initial position to the given position.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done in Lifting a Particle
concepts.description: The work done in lifting a particle from a distance r1 to r2 is given by the integral of gravitational force over the displacement.
Conservative forces are those for which the work done is independent of the path of displacement.
Gravitational potential energy is the energy associated with the force of gravity and is given by the negative integral of gravitational force over the displacement.
The potential energy at infinity is defined as zero and is used as a reference point to calculate the potential energy at other points.
The gravitational potential is defined as the potential energy of a unit mass at a given point and is used to calculate the potential energy of other masses.

concepts.concept_title: Gravitational Potential at the Centre of a Surface
concepts.description: The gravitational potential at the centre of a square of side length l is given by a specific formula involving the gravitational constant G and the mass M of the surface.
The total potential energy of an isolated system is the sum of the potential energies of its constituent particles.
The minimum speed required for an object to escape from the earth corresponds to the case when the total energy of the projectile at infinity equals zero.

The gravitational potential energy of an object is given by the negative product of the gravitational constant, the mass of the object, and the mass of the Earth, divided by the distance between their centers.
The total energy of a closed system remains constant over time, which helps in understanding various phenomena, such as the trajectory of a projectile.

The minimum speed required for an object to escape from a massive body such as the Earth is given by a specific expression which balances the initial kinetic energy and gravitational potential energy.

concepts.concept_title: Kinetic Energy and Potential Energy Relationship
concepts.description: The kinetic energy of an object equals the difference between the total energy at the point of projection and the gravitational potential energy at that point.
An object in a uniform gravitational field is acted upon by two mutually opposing gravitational forces, resulting in a specific trajectory.
The minimum energy required for an object to escape from a massive body, such as the Earth, is zero, which corresponds to a specific expression involving the initial kinetic energy.

concepts.concept_title: Gravitational Potential Energy at a Height above the Earth
concepts.description: Gravitational potential energy of an object at a height above the earth is given by the equation E = -GMm/2h, where M is the mass of the earth, m is the mass of the object, G is the gravitational constant, and h is the height above the earth.

concepts.concept_title: Escape Velocity from the Earth's Surface
concepts.description: Escape velocity from the earth's surface is the minimum speed required for an object to escape the earth's gravitational pull. It is given by the equation v escape = sqrt(2GM/R), where M is the mass of the earth, G is the gravitational constant, and R is the radius of the earth.

concepts.concept_title: Escape Velocity from the Surface of a Celestial Body
concepts.description: Escape velocity from the surface of a celestial body is different from that of the earth and is given by the equation v escape = sqrt(2GM/R), where M is the mass of the celestial body and R is its radius.

concepts.concept_title: Period of a Satellite Orbiting the Earth
concepts.description: The period of a satellite orbiting the earth is given by the equation T0 = 2πr0 / g, where r0 is the radius of the satellite's orbit and g is the acceleration due to gravity on the earth's surface.
Tidal forces are the differences in gravitational pull between two celestial bodies, resulting in the displacement of an object from the center of the celestial body.

concepts.concept_title: Centripetal Force in a Circular Orbit
concepts.description: The centripetal force required for an object to move in a circular orbit is provided by the gravitational force. The centripetal force can be calculated using the formula F(centripetal) = (R + h) * m * v^2.

concepts.concept_title: Gravitational Force in a Circular Orbit
concepts.description: The gravitational force acting on a satellite in a circular orbit is equal to the mass of the satellite multiplied by the acceleration due to gravity at the radius of the orbit.

concepts.concept_title: Kepler's Third Law: Time Period and Orbital Radius
concepts.description: According to Kepler's third law, the square of the time period of a planet's orbit is proportional to the cube of its orbital radius.

concepts.concept_title: Orbital Period and Mass of Mars
concepts.description: The orbital period of Mars can be used to calculate its mass, given the fact that Mars and Earth move in circular orbits around the Sun.

concepts.concept_title: Mass of the Earth using the Moon's Orbit
concepts.description: The mass of the Earth can be calculated using the data of the time period and radius of the moon's orbit, assuming a circular orbit.
The kinetic energy of an orbiting satellite can be calculated using the formula 1/2 * m * v^2, where m is the mass of the satellite and v is its speed.

concepts.concept_title: Gravitational Potential Energy and Total Energy
concepts.description: The gravitational potential energy of a satellite in a circular orbit can be calculated using the formula - G * m * M / (R + h), where G is the gravitational constant, m is the mass of the satellite, M is the mass of the Earth, and h is the height of the satellite.
The escape velocity from the Earth can be calculated using the formula v = sqrt(2 * G * M / R), where G is the gravitational constant, M is the mass of the Earth, and R is the radius of the Earth.
Gravitational force is given by the law of universal gravitation, F = G (m1m2)/r^2, where G is the universal gravitational constant.
When finding the resultant gravitational force on a particle due to multiple masses, the principle of superposition is used to add the individual forces.

Kepler's laws state that planets move in elliptical orbits with the Sun at one of the focal points, the radius vector sweeps out equal areas in equal time intervals, and the square of the orbital period is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis.

concepts.concept_title: Relation between Period and Radius for Circular Orbits
concepts.description: The period T and radius R of a circular orbit are related by the equation T^2 = (4π^2/GM_s)R^3, where M_s is the mass of the Sun.

concepts.concept_title: Total Energy of a Satellite in a Circular Orbit
concepts.description: The total energy of a satellite in a circular orbit is given by E = -GM_m / (2R), where M_m is the mass of the satellite and R is its orbital radius.
The semi-major axis a is used in place of R in the equation for elliptical orbits.

concepts.concept_title: Equation for gravitational potential at height h
concepts.description: The gravitational potential g(h) at height h is given by the equation g(h) = g(0)/(1 - 2h/R) for h << R.

concepts.concept_title: Equation for gravitational acceleration at depth d
concepts.description: The gravitational acceleration g(d) at depth d is given by the equation g(d) = g(0)/(1 - d/R) for depth d below the earth's surface.
The gravitational potential energy V is given by the formula V = -Gm1m2/r.

concepts.concept_title: Total potential energy for a system of particles
concepts.description: The total potential energy for a system of particles is the sum of energies for all pairs of particles, using the formula given by the gravitational potential energy formula.

concepts.concept_title: Total mechanical energy of a particle
concepts.description: The total mechanical energy of a particle is given by E = (1/2)mv^2 - GmM/r, where m, v, and r are the mass, velocity, and distance of the particle.

concepts.concept_title: Total energy of a particle in a circular orbit
concepts.description: The total energy of a particle in a circular orbit is given by E = -GmM/(2a), where a is the radius of the orbit.

concepts.concept_title: Escape speed from the surface of the Earth
concepts.description: The escape speed from the surface of the Earth is given by ve = sqrt(2GME/R), where M is the mass of the Earth and R is the radius of the Earth.

concepts.concept_title: Gravitational force on a particle outside a uniform spherical shell or solid sphere
concepts.description: The gravitational force on a particle outside a uniform spherical shell or solid sphere is as though the mass of the sphere or shell were concentrated at the centre of the sphere.

concepts.concept_title: Gravitational force on a particle inside a uniform spherical shell
concepts.description: The gravitational force on a particle inside a uniform spherical shell is zero.

concepts.concept_title: Gravitational force on a particle inside a homogeneous solid sphere
concepts.description: The gravitational force on a particle inside a homogeneous solid sphere acts towards the centre of the sphere.

concepts.concept_title: Conservation of Angular Momentum in Gravitational Influence
concepts.description: Total angular momentum of an object is conserved under gravitational influence.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship Between Angular Momentum Conservation and Kepler's Second Law
concepts.description: Angular momentum conservation leads to Kepler's second law, which is applicable to central forces, not just inverse square law of gravitation.

concepts.concept_title: Kepler's Third Law for Planets and Satellites in Circular Orbits
concepts.description: The constant K in Kepler's third law (T2 = K R3) is the same for all planets in circular orbits and satellites orbiting the Earth.

concepts.concept_title: Weightlessness in Space Satellites Due to Free Fall
concepts.description: An astronaut in a space satellite experiences weightlessness due to both the astronaut and the satellite being in 'free fall' towards the Earth.
The gravitational potential energy (V) of two particles separated by a distance 'r' is given by V = -Gm1m2 / 2r.

concepts.concept_title: Total Mechanical Energy and Gravitational Potential Energy
concepts.description: The total mechanical energy of an object is the sum of its kinetic energy and gravitational potential energy, which can be negative, especially for objects at a large distance from a massive body.
The expression mgh for potential energy is an approximation of the difference in the gravitational potential energy.

Although the gravitational force between two particles is central, the force between two finite rigid bodies is not necessarily along the line joining their centre of mass, except for spherically symmetric bodies.

concepts.concept_title: Gravitational Force Inside a Spherical Shell
concepts.description: The gravitational force on a particle inside a spherical shell is zero, even though it is not shielded from other gravitational forces.
Gravitational shielding is not possible by putting a body in a hollow sphere or by any other means.

concepts.concept_title: Gravitational force on Earth by Sun vs Moon
concepts.description: The gravitational force of the Sun on Earth is greater than that of the Moon, but the tidal effect of the Moon's pull is greater.

concepts.concept_title: Increase of acceleration due to gravity with increasing altitude
concepts.description: Acceleration due to gravity decreases with increasing altitude.

concepts.concept_title: Increase of acceleration due to gravity at increasing depth
concepts.description: Acceleration due to gravity increases with increasing depth.

concepts.concept_title: Independence of acceleration due to gravity on mass
concepts.description: Acceleration due to gravity is independent of both the mass of the Earth and the mass of the body.

concepts.concept_title: Accuracy of potential energy difference formula
concepts.description: The formula –GMm(1/r – 1/r2) is more accurate than the formula 2mg(r – r) for the difference of potential energy between two points.

concepts.concept_title: Orbital size of a planet moving twice as fast as Earth
concepts.description: The orbital size of a planet moving twice as fast as Earth around the Sun would be less than that of Earth.
The mass of Jupiter can be calculated using the orbital period and radius of its satellite Io.

concepts.concept_title: Time taken by a star to complete one revolution in a galaxy
concepts.description: The time taken by a star to complete one revolution in a galaxy can be calculated using its distance from the galactic center and the diameter of the galaxy.
The total energy of an orbiting satellite is the negative of its kinetic energy.

concepts.concept_title: Energy required to launch a satellite
concepts.description: The energy required to launch an orbiting satellite out of Earth's influence is less than the energy required to project a stationary object at the same height out of Earth's influence.

The escape speed of a body from the Earth does not depend on the mass of the body, the location from where it is projected, the direction of projection, or the height of the location from where the body is launched.
A comet in a highly elliptical orbit around the Sun has constant angular momentum and total energy throughout its orbit.
An astronaut in space may experience orientation problems.

concepts.concept_title: Gravitational intensity at the center of a hemispherical shell
concepts.description: The direction of the gravitational intensity at the center of a hemispherical shell of uniform mass density is along the radius vector toward the center.
Elasticity is the property of a body by which it tends to regain its original size and shape when the applied force is removed.

concepts.concept_title: Types of Materials: Elastic and Plastic
concepts.description: Materials like putty or mud have no tendency to regain their previous shape and are called plastic, whereas materials like elastic springs regain their original shape.

concepts.concept_title: Importance of Elastic Behaviour in Engineering Design
concepts.description: Knowledge of elastic properties of materials like steel, concrete, etc. is essential in engineering design of buildings, bridges, automobiles, and other structures.
Solids are deformed to a small or large extent depending on the nature of the material and the magnitude of the deforming force.
When forces are applied to a body, it experiences a restoring force per unit area or stress, which causes strain or relative displacement between opposite faces.
Stress is defined as the restoring force per unit area, given by the formula F/A.

concepts.concept_title: Unit and Dimensional Formula of Stress
concepts.description: The SI unit of stress is N m–2 or pascal (Pa), and its dimensional formula is [ ML–1T–2 ].

concepts.concept_title: Types of Stress: Tensile, Compressive and Shearing Stress
concepts.description: Tensile stress occurs when a cylinder is stretched by two equal forces, compressive stress occurs when a cylinder is compressed, and shearing stress occurs when a cylinder is subjected to a deforming force.
Shearing strain is defined as the ratio of relative displacement of the faces ∆x to the length of the cylinder L, and can be approximated as tan θ ≈ θ for small angles.
Longitudinal strain is the change in the length of a cylinder when subjected to tensile or compressive stress.
The internal restoring force per unit area is known as hydraulic stress and is equal to the hydraulic pressure.
The ratio of change in volume to the original volume is known as volume strain.
For small deformations, stress and strain are proportional to each other.
The proportionality constant in Hooke's law is known as modulus of elasticity.
The point at which the stress and strain relationship changes is known as the yield point.
The stress at the yield point is known as yield strength.
The deformation that does not return to the original shape when the load is removed is known as plastic deformation.
The maximum stress a material can withstand without fracturing is known as ultimate tensile strength.
A graph that shows the relationship between the applied force and the resulting strain is known as a stress-strain curve.
The proportional region within the elastic limit of the stress-strain curve is of great importance for structural and manufacturing engineering designs.
The ratio of stress and strain, called modulus of elasticity, is found to be a characteristic of the material.
A stress-strain curve can be used to determine whether a material is brittle or ductile.
Stress and strain are directly proportional to each other within the elastic limit.
The ratio of tensile (or compressive) stress to the longitudinal strain is defined as Young’s modulus.
Strain is a dimensionless quantity.
The unit of Young’s modulus is the same as that of stress, i.e., N m–2 or Pascal (Pa).
Young’s moduli and yield strengths of some materials are given in a table.
Steel is more elastic than copper, brass, and aluminium.
Stress can be calculated using the formula: W/A = Y × (∆L/L).
Stress on an object is calculated as the total force applied divided by its cross-sectional area. Mathematically, it is represented as F/A.

Young's modulus is a measure of the stiffness of a material and is calculated by dividing the stress applied to a material by the strain it undergoes within the proportional limit. It is represented as Y = F/A × L/∆L.
Strain is a measure of the deformation of an object and is calculated as the ratio of the change in length to the original length. It is represented as ∆L/L.

concepts.concept_title: Calculation of Elongation due to Stretching
concepts.description: Elongation is the change in length of an object when a force is applied. It can be calculated using the formula ∆L = (F/A) × L/Y.
The load applied to a material can be calculated if the elongation and Young's modulus of the material are known. It can be calculated using the formula F/A = Y × ∆L/L.
Shear modulus is a measure of the rigidity of a material and is calculated by dividing the shear force applied to a material by the area of the material and the change in the angle of the two planes.
key_terms: change in the angle of the two planes

concepts.concept_title: Application of Young's Modulus in Real-Life Scenarios
concepts.description: Young's modulus is an essential concept in understanding the behavior of materials in various real-life scenarios, including the support of loads by human bones.
Shear modulus or modulus of rigidity is the ratio of shearing stress to the corresponding shearing strain.
Shear modulus (G) can be calculated using the formula G = (F/A)/(∆x/L) = (F × L)/(A × ∆x).
The SI unit of shear modulus is N m–2 or Pa.
The shear modulus values for various materials are given in Table 9.2.
Bulk modulus (B) is the ratio of hydraulic stress to the corresponding hydraulic strain.
Bulk modulus (B) can be calculated using the formula B = – p/(∆V/V).
The SI unit of bulk modulus is the same as that of pressure, i.e., N m–2 or Pa.
The reciprocal of bulk modulus is called compressibility and is denoted by k.
Compressibility (k) can be calculated using the formula k = (1/B) = – (1/∆p) × (∆V/V).
Tensile stress is a type of stress where two equal and opposite forces are applied parallel to the opposite faces of a body, causing elongation or compression.
Compressive stress is a type of stress where two equal and opposite forces are applied perpendicular to the opposite faces of a body, causing compression or shortening.
Shear stress is a type of stress where two equal and opposite forces are applied parallel to opposite surfaces of a body, causing a change in shape without a change in volume.
Bulk modulus is the measure of a body's resistance to change in volume when compressed or expanded by a force.
Young's modulus is the measure of a body's stiffness, or its ability to resist deformation under a tensile or compressive stress.
Elastic potential energy is the energy stored in a stretched or compressed body due to the work done against the internal force.
The strain perpendicular to the applied force is called lateral strain.
Poisson’s ratio is a ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain, and is directly proportional to longitudinal strain within the elastic limit.
Longitudinal strain is the ratio of elongation under stress to the original length of the wire.
The elastic behaviour of materials plays an important role in everyday life, and is crucial for designing engineering structures.
Young’s modulus is a measure of the ability of a material to resist stretching under load, and is crucial for designing structures that can withstand bending.
Buckling occurs when a beam bends or compresses beyond its elastic limit, resulting in a loss of structural integrity.
The elastic limit is the maximum stress a material can withstand without undergoing permanent deformation.
Yield strength is the maximum stress a material can withstand before undergoing plastic deformation.
Shearing stress is the force per unit area that causes deformation by sliding along a plane. Elastic limit is the maximum stress a material can withstand without permanent deformation.
Beams bend under load due to the distribution of stress across their cross-sections. The amount of bending depends on the material's Young's modulus and the beam's dimensions.

concepts.concept_title: Pressure Due to Weight of a Mountain
concepts.description: The pressure due to the weight of a mountain is calculated as hρg, where h is the height of the mountain, ρ is the density of the material, and g is the acceleration due to gravity.
Critical shearing stress is the maximum stress at which a material starts to flow or deform permanently. For rocks, this is typically around 30 × 10^7 N m^-2.

concepts.concept_title: Equation for Sagging of a Beam
concepts.description: The amount of sagging of a beam under load is given by the equation δ = Wl^3 / (4bd^3Y), where δ is the sag, W is the load, l is the length, b is the breadth, d is the depth, and Y is the Young's modulus.
Stress is the restoring force per unit area, while strain is the fractional change in dimension.
Tensile, compressive, shearing, and hydraulic stresses are four types of stresses.
Stress is directly proportional to strain for many materials within the linear part of the stress-strain curve.

concepts.concept_title: Young's modulus, shear modulus, and bulk modulus
concepts.description: Young's modulus, shear modulus, and bulk modulus are used to describe the elastic behavior of objects.
Tensile strain is proportional to the applied force and inversely proportional to the area and Young's modulus.
Shear stress is proportional to the applied force and inversely proportional to the area and shear modulus.
Bulk modulus is used to describe the compression of a fluid and is related to bulk modulus.
Hooke's law is valid only in the linear part of the stress-strain curve.
Metals have Young's modulus and shear modulus, while elastomers do not obey Hooke's law.

Stress is the ratio of force to cross-sectional area, while strain is the ratio of change in length to original length. Elastic constants include Young's modulus, Poisson ratio, and modulus of rigidity.

Young's modulus is the ratio of stress to strain within the proportional limit. A higher Young's modulus means a material requires more force to produce a certain deformation. Elasticity is often misdefined as the ability of a material to stretch.

concepts.concept_title: Stress Distribution and Strain in Multiple Directions
concepts.description: A material deformed in one direction may undergo strain in other directions. Poisson’s ratio describes this lateral strain.
Stress is a scalar quantity, not a vector, as it does not have direction.

concepts.concept_title: Comparing Young's Modulus from Different Materials
concepts.description: Two wires of different materials stretching the same amount under the same load have the same stress, which can be used to find their ratio of Young’s moduli.
A strain-stress graph can be used to find Young’s modulus and approximate yield strength from the graph’s slope and the point where it begins to deviate from linearity.
Comparing stress-strain graphs of two materials allows for the identification of the material with greater Young’s modulus and the stronger material.
Analyzing two statements on the Young’s modulus of rubber and steel, as well as the stretching of a coil and its shear modulus, to determine which ones are true or false.
Calculating the elongations of two wires under different conditions to determine their Young’s moduli or their material properties.
Using the shear modulus to calculate the vertical deflection of a face of a cube under a certain load.
Using the load distribution and shear modulus to calculate the compressional strain of a column.

concepts.concept_title: Calculating Strain Due to Tensile Stress
concepts.description: Using the tensile stress and shear modulus to calculate the resulting strain in a material.
Calculating the resulting stress on a cable due to a certain load and radius.
The maximum stress is the maximum force per unit area that a material can withstand. The maximum load is the maximum weight that a material can support.
The elastic behavior of a solid under stress is described by the relationship between stress, strain, and Young's modulus. Young's modulus is a measure of the stiffness of a solid.

concepts.concept_title: Ratios of wire diameters for equal tension
concepts.description: The forces in three wires, each supporting a load, are in the ratio of their diameters when the tension is equal.

concepts.concept_title: Elongation of a wire in a vertical circle
concepts.description: The elongation of a wire whirled in a vertical circle is due to the tensile force acting on it. The force is directed along the wire and parallel to its axis.
The bulk modulus is a measure of the resistance of a fluid to change in volume when subjected to pressure. The bulk modulus of water is much larger than that of air.
The bulk modulus of a fluid can be calculated from the change in pressure and the resulting change in volume.

concepts.concept_title: Density of water at a given depth
concepts.description: The density of water varies with depth due to pressure. The density of water at a given depth can be calculated from the pressure at that depth.

concepts.concept_title: Fractional change in volume of a fluid under pressure
concepts.description: The change in volume of a fluid under pressure is proportional to the pressure and the bulk modulus of the fluid.

concepts.concept_title: Volume contraction of a solid under pressure
concepts.description: The change in volume of a solid under pressure is proportional to the pressure and the bulk modulus of the solid.

concepts.concept_title: Change in volume of a fluid with a given pressure
concepts.description: The change in volume of a fluid with a given pressure is inversely proportional to the bulk modulus of the fluid.

concepts.concept_title: Pressure to compress a fluid by a given percentage
concepts.description: The pressure required to compress a fluid by a given percentage can be calculated from the bulk modulus and the change in volume.
Fluids are substances that can flow and include both liquids and gases. They exhibit properties that distinguish them from solids.

concepts.concept_title: Compressibility of Solids, Liquids, and Gases
concepts.description: Solids and liquids have lower compressibility compared to gases. This means a small change in volume due to external pressure.
Fluids offer very little resistance to shear stress and their shape can change with application of a small shear stress.
Pressure is a scalar quantity that is the force exerted per unit area on an object's surface. A smaller area results in greater pressure.

concepts.concept_title: Impact of Force and Coverage Area
concepts.description: The impact or pressure is increased when the force and its coverage area are smaller.
Pressure is a scalar quantity defined as the normal force acting per unit area.
The dimension of pressure is [ML–1T–2].
The SI unit of pressure is the pascal (Pa).
Density is a scalar quantity defined as mass per unit volume.
The dimension of density is [ML–3].
The SI unit of density is kg m–3.
Relative density is the ratio of density of a substance to the density of water at 4°C.
The total cross-sectional area of the femurs is calculated as A = 2 × 10 cm2 = 20 × 10–4 m2.

concepts.concept_title: Pressure Exerted by a Fluid at Rest
concepts.description: The pressure exerted by a fluid at rest is the same in all directions, and it is a scalar quantity.

concepts.concept_title: Pressure at Opposite Ends of a Fluid Element
concepts.description: The pressure at opposite ends of a fluid element must be equal for the element to be in equilibrium.

concepts.concept_title: Variation of Pressure with Depth in a Fluid at Rest
concepts.description: The pressure in a fluid at rest increases with depth due to the force of gravity.
The pressure in a fluid at rest is the same at all points if they are at the same height.
Pressure is a scalar quantity and cannot be assigned a direction.
The pressure difference (P - P0) exerted on an area A is equal to the weight (mg) of the fluid above that area.
The pressure of the atmosphere at any point is equal to the weight of a column of air of unit cross-sectional area extending from that point to the top of the atmosphere.
Absolute pressure is the pressure at a point in a fluid, greater than atmospheric pressure due to the weight of the fluid.
Water at the bottom of vessels of different shapes and sizes will be at the same level due to hydrostatic pressure.
P = ρgh, where P is the gauge pressure, ρ is the density of the fluid, g is the acceleration due to gravity and h is the height of the fluid column.
A device used to measure atmospheric pressure, consisting of a long glass tube containing mercury inverted into a trough of mercury.
The pressure increases with depth due to the weight of the fluid, with an increase of 10 m of water approximately equal to 1 atm.
The pressure at any point in a fluid is the same at all points at the same horizontal level due to hydrostatic pressure.
1 torr is equal to 133 Pa, which is a pressure equivalent for mm of mercury (Hg) used in medicine and physiology.
The atmospheric pressure at sea level is equivalent to 760 mm of mercury (Hg) or one atmosphere (1 atm), and it is also expressed in millibar, where 1 millibar is equal to 0.001 atmosphere.
Gauge pressure is the difference between the absolute pressure and atmospheric pressure, and it is typically measured in torr or mm of Hg.
An open tube manometer is used to measure pressure differences by comparing the heights of two columns of different densities of liquids.

concepts.concept_title: Pressure and density of air at high altitude
concepts.description: The density of air decreases with height, and the atmospheric pressure also decreases with altitude, leading to a decrease in the value of g.

concepts.concept_title: Pressure and gauge pressure at ocean depth
concepts.description: The absolute pressure increases with depth, while the gauge pressure remains the same as the surrounding atmospheric pressure.

concepts.concept_title: Force on submarine window at depth
concepts.description: The force acting on a window of a submarine at a certain depth is determined by the pressure difference between the surrounding water and the interior of the submarine.
Fluids are used in hydraulic devices to transmit pressure from one piston to another, as shown in a hydraulic lift.
The pressure exerted by a fluid is determined by the product of its density and acceleration due to gravity multiplied by the height of the fluid column.
Hydrostatic pressure is given by the equation P = ρgh, where ρ is the density of the fluid, g is the acceleration due to gravity, and h is the height of the fluid column.
Gauge pressure is the pressure of a fluid relative to atmospheric pressure and is given by P = ρgh.
When external pressure is applied to a fluid, it is transmitted undiminished and equally in all directions.
The mechanical advantage of a hydraulic machine is given by the ratio of the area of the larger piston to the area of the smaller piston.
Pascal's law states that when external pressure is applied on any part of a fluid, it is transmitted undiminished and equally in all directions.
In a hydraulic lift, the pressure is transmitted undiminished throughout the system, resulting in a large force being exerted on the load.
The force ratio in a hydraulic machine is given by the ratio of the forces exerted on the larger and smaller pistons.
The force exerted on a piston due to air pressure is transmitted undiminished throughout the fluid.
An ideal fluid, such as water, is considered to be perfectly incompressible, meaning its volume remains constant under all conditions.
Hydraulic brakes in automobiles use the principle of pressure transmission to produce a large retarding force on the wheels with a small input force on the pedal.
Streamline flow occurs when fluid particles follow smooth paths, with no crossing of paths, and the velocity of each particle remains constant at a given point in space.
Steady flow occurs when the velocity of each fluid particle remains constant at a given point in space, even if it changes at different points in space.
Streamlines are curves whose tangent at any point is in the direction of the fluid velocity at that point, describing the path of a fluid particle under steady flow.
In steady flow, no two streamlines can cross, and the map of flow is stationary in time.
The mass of fluid flowing out equals the mass flowing in, in all cases, for incompressible fluids.
The equation of continuity states that the volume flux or flow rate remains constant throughout the pipe of flow.
Bernoulli's equation relates the pressure difference between two points in a pipe to both velocity changes (kinetic energy change) and elevation (height) changes (potential energy change).
Bernoulli's equation can be applied to problems involving the flow of fluids, considering variables like pressure, velocity, and elevation.
Steady flow is achieved when fluid speed is low. Beyond a limiting value called critical speed, the flow loses steadiness and becomes turbulent.
Bernoulli's relation states that the sum of pressure, kinetic energy, and potential energy of a fluid remains constant along a streamline, assuming no energy loss due to friction.
The work done on a fluid is given by the product of pressure difference and volume displaced. Part of this work is used to change kinetic energy and part to change gravitational potential energy.

concepts.concept_title: Change in Kinetic Energy of a Fluid
concepts.description: The change in kinetic energy of a fluid is given by ρ∆V (v^2 - v'^2), where ρ is density, ∆V is volume, and v and v' are initial and final velocities.

concepts.concept_title: Change in Gravitational Potential Energy of a Fluid
concepts.description: The change in gravitational potential energy of a fluid is given by ρg∆V (h - h'), where ρ is density, g is acceleration due to gravity, and h and h' are initial and final heights.
According to Bernoulli's theorem, fluids must be incompressible and the elastic energy of the fluid is not taken into consideration.
Bernoulli's equation is derived from the equation of continuity and the equation of motion for a fluid.
Bernoulli's equation does not hold true for non-steady or turbulent flows.
The speed of efflux from an open tank is given by a formula identical to that of a freely falling body.
Bernoulli's principle can be used to explain the deviation of a ball's trajectory as it moves through the air.
The speed of efflux of fluid from a small hole is given by v = 2gh, where g is the acceleration due to gravity and h is the height of the fluid column
The spinning ball experiences a net upward force due to the difference in air pressure above and below it, resulting in an upward dynamic lift
Aeroplanes use a curved wing or aerofoil shape to generate an upward dynamic lift when air flows over and under it
The force that acts on a body, such as an airplane wing or a spinning ball, by virtue of its motion through a fluid, resulting in an upward force
Resistance to fluid motion, similar to friction when a solid moves on a surface, arising from relative motion between layers of the liquid
Not explicitly given in this chapter text, but can be used to estimate the increase in speed of air on the upper surface of a wing
Pressure difference between two points in a fluid is given by the product of the density of the fluid and the acceleration due to gravity and the height difference between the two points.
Viscosity is the measure of a fluid's resistance to flow, and it is defined as the ratio of shearing stress to the strain rate.
Fluid flow is classified into two types: laminar and turbulent, depending on the smoothness of the flow and the velocity distribution.
The coefficient of viscosity is a measure of a fluid's resistance to flow, and it is defined as the ratio of shearing stress to the strain rate.
The velocity of the fluid in a pipe varies from zero at the wall to a maximum at the center, and it can be calculated using the formula for shear stress.
The coefficient of viscosity can be measured using a device called a viscometer, which consists of a rotating cylinder and a stationary cylinder.
The viscosity of a fluid decreases with increasing temperature, but the opposite is true for gases.
The shear force on a fluid is the force that acts per unit area on the surface of the fluid, and is equal in magnitude to the tension in the string. The shear stress is the shear force per unit area.
The strain rate is the rate of change of the shear strain with time, and is given by the ratio of the change in displacement to the initial displacement.
Stokes' Law states that the viscous force experienced by a falling body is proportional to its velocity, and is given by the equation 6πηav = (4π/3) a3 (ρ-σ)g.

The terminal velocity of a falling body is the maximum velocity it reaches when the retarding force (viscous force) becomes equal to the force due to gravity, and is given by the equation v = 2a2 (ρ-σ)g / (9η).
The viscosity of a fluid is a measure of its resistance to flow, and is given by the ratio of the shear stress to the strain rate.
The equation of terminal velocity is v = 2a2 (ρ-σ)g / (9η), which relates the terminal velocity of a falling body to its radius, density, the density of the fluid, and the viscosity of the fluid.
A liquid has a free surface energy due to attraction between molecules
Surface phenomena are related to free surfaces of liquids and their energy
Molecules at a liquid surface have extra energy compared to interior molecules
Molecules near the surface experience a negative potential energy due to surrounding molecules
The average potential energy of all liquid molecules remains the same
The energy required to remove a molecule from the liquid is related to heat of evaporation
A surface of a liquid cannot be perfectly sharp due to molecular motion
The density of liquid molecules drops rapidly to zero near the surface
Surface tension is a force per unit length or surface energy per unit area acting in the plane of the interface between two substances.

concepts.concept_title: Surface Tension as Extra Energy at Interface
concepts.description: Surface tension is the extra energy that molecules at the interface have compared to molecules in the interior of the liquid.
Surface tension force per unit length is the force exerted by the liquid on the movable bar per unit length.
Work done against an internal force is stored as additional energy in the film, which is related to surface tension.
Surface energy is given by the formula: S = Fd/2l, where S is the surface tension, F is the force and l is the length of the film.
Surface tension falls with increasing temperature, similar to viscosity.
Surface energy is the energy of the interface between two materials and depends on both of them.
The ability of a liquid to spread on the surface of another material is determined by the angle of contact.
The surface tension of a liquid is the energy per unit area of its surface and can be measured by the weight required to balance the surface tension force.
Surface tension can be measured by balancing a weight against the surface tension force on a flat vertical glass plate.
