The angle between the tangent to the liquid surface and the surface of the other material is a measure of the wettability of the liquid.
At the contact line between the three phases, the surface forces between the media must be in equilibrium.
The angle of contact is the angle at which a liquid meets a solid surface. It is affected by the attractive forces between liquid and solid molecules.
Surface tension is a measure of the energy per unit area of a liquid surface. It causes liquids to exhibit surface phenomena such as capillary rise and droplet shapes.

concepts.concept_title: Pressure inside and outside a drop or bubble
concepts.description: The pressure inside a drop or bubble is higher than the pressure outside due to surface tension. The pressure difference depends on the radius of the drop or bubble.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of gravity on droplet shape
concepts.description: Gravity causes droplets to form a shape which minimizes surface area, resulting in a sphere. The effect of gravity can be neglected for small drops.

concepts.concept_title: Pressure in a liquid - air interface
concepts.description: The pressure in a liquid - air interface depends on the radius of curvature of the interface.
Water rises up in a narrow tube due to the pressure difference across the curved liquid-air interface.

The height of capillary rise can be calculated using the formula: (2T cosθ)/(rρg), where T is the surface tension, θ is the contact angle, r is the radius of the tube, ρ is the density of water, and g is the acceleration due to gravity.

concepts.concept_title: Extra Surface Energy of a spherical drop
concepts.description: The extra surface energy of a spherical drop can be calculated using the formula: (8πrΔS), where r is the radius of the drop and ΔS is the change in surface energy.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of wetting agents on contact angle
concepts.description: Wetting agents reduce the contact angle between a liquid and a solid, allowing the liquid to penetrate more effectively.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of non-wetting agents on contact angle
concepts.description: Non-wetting agents increase the contact angle between a liquid and a solid, making it harder for the liquid to penetrate.
The capillary rise is due to the pressure difference between the inside and outside of the bubble, which is balanced by the energy cost of expansion.
The pressure inside a bubble is given by the sum of atmospheric pressure and excess pressure due to surface tension.
The excess pressure inside a bubble is given by 2S/r, where S is the surface tension of the liquid-gas interface and r is the radius of the bubble.
The pressure outside a bubble is given by atmospheric pressure plus the pressure due to the height of the water column, h.
The capillary rise is related to the pressure difference between the inside and outside of the bubble, which is balanced by the energy cost of expansion.
The shape of the liquid meniscus (convex or concave) determines the pressure inside the bubble.
A fluid can flow and does not resist shape change due to its container. Liquids are incompressible with a free surface, while gases are compressible.
Average pressure is the force per unit area, calculated as P = F / A. Its unit is the pascal (Pa), which is also equal to N m^-2.
In a fluid at rest, pressure is the same at all points at the same height. Pressure changes applied to an enclosed fluid are undiminished and transmitted to all points.
The pressure in a fluid varies with depth h according to the expression P = P0 + ρgh, where ρ is the fluid density.
For an incompressible fluid, the volume passing any point per second is constant, given by v A = constant, where v is the velocity and A is the area of cross-section.
The sum of pressure (P), kinetic energy per unit volume (ρv^2 / 2), and potential energy per unit volume (ρgy) remains constant along a streamline in steady flow.
The ratio of shear stress to the time rate of shearing strain is known as the coefficient of viscosity (η).
The viscous drag force on a sphere moving through a fluid is given by F = 6πηav, where a is the sphere's radius and η is the fluid's viscosity.
Surface tension is the extra energy of molecules at an interface, given by force per unit length or surface energy per unit area.
Pressure exists in a fluid at all points, not just on solid boundaries.
An element of a fluid is in equilibrium due to equal pressures on its faces.
For incompressible fluids, the expression P + ρgh holds true.
Gauge pressure is the difference between actual pressure and atmospheric pressure.
Streamlines are a map of fluid flow, with two streamlines not intersecting in a steady flow.
Bernoulli's principle does not hold in the presence of viscous drag.
The coefficient of viscosity (η) decreases with temperature increase in liquids and increases in gases.
Surface tension arises due to excess potential energy of molecules at the surface.
Viscosity of gases decreases with temperature, while viscosity of liquids increases with temperature.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship between shear force and shear strain for solids and fluids
concepts.description: For solids, shearing force is proportional to shear strain. For fluids, shearing force is proportional to the rate of shear strain.

concepts.concept_title: Conservation of mass and Bernoulli's principle in fluid flow
concepts.description: Bernoulli's principle explains the increase in flow speed at a constriction in a fluid, following conservation of mass.
A plane model in a wind tunnel experiences turbulence at a smaller speed compared to an actual plane.
Blowing over a horizontal piece of paper keeps it horizontal due to pressure difference created by air flow.
Fast jets of water gush through openings between fingers while closing a water tap due to conservation of mass.

concepts.concept_title: Control of fluid flow using needle size and pressure
concepts.description: The size of the needle of a syringe controls flow rate better than the thumb pressure exerted while administering an injection.
A fluid flowing out of a small hole in a vessel results in a backward thrust on the vessel due to conservation of momentum.

concepts.concept_title: Trajectory of a spinning cricket ball
concepts.description: A spinning cricket ball in air does not follow a parabolic trajectory due to the influence of surface tension.

concepts.concept_title: Pressure exerted by a stationary object
concepts.description: A 50 kg girl wearing high heel shoes balances on a single heel, which exerts pressure on the horizontal floor.

concepts.concept_title: Height of a liquid column in a barometer
concepts.description: Torielli's barometer uses mercury, while Pascal's barometer uses French wine. The height of the wine column is determined for normal atmospheric pressure.

concepts.concept_title: Stress and pressure in ocean environments
concepts.description: A vertical off-shore structure is built to withstand a maximum stress. The structure's suitability is determined for an ocean depth of 3 km.
A hydraulic automobile lift is designed to lift cars with a maximum mass. The smaller piston bears a maximum pressure for the given area of cross-section.

concepts.concept_title: Densities and levels of liquid columns
concepts.description: U-tube contains water and methylated spirit separated by mercury. The levels of mercury columns are determined, and specific gravity of spirit is calculated.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of additional liquid on liquid levels
concepts.description: Additional water and spirit are poured into the U-tube, and the difference in mercury levels is calculated to determine the change in densities.
Bernoulli's equation is not applicable for the flow of water through a rapid in a river due to turbulent flow.

concepts.concept_title: Use of gauge and absolute pressures
concepts.description: It does not matter if one uses gauge or absolute pressures when applying Bernoulli's equation due to the principle remaining the same.
Glycerine flows steadily through a horizontal tube. The pressure difference between the two ends of the tube is determined based on the flow rate and viscosity.
The amount of upward force exerted on a wing due to the difference in air pressure above and below it.
The pressure of a fluid decreases as its velocity increases.

concepts.concept_title: Steady Flow of a Non-Viscous Liquid
concepts.description: The flow of a liquid with minimum or zero viscosity.
The speed of ejection of a liquid through small holes in a cylindrical tube.
The force acting along the surface of a liquid due to intermolecular forces.
The pressure inside a drop of a liquid, influenced by external pressure and its own surface tension.
The additional pressure inside a drop beyond the external atmospheric pressure.
The additional pressure inside a bubble beyond the external atmospheric pressure.
Temperature is a relative measure of an object's 'hotness' or 'coldness'. It is a comparison of the thermal energy of two objects.
Temperature is typically measured in units of Kelvin (K), degree Celsius (oC), or Fahrenheit (oF).
Heat transfer occurs between two systems or a system and its surroundings due to a temperature difference. Heat flows from a hotter body to a cooler body.

concepts.concept_title: SI Units of Heat Energy and Temperature
concepts.description: The SI unit of heat energy is joule (J) and the SI unit of temperature is Kelvin (K).
Temperature can be measured on various scales such as Celsius (oC) and Kelvin (K).
When an object is heated, changes may occur such as an increase in temperature, expansion, or a change in state.
Two familiar temperature scales are Fahrenheit and Celsius.
Volume of a liquid varies linearly with temperature over a wide range.
Thermometers are calibrated so that a numerical value may be assigned to a given temperature.
Ice point and steam point of water are two convenient absolute reference points.
When temperature is held constant, the pressure and volume of a quantity of gas are related as PV = constant.
When the pressure is held constant, the volume of a quantity of gas is related to the temperature as V/T = constant.
The variables that describe the behaviour of a given quantity of gas are pressure, volume, and temperature (P, V, and T).
PV/T is a constant for a given quantity of an ideal gas, represented as PV = µRT.
Universal gas constant (R) is a constant that represents the proportionality between PV and T.
A gas thermometer that measures temperature by holding the volume of a gas constant.
A temperature scale where the absolute zero is – 273.15 °C.
Pressure is directly proportional to temperature at constant volume, represented as P ∝ T.
Real gases deviate from the ideal gas law at low temperatures, resulting in a non-linear pressure-temperature relationship.
The theoretical temperature at which the pressure of an ideal gas becomes zero, estimated to be – 273.15 °C.
Increase in the dimensions of a body due to the increase in its temperature is called linear expansion
Characteristic property of a material that represents the fractional change in length due to a temperature change
Fractional change in length (∆l/l) is directly proportional to the change in temperature (∆T) for small changes
Increase in area of a body due to the increase in its temperature
Increase in volume of a body due to the increase in its temperature

concepts.concept_title: Coefficient of Volume Expansion (or Volume Expansivity)
concepts.description: Characteristic property of a substance that represents the fractional change in volume due to a temperature change
Increase in dimensions of a body due to the increase in its temperature

concepts.concept_title: Comparison of Thermal Expansion in Different Materials
concepts.description: Variation of thermal expansion in different materials (metals, alloys, glass, etc.)

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Temperature on Coefficient of Expansion
concepts.description: Change in coefficient of expansion with temperature
Difference in thermal expansion of solids and liquids
Thermal expansion of gases, more than solids and liquids

concepts.concept_title: Specific Examples of Materials and their Expansion Coefficients
concepts.description: Comparison of thermal expansion coefficients and properties of various materials (alloys, glass, etc.)

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Freezing on Lakes and Ponds
concepts.description: Thermal expansion and freezing effect on lakes and ponds due to water density change
Coefficient of volume expansion (α) is the change in volume per unit original volume per unit change in temperature.
Ideal gas equation: PV = μRT, where P is pressure, V is volume, μ is the number of moles, R is the gas constant, and T is the temperature.
Coefficient of linear expansion is the fractional change in length per unit change in temperature.

concepts.concept_title: Relation between Coefficient of Linear and Volume Expansion
concepts.description: α (volume expansion) = 3α (linear expansion), derived from the relationship between linear and volume expansion of a material.
Thermal stress is the stress developed in a material due to thermal expansion when constrained from expanding, given by α A ΔT (2 + α ΔT).
The linear expansion coefficient, α, represents the change in length of a material per unit length per degree change in temperature.
Thermal strain is the fractional change in length of a material due to a change in temperature.
Thermal stress is the force required to keep a material at a constant length when its temperature changes.
Young's modulus is a measure of the stiffness of a solid material, defined as the ratio of stress to strain within the proportional limit of the material.
The area expansion coefficient is twice the linear expansion coefficient and represents the change in area of a material per unit area per degree change in temperature.
Specific heat capacity is the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of a unit mass of a substance by one degree Celsius.
The specific heat capacity depends on the nature of the substance and its temperature.
The quantity of heat required to raise the temperature of a substance by a given amount.
The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of one mole of a substance by one degree Kelvin.
The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of one mole of a gas by one degree Kelvin, considering constant pressure or volume.

concepts.concept_title: Molar Specific Heat Capacity at Constant Pressure
concepts.description: The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of one mole of a gas by one degree Kelvin, while keeping the pressure constant.

concepts.concept_title: Molar Specific Heat Capacity at Constant Volume
concepts.description: The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of one mole of a gas by one degree Kelvin, while keeping the volume constant.
The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of a substance by one degree Kelvin, considering its mass and nature.

concepts.concept_title: SI Unit of Molar Specific Heat Capacity
concepts.description: The SI unit of molar specific heat capacity is J mol–1 K–1.
The heat transfer by a gas can be achieved by keeping either pressure or volume constant.
The change in temperature of a substance is characterized by a quantity called the heat capacity of that substance.
The heat capacity of a substance is given by the formula: ∆Q = S × ∆T.
Calorimetry is the measurement of heat. It can be defined as the quantity of heat transferred between a system and its surroundings due to a temperature difference.
Specific heat capacity is the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of a unit mass of substance by one degree Celsius. It is a measure of the ability of a substance to absorb heat.
Heat capacity of a system is the amount of heat required to change the temperature of the system by one degree Celsius. It depends on the mass and specific heat capacity of the system.

The principle of calorimetry states that heat lost by a system is equal to the heat gained by its surroundings at a given temperature. It is used to determine the specific heat capacity of a substance.

In an isolated system, heat transfer occurs between parts at different temperatures until equilibrium is achieved. The heat lost by a part at higher temperature equals the heat gained by the part at lower temperature.
A calorimeter is a device used to measure heat. It consists of a metallic vessel and a stirrer of the same material, and is kept inside a wooden jacket with heat insulating material.
Molar specific heat capacities are the amounts of heat energy required to raise the temperature of one mole of a substance by one degree Celsius.
A transition from one state to another, occurring when heat exchange takes place between the substance and its surroundings.
The temperature at which the solid and liquid states of a substance coexist in thermal equilibrium.
The melting point of a substance at standard atmospheric pressure.
The boundary region between the solid and liquid states of a substance during a change of state.
The phenomenon of ice melting just below a wire and freezing above it when the wire is slowly passed through a slab of ice.

concepts.concept_title: Thermal Equilibrium During Change of State
concepts.description: The condition where the solid and liquid states of a substance coexist at a constant temperature during a change of state.
A change of state of matter occurs when a substance changes from one state to another, such as liquid to gas or solid to liquid.
Vaporization is the change of state from liquid to gas, characterized by the constant temperature of the mixture of liquid and gas.
The boiling point is the temperature at which the liquid and gas phases of a substance coexist in thermal equilibrium.
The triple point is the temperature and pressure at which the solid, liquid, and gas phases of a substance coexist.
A phase diagram is a graph showing the relationship between temperature and pressure of a substance, illustrating different phases and states of matter.
Sublimation, fusion, and vaporization curves in a phase diagram represent states where the solid, liquid, and gas phases coexist.
The boiling point of a substance at standard atmospheric pressure is called its normal boiling point.
The change from solid state to vapour state without passing through the liquid state is called sublimation, and the substance is said to sublime.
The amount of heat per unit mass transferred during change of state of a substance is called latent heat of the substance for the process.

concepts.concept_title: Latent Heat of Fusion and Latent Heat of Vaporization
concepts.description: The heat required during melting and vaporization of substances depends upon their heat of transformation.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Pressure on Boiling Point
concepts.description: Boiling point of a substance increases with increase in pressure.
A characteristic property of a substance that represents the amount of energy required to change its state from solid to liquid or liquid to gas.

concepts.concept_title: Latent Heat of Fusion and Vaporization
concepts.description: The energy required to change a substance from solid to liquid (fusion) or liquid to gas (vaporization) is known as latent heat. The units are J kg–1.
The amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of a substance by 1°C is known as specific heat capacity.
There are three distinct modes of heat transfer: conduction, convection, and radiation.
Heat lost by one substance equals heat gained by another substance in a thermodynamic process.
A technique used to measure the heat transfer between two substances, specifically the heat gained by one substance is equal to the heat lost by the other.
Heat transfer between two adjacent parts of a body due to their temperature difference.

concepts.concept_title: Conduction in Gases, Liquids, and Solids
concepts.description: Gases are poor thermal conductors, while liquids have conductivities intermediate between solids and gases.
Heat conduction can be described quantitatively as the time rate of heat flow in a material for a given temperature difference.
Temperature of the bar decreases uniformly with distance from T to T in a steady state.
Importance of heat transfer systems in various applications, including cooling of rooms and prevention of overheating in reactors.
Thermal conductivities of various materials, including metals, silver, and copper.
The thermal conductivity of a material is a measure of its ability to conduct heat. It is the constant of proportionality in the heat transfer equation.
The SI unit of thermal conductivity is J s–1 m–1 K–1 or W m–1 K–1.

concepts.concept_title: Properties of Metals as Thermal Conductors
concepts.description: Metals are good thermal conductors, with high thermal conductivity values. Examples include metals like copper and steel.

concepts.concept_title: Thermal Conductivities of Good Thermal Insulators
concepts.description: Some materials, like wood and glass wool, have low thermal conductivity values and are good thermal insulators.
In the steady state, heat flowing into a rod equals the heat flowing out, allowing the temperature of the junction to be calculated.
The equation for heat transfer is H = KA(T1 - T2)/L.
The heat current through a rod is proportional to the temperature gradient and inversely proportional to the length of the rod.

concepts.concept_title: Equivalent Thermal Conductivity of a Compound Bar
concepts.description: The equivalent thermal conductivity of a compound bar is given by the harmonic mean of the thermal conductivities of the individual components.
Natural convection is a mode of heat transfer by actual motion of matter, where gravity plays an important part in driving the motion.
A thermal convection cycle involves the rising of warm air and the descending of cooler air, transferring heat away from the land or a warm surface.

Trade winds are formed due to the difference in solar heat received by the equatorial and polar regions of the earth. The equatorial region receives more solar heat, causing air to rise and move towards the poles, resulting in trade winds.
Forced convection is a mode of heat transfer where the material is forced to move by a pump or other physical means, resulting in a faster rate of heat transfer.
Natural convection is a mode of heat transfer where the material moves due to differences in density, resulting in a slower rate of heat transfer.
Radiation is a mode of heat transfer that does not require a medium, allowing heat to be transferred across a distance without any obstruction.
Blackbody radiation is the thermal radiation emitted by an object in thermal equilibrium, which has a continuous spectrum of wavelengths.
Radiant energy is the energy transferred by electromagnetic waves, which can travel through a vacuum without any medium.
The speed of light in a vacuum is a constant, approximately 3 × 10^8 m s^-1.
Radiation energy varies with wavelength, with different wavelengths having different energy content.
The relation between a body's temperature and the wavelength of its thermal radiation is given by Wien’s Displacement Law.

concepts.concept_title: absorption and emission of radiant energy
concepts.description: The amount of heat a body can absorb or emit through radiation depends on its surface color and temperature.
Wien’s constant is approximately 2.9 × 10^-3 m K.
The emissivity of a body affects its thermal radiation, with a higher emissivity leading to more efficient heat transfer.
The modified Stefan-Boltzmann’s Law accounts for a body's emissivity when calculating its radiated heat.
The total energy radiated by a body at an absolute temperature T is proportional to its size, emissivity, and most importantly to its temperature.
Blackbody radiation is the universal property of all matter and is independent of size, shape, or material.
The rate of heat loss from a body is dependent on its temperature, surface area, and ability to radiate.
Newton's Law of Cooling states that the rate of cooling of a body is directly proportional to the temperature difference between the body and its surroundings.
Emissivity is a dimensionless fraction that represents the ability of a body to radiate energy.
The Stefan-Boltzmann constant is a fundamental constant in physics that represents the energy radiated per unit area of a blackbody at a given temperature.
Newton's law of cooling states that the rate of loss of heat of a body is directly proportional to the difference of temperature of the body and the surroundings.
Heat transfer occurs through conduction, convection, and radiation mechanisms, each with a different rate of heat transfer.
The rate of loss of heat of a body is directly proportional to the difference in temperature of the body and the surroundings.
Newton's law of cooling can be verified using an experimental set-up with a double-walled vessel and a copper calorimeter.

The heat transfer equation is given by H = σA (T4 – T4), where H is the heat transfer, σ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant, A is the area, T is the temperature of the body, and T is the temperature of the surroundings.
The law of cooling equation is given by ∆T = T – T, where ∆T is the temperature difference, T is the initial temperature, T is the final temperature, and t is the time.
The cooling of a body depends on the temperature difference between the body and the surroundings.
An experimental set-up for cooling involves a double-walled vessel, a copper calorimeter, and two thermometers for noting temperatures.
Heat is a form of energy that flows between a body and its surroundings due to a temperature difference.
Thermometers use a measurable property that changes with temperature to measure temperature, and different thermometers lead to different temperature scales.
The Celsius and Fahrenheit temperature scales are related by the equation t = (9/5)t + 32.
The ideal gas equation connects pressure (P), volume (V), and absolute temperature (T) as PV = µRT.
The Kelvin absolute temperature scale has the same unit size as the Celsius scale but differs in origin, T = T - 273.15.
The coefficient of linear expansion (α) and volume expansion (α) are defined by the relations ∆l = α∆T and ∆V = α∆T.

concepts.concept_title: Relation between Linear and Volume Expansion
concepts.description: The relation between linear and volume expansion coefficients is α = 3α.
The specific heat capacity of a substance is defined by s = ∆Q / (m∆T), where m is the mass of the substance and ∆Q is the heat required to change its temperature by ∆T.

concepts.concept_title: Definition of Molar Specific Heat Capacity
concepts.description: The molar specific heat capacity of a substance is defined as the amount of heat required to raise the temperature of a mole of the substance by one degree.

concepts.concept_title: Latent Heat of Fusion and Vaporization
concepts.description: The latent heat of fusion and vaporization are defined as the heat required to change a substance from solid to liquid and liquid to gas, respectively, at the same temperature and pressure.
Heat can be transferred through conduction, convection, and radiation.
Heat is transferred between neighbouring parts of a body through molecular collisions in conduction.

concepts.concept_title: Equation for Rate of Heat Flow
concepts.description: The rate of heat flow (H) is given by H = (T - T') / L, where K is the thermal conductivity, A is the cross-sectional area, C is the specific heat capacity, and L is the length of the bar.
Newton's Law of Cooling states that the rate of cooling of a body is proportional to the excess temperature of the body over the surroundings.
The Celsius and Fahrenheit scales can be defined in terms of the Kelvin temperature scale.
A system in equilibrium has the same pressure and temperature throughout, but different phases can have different molar volumes.
Heat transfer always involves a temperature difference between two systems or two parts of the same system.
Convection involves the flow of matter within a fluid due to unequal temperatures of its parts.
The triple-point of water is a standard fixed point in modern thermometry due to its reproducibility.
The original Celsius scale had two fixed points, melting point of ice and boiling point of water, which were not reproducible and hence not suitable for thermometry.
The Kelvin absolute scale has a fixed point at the triple-point of water, which is 273.16 K.
The temperature in the Kelvin scale is related to temperature in Celsius scale by the equation t = T - 273.15, where T is temperature in K.
A temperature scale with a unit interval size equal to that of the Fahrenheit scale is described.
Ideal gas thermometers can be used for thermometry, but their readings may have slight discrepancies due to differences in properties of gases.

The linear expansion of materials can be described using the equation ΔL = αL₀ΔT, where ΔL is change in length, α is coefficient of linear expansion, L₀ is initial length, and ΔT is change in temperature.
The linear expansion of materials depends on temperature, and coefficients such as α = 1.20 × 10^(-5) K^(-1) are used to describe these relationships.
Materials can develop thermal stresses and undergo deformation when heated or cooled.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Linear Expansion on Physical Quantities
concepts.description: The linear expansion of materials can affect physical quantities such as force, pressure, and tension.
The change in length or volume of a solid due to a change in temperature is known as thermal expansion.

concepts.concept_title: Change in Length of Composite Rod
concepts.description: When two rods of different materials are joined together, the change in length of the combined rod is the sum of the changes in length of the individual rods.
The fractional change in density of a substance is calculated using the co-efficient of volume expansion.

concepts.concept_title: Rise in Temperature of Aluminium Block
concepts.description: The rise in temperature of an aluminum block due to a drilling machine can be calculated using the specific heat of aluminum and the power input.

concepts.concept_title: Maximum Amount of Ice that can Melt
concepts.description: The maximum amount of ice that can melt when a copper block is heated and placed on an ice block is calculated using the heat of fusion of water and the specific heat of copper.
The specific heat of a metal can be calculated by dropping a heated metal block into a copper calorimeter containing water and measuring the final temperature.
The molar specific heat of gases can be explained by the difference in the number of degrees of freedom of the molecules of the gas.
The rate of extra evaporation caused by a medicine that lowers fever can be calculated by assuming the evaporation mechanism to be the only way by which heat is lost.
The thermal conductivity of an insulating material such as thermacole can be calculated by measuring the temperature difference across the material and the thickness of the material.
Estimation of ice remaining after heating at a constant temperature.
Understanding why an optical pyrometer might give incorrect readings for certain temperatures.

concepts.concept_title: Heat Transfer through a Metal Wall
concepts.description: Estimation of temperature of a part of a flame in contact with a metal boiler.

concepts.concept_title: Temperature Dependence of Emission and Absorption
concepts.description: Understanding how the reflectivity of a surface affects its emissivity.

concepts.concept_title: Heat Loss due to Convective Cooling
concepts.description: Estimation of cooling time for a body in a given environment.
Understanding why steam-based heating systems are more efficient.
Why the earth would be inhospitably cold without its atmosphere.
The idea that heat is a fine invisible fluid filling in the pores of a substance.

concepts.concept_title: Conversion of Energy from One Form to Another
concepts.description: The experiment of Benjamin Thomson (1798) showed that heat is a form of energy and can be converted from work.
A state of a system where the macroscopic variables characterise the system do not change in time.

concepts.concept_title: Heat as a Form of Energy
concepts.description: Heat is a form of energy that can be converted from one form to another.

concepts.concept_title: The Importance of Defining Heat, Temperature, and Work
concepts.description: The need to define heat, temperature, and work more carefully in physics.

concepts.concept_title: The Concept of Specific Heat Capacity
concepts.description: The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of a substance by a given amount.

concepts.concept_title: Thermodynamic State Variables and Equation of State
concepts.description: Variables that characterise the state of a thermodynamic system and an equation that relates them.
The principle that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another.
The definitions and relationships between heat, internal energy, and work in a thermodynamic system.
The principle that the total entropy of an isolated system always increases over time.
The distinction between processes that can be reversed and those that cannot.
A theoretical engine that demonstrates the efficiency of a heat engine.
Equilibrium in mechanics is when external forces and torques are zero, while in thermodynamics it means the macroscopic state of a system remains constant.
Macroscopic variables like pressure, volume, temperature, mass, and composition are used to describe the state of a system in thermodynamics.
Mechanics deals with the motion of particles or bodies under forces, while thermodynamics focuses on the internal state of a system and macroscopic variables.
The internal state of a body is related to the energy of its internal motion, not its macroscopic motion.
The state of a system depends on its surroundings, particularly the nature of the wall separating it from the surroundings.
Temperature is a measure of thermal energy, while heat is a measure of energy transfer.
Entropy is a measure of the disorderness or randomness of a system.
Enthalpy is a measure of the total heat content of a system.
An adiabatic wall is an insulating wall that does not allow flow of energy (heat) from one system to another.

Thermal equilibrium is a state where two systems have equal temperature, and the zeroth law states that if two systems are in thermal equilibrium with a third system, they are also in thermal equilibrium with each other.
The zeroth law states that two systems in thermal equilibrium with a third system separately are in thermal equilibrium with each other.

concepts.concept_title: Temperature as a Marker of Hotness
concepts.description: Temperature is a marker of the 'hotness' of a body and determines the direction of flow of heat when two bodies are in thermal contact.
Internal energy is the sum of the kinetic and potential energies of the molecules of a system.
Heat flows from the body at a higher temperature to the one at lower temperature.
Temperature scales can be constructed to assign temperatures to different bodies.

concepts.concept_title: Internal Energy as a State Variable
concepts.description: Internal energy depends only on the state of the system, not on how it was achieved.

concepts.concept_title: Heat as Energy Transfer due to Temperature Difference
concepts.description: Heat is energy transfer from a system at higher temperature to surroundings or vice versa.

concepts.concept_title: Work as Energy Transfer by Means not Involving Temperature Difference
concepts.description: Work is energy transfer by means such as moving the piston that do not involve temperature difference.

concepts.concept_title: First Law of Thermodynamics (ΔQ = ΔU + ΔW)
concepts.description: The total energy supplied to a system is equal to the change in internal energy plus the work done by the system.

concepts.concept_title: Change in Internal Energy of a System
concepts.description: The change in internal energy of a system can be due to heat transfer or work done on the system.

concepts.concept_title: Internal Energy of a Gas as Sum of Kinetic Energies
concepts.description: The internal energy of a gas is simply the sum of kinetic energies of its molecules.
Internal energy is the energy of a thermodynamic system that defines its state.
Heat is a mode of energy transfer, while internal energy is a state variable.
The law states that change in internal energy is equal to the heat added minus the work done by the system.
Work done depends on the path taken by the system to change its state.
Heat capacity is the amount of heat required to change the temperature of a substance by a given amount.
Molar specific heat capacity is the heat capacity per mole of a substance.

concepts.concept_title: Isothermal Expansion of an Ideal Gas
concepts.description: Isothermal expansion is a process in which an ideal gas expands at constant temperature.

The change in internal energy (ΔU) is related to the amount of heat supplied (∆Q) and the change in volume (∆V) of the gas. The heat capacity of a substance is defined as the amount of heat required to raise its temperature by a given amount.
The latent heat of vaporization is the energy required to change the state of a substance from its liquid phase to its vapor phase. For 1 g of water, the latent heat of vaporization is 2256 J.
When a substance undergoes a change in state, the heat supplied is related to the change in volume of the substance. For a solid, the change in volume is negligible.

concepts.concept_title: Heat Capacity and Specific Heat Capacity
concepts.description: The heat capacity of a substance is the amount of heat required to raise its temperature by a given amount. The specific heat capacity is the heat capacity per unit mass of the substance.

concepts.concept_title: Equation of State for an Ideal Gas
concepts.description: The change in internal energy of an ideal gas is given by △U = △Q - P△V.
The molar heat capacity of a substance is the heat capacity per molecule of the substance.
The equipartition of energy states that each degree of freedom contributes 1/2 kT to the average energy of a molecule.

concepts.concept_title: Predicting Molar Specific Heat Capacities of Solids
concepts.description: The molar specific heat capacities of solids can be predicted using the law of equipartition of energy.

concepts.concept_title: Specific Heat Capacity as a Function of Temperature
concepts.description: The specific heat capacity of a substance is a function of temperature.
The experimentally measured specific heat capacities of solids generally agree with predicted values.
Molar specific heat capacity is the heat required to raise the temperature of one mole of an ideal gas at constant pressure or volume.

concepts.concept_title: Relation Between Molar Specific Heat Capacity and Universal Gas Constant
concepts.description: The relation between molar specific heat capacity, universal gas constant, and temperature is given by Eq. (11.8).
The first law of thermodynamics states that the change in internal energy of a system is equal to the heat absorbed by the system minus the work done by the system, given by ∆Q = ∆U + P∆V.
The specific heat capacity of water varies slightly with temperature, and its value is approximately 4186 J kg–1 K–1.
The mechanical equivalent of heat is the work needed to produce one calorie of heat, which is now a conversion factor between two different units of energy.
Thermodynamic state variables are macroscopic variables that completely describe an equilibrium state of a thermodynamic system.

concepts.concept_title: Specific Heat Capacity at Constant Volume and Constant Pressure
concepts.description: The specific heat capacity at constant volume and constant pressure are given by Eqs. (11.9) and (11.10).
Extensive variables change with the size of the system, while intensive variables remain the same regardless of the system's size.

concepts.concept_title: Examples of extensive and intensive variables
concepts.description: Examples of extensive variables include internal energy, volume and mass, while examples of intensive variables include pressure, temperature, and density.
Thermodynamic variables can be classified as either extensive or intensive based on their behavior in a system.
An equation of state is a mathematical relationship between two or more state variables.
The ideal gas relation is a specific equation of state for an ideal gas, described by the equation PV = µRT.
The relationship between state variables in a thermodynamic system is described by the equation of state.
Equilibrium states are characterized by well-defined temperature and pressure.
Thermodynamic processes are the paths followed by a system as it evolves from an initial to a final state.
A quasi-static process is a process that takes place slowly and smoothly, allowing the system to remain in equilibrium at all stages.
Non-equilibrium states are characterized by rapid exchange of heat and lack of well-defined temperature and pressure.
Thermodynamic state variables are of two kinds: extensive and intensive.

concepts.concept_title: Examples of Extensive and Intensive Variables
concepts.description: Examples of extensive variables include mass, volume, and total heat (Q) transferred, while examples of intensive variables include pressure and temperature.
A quasi-static process is one in which the system changes its variables (P, T, V) slowly, remaining in thermal and mechanical equilibrium with its surroundings throughout.
An isothermal process is one in which the temperature of the system is kept fixed throughout.
Boyle's Law states that the pressure of a given mass of gas varies inversely as its volume during an isothermal process.
An isotherm is a pressure-volume curve of a gas undergoing an isothermal process.
An adiabatic process is one in which no heat flows between the system and its surroundings.

A hypothetical construct where the external pressure and temperature of the system differ only infinitesimally from the system pressure and temperature at any intermediate stage. Work done is given by ∆W = P ∆V.

A reasonable approximation to an ideal quasi-static process when processes are sufficiently slow and do not involve accelerated motion of the piston, large temperature gradient, etc. Work done is given by W = ∫ P dV.
Heat supplied to the gas equals the work done by the gas: Q = W. This implies that there is no change in internal energy of an ideal gas in an isothermal process.

concepts.concept_title: Isothermal Process of an Ideal Gas
concepts.description: The work done in an isothermal process of an ideal gas is given by W = ∫ P dV. This implies that there is no change in internal energy of an ideal gas in an isothermal process.

concepts.concept_title: Adiabatic Process of an Ideal Gas
concepts.description: The work done by an ideal gas in an adiabatic process results in a decrease in its internal energy (and hence its temperature): T < T . For an ideal gas, P V γ = const.
In an isochoric process, V is constant. No work is done on or by the gas. The heat absorbed by the gas goes entirely to change its internal energy and temperature.
Ratio of specific heats (ordinary or molar) at constant pressure and at constant volume: γ = Cp/Cv.
Work done by a gas in an isobaric process is given by W = P(V - V) = moR(T - T).
Heat absorbed by a gas in an isobaric process goes partly to increase internal energy and partly to do work.
In a cyclic process, the system returns to its initial state with zero change in internal energy.
Reversible processes are ideal and irreversible processes are observed in nature.
The Second Law disallows many phenomena that are consistent with the First Law of Thermodynamics.

concepts.concept_title: First Law of Thermodynamics Principle of Conservation of Energy
concepts.description: The First Law states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted.
A fundamental law that states no process can convert heat entirely into work, and heat cannot spontaneously transfer from a colder object to a hotter object.
A measure of the maximum amount of work that can be done by a heat engine, given by 1 - (T2/T1) where T1 and T2 are the initial and final temperatures.

concepts.concept_title: Co-Efficient of Performance of a Refrigerator
concepts.description: A measure of the ratio of heat absorbed from a reservoir to the work done on the refrigerator, given by Q_cold / W, where Q_cold is the heat absorbed and W is the work done.
A process is reversible if it can be turned back such that both the system and the surroundings return to their original states, with no other change anywhere else in the universe.
No process is possible whose sole result is the absorption of heat from a reservoir and the complete conversion of the heat into work.
No process is possible whose sole result is the transfer of heat from a colder object to a hotter object.
A fundamental aspect of thermodynamic processes, where most processes cannot be reversed due to the presence of dissipative effects such as heat transfer and friction.
A thermodynamic process that occurs at a constant temperature, where heat is absorbed or released from the system to maintain thermal equilibrium with the surroundings.
A process that is quasi-static and non-dissipative is reversible.
The efficiency of a heat engine is the ratio of work done to heat absorbed.
The limiting efficiency of a heat engine is the maximum efficiency possible, which cannot be achieved in practice.
A reversible engine operating between two temperatures, which has the maximum possible efficiency.
Processes that bring about temperature change in the system without heat flow from the reservoir, used for reversible engines.
For a process to be quasi-static, there should be no finite temperature difference between the system and the reservoir.
The Carnot engine uses an ideal gas as its working substance.
Ideal gas expands isothermally from (P, V, T) to (P, V', T).
Ideal gas expands adiabatically from (P, V, T) to (P, V', T).
An adiabatic process is characterized by the relation pV^γ = constant.
The work done by an ideal gas in isothermal expansion is given by W = Q.
The work done by an ideal gas in adiabatic expansion is given by W = (Cv - Cv') T.
The efficiency of a Carnot engine is given by η = 1 - (T2 / T1).
A reversible engine is an engine that can be reversed without any loss of energy.
No engine can have efficiency more than that of the Carnot engine.
A thermodynamic cycle is a series of processes that return to their initial state.

concepts.concept_title: Kelvin-Planck Statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics
concepts.description: The Kelvin-Planck statement of the second law of thermodynamics asserts that no heat engine can have a higher efficiency than a Carnot engine.

An irreversible engine can be coupled with a reversible refrigerator to show the second law of thermodynamics, but an irreversible refrigerator cannot be used as a more efficient engine than a Carnot engine.
Equation (11.28) provides a universal thermodynamic temperature scale that is independent of any particular properties of the system used in the Carnot cycle.
The zeroth law of thermodynamics states that two systems in thermal equilibrium with a third system separately are in thermal equilibrium with each other, leading to the concept of temperature.
Internal energy is the sum of kinetic energies and potential energies of the molecular constituents of the system, not including the over-all kinetic energy of the system.

Heat and work are two modes of energy transfer, with heat being the energy transfer due to temperature difference between the system and the surroundings, and work being the energy transfer by other means.
The first law is the general law of conservation of energy for a system with energy transfer from or to surroundings through heat and work.
Specific heat capacity is the amount of heat required to change a substance's temperature by a given amount.
Molar specific heat capacity is the amount of heat required to change the temperature of one mole of a substance by a given amount.
Ideal gas specific heat capacities at constant pressure and volume have a specific relation.
Equation of state is a relation connecting different state variables of a thermodynamic system.
Quasi-static process is a process that occurs infinitely slowly, allowing the system to remain in thermal and mechanical equilibrium with the surroundings.

concepts.concept_title: Isothermal Expansion of an Ideal Gas
concepts.description: Isothermal expansion of an ideal gas is a process where the temperature remains constant while the volume changes.

concepts.concept_title: Adiabatic Process of an Ideal Gas
concepts.description: Adiabatic process of an ideal gas is a process where there is no heat transfer between the system and the surroundings.
Efficiency of a heat engine is limited by the second law of thermodynamics.
Clausius statement is a formulation of the second law of thermodynamics.
Reversible process is a process that can be reversed without any change in the system or the surroundings.

concepts.concept_title: Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics and Equilibrium
concepts.description: Equilibrium in thermodynamics refers to a situation when macroscopic variables describing the thermodynamic state of a system do not depend on time, and the state of a system is defined only during steady-state, equilibrium processes.
Spontaneous processes of nature are irreversible while quasi-static processes are considered reversible with no dissipative factors.
The Carnot engine is a reversible engine operating between two temperatures with an efficiency given by η = 1 - T2 / T1, where T1 is the source temperature and T2 is the sink temperature.
Heat transferred to or from the system is denoted by Q > 0 or Q < 0 respectively and work done by or on the system is represented by W > 0 or W < 0.
Thermal conductivity is the ability of a material to conduct heat while heat capacity is the energy required to raise the temperature of a substance by a unit degree.
Temperature of a body is related to its average internal energy, not the kinetic energy of motion of its centre of mass.

Thermodynamic equilibrium refers to when macroscopic variables describing the thermodynamic state of a system do not depend on time, whereas mechanical equilibrium refers to net external force and torque on the system being zero.
Heat capacity of a system generally depends on the process through which heat is supplied.
In isothermal quasi-static processes, heat is absorbed or given out by the system even though at every stage the gas has the same temperature as the surrounding reservoir.
The Carnot cycle consists of two isothermal processes connected by two adiabatic processes and the efficiency of a Carnot engine is given by η = 1 - T2 / T1.
Two bodies at different temperatures T and T2 do not necessarily settle to the mean temperature (T + T2) / 2 when brought in thermal contact.
A coolant in chemical or nuclear plants should have high specific heat capacity.

concepts.concept_title: Temperature and Pressure in Gas Expansion
concepts.description: The temperature of a gas and the pressure of the gas in a tyre increase during driving in a car.
Standard temperature and pressure (STP) is defined as 0°C and 1 atm
In an adiabatic process, no heat is transferred between the system and its surroundings
The work done in an adiabatic process is equal to the change in internal energy
In a diabatic process, heat is transferred between the system and its surroundings
The ideal gas law states that PV = nRT, where P is pressure, V is volume, n is number of moles, R is gas constant, and T is temperature
The compressibility factor is a measure of the deviation of a real gas from ideal gas behavior
No work is done in an isochoric process, as there is no change in volume
In an isobaric process, pressure remains constant, and heat may be transferred
The first law of thermodynamics states that the change in internal energy (ΔU) is equal to the heat added (Q) minus the work done (W)
The efficiency of a system is defined as the ratio of the work done to the heat added
Atoms are considered as little particles that move in perpetual motion, attracting each other when apart, but repelling when close.
Kinetic theory explains the behaviour of gases based on the idea that gas consists of rapidly moving atoms or molecules.
Inter-atomic forces are short-range forces that are important for solids and liquids, but can be neglected for gases.

concepts.concept_title: Equation of State of Ideal Gas
concepts.description: Kinetic theory of ideal gas explains the behaviour of gases based on the equation of state.

concepts.concept_title: Molecular Parameters and Measurable Properties of Gases
concepts.description: Kinetic theory relates measurable properties of gases to molecular parameters, yielding estimates of molecular sizes and masses.
The law of equipartition of energy states that energy of a degree of freedom is equally distributed among the atoms or molecules of a gas.
Mean free path is the average distance travelled by a molecule between successive collisions with gas molecules.
The concept that matter is composed of indivisible particles called atoms, which are the building blocks of matter.
Democritus' idea of atoms as indivisible, differing in shape, size, and properties, resulting in different substances upon combination.
Dalton's theory stating that elements are composed of atoms, different elements have different atoms, and chemical reactions involve the combination or separation of atoms.
The characteristic properties of atoms, such as shape, size, and mass, which determine the properties of substances formed by their combination.
The estimated size of atoms, ranging from 10^-10 m, as mentioned in the Lalitavistara, and its relevance in understanding atomic properties.
The composition and size of molecules, made up of one or more atoms, which constitute matter, and the separation between atoms in solids, liquids, and gases.
The arrangement and spacing of atoms in solids, liquids, and gases, influencing their properties and behavior.
When gases combine chemically to yield another gas, their volumes are in the ratios of small integers.
Equal volumes of all gases at equal temperature and pressure have the same number of molecules.
The mean free path is the average distance a molecule in a gas can travel without colliding.
The interatomic forces in solids and liquids have long range attraction and short range repulsion.
The Avogadro number is 6.02 x 10^23, denoted by N_A.
The mass of 22.4 liters of any gas is equal to its molecular weight in grams at S.T.P.
Kinetic theory justifies Avogadro's hypothesis, explaining the behavior of gases as a collection of moving molecules.
The perfect gas equation can be written as PV = nRT, where n is the number of moles and R is the universal gas constant.
The mass of the gas containing N_A molecules is M_N = N_A m, where M_N is the molar mass and m is the mass of a molecule.
The pressure of a gas is directly proportional to the number of molecules and the temperature, while being inversely proportional to the volume.
The Boltzmann constant (k) relates the pressure of a gas to the number of molecules and the temperature.

concepts.concept_title: Low pressure and high temperature ideal gas
concepts.description: At low pressures and high temperatures, the gas molecules are far apart, and their interactions are negligible, allowing the gas to behave almost like an ideal one.
The number of molecules per unit volume is the same for all gases at a fixed temperature and pressure.
The total pressure of a mixture of ideal gases is the sum of the partial pressures of the individual gases.
For a given mass of gas, the pressure is inversely proportional to the volume at constant temperature.
The ideal gas equation is given by PV = NkT, where P is the pressure, V is the volume, N is the number of molecules, k is the Boltzmann constant, and T is the temperature.

concepts.concept_title: Boyle's Law and Proportional Relationship of Volume with Pressure
concepts.description: Boyle's law states that P is inversely proportional to V at constant temperature. The volume of gas is also proportional to its absolute temperature T at a fixed pressure.

concepts.concept_title: Charles' Law and Inverse Proportionality of Volume with Pressure
concepts.description: Charles' law states that at a fixed pressure, the volume of a gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature T.

concepts.concept_title: Molecular Volume and Density of Water Vapour
concepts.description: The volume occupied by a single water molecule is estimated to be 1000/0.6 times larger than the volume occupied by vapour under given conditions.

concepts.concept_title: Fractional Volume of Molecular State and Bulk State
concepts.description: The fractional volume of molecular state in liquid state is 1, while the fractional volume of vapour state is 6×10^-4 compared to the bulk state.

concepts.concept_title: Partial Pressure in a Mixture of Non-Reacting Gases
concepts.description: Each gas in a mixture of non-reacting gases obeys the gas law. The total pressure of the mixture is the sum of partial pressures due to each constituent gas.

concepts.concept_title: Number of Mole and Number of Molecules in a Gas
concepts.description: The ratio of the number of molecules of two gases in a mixture is equal to the ratio of their number of moles divided by Avogadro's number.
Avogadro's number is approximately 6 × 10^23 molecules and relates the mass of a substance to its molecular mass.
The molecular mass and volume of a substance relate to its mass and molar volume through Avogadro's number.
The radius of a water molecule can be calculated from its volume using the formula (4/3) π (Radius)^3.
The average distance between atoms in a substance can be calculated from its interatomic distance using the volume of the substance.

concepts.concept_title: Kinetic Theory of an Ideal Gas
concepts.description: The kinetic theory of an ideal gas assumes molecules in incessant random motion, colliding with each other and the walls of a container.
key_terms: Kinetic theory of an ideal gas

concepts.description: The pressure of a gas can be derived from the force exerted on the walls of a container due to molecular collisions.
The rate of momentum is the measure of the rate at which the momentum of a system changes over time.
When a molecule collides with a wall, it exerts a force on the wall, resulting in a transfer of momentum.

concepts.concept_title: Elastic Collision and Conservation of Momentum
concepts.description: In an elastic collision, the total momentum of the system remains conserved.

concepts.concept_title: Average Speed of Molecules in a Gas
concepts.description: The average speed of molecules in a gas is proportional to the square root of the temperature.

concepts.concept_title: Distribution of Velocities in a Gas
concepts.description: The velocities of molecules in a gas follow a distribution, with a mean of 0 and a root mean square speed proportional to the square root of the temperature.

concepts.concept_title: Pressure as Force per Unit Area
concepts.description: Pressure is defined as the force exerted per unit area on the surface of an object.
The pressure in an ideal gas is given by the average kinetic energy of the molecules per unit area.

concepts.concept_title: Number Density of Molecules in a Gas
concepts.description: The number density of molecules in a gas is defined as the number of molecules per unit volume.
Pressure in a fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions and is equal in all parts of the fluid at rest.
Boltzmann's constant (k) relates the average kinetic energy of a molecule to the absolute temperature of the gas.
The internal energy (E) of an ideal gas is purely kinetic and is given by E = (3/2) k NT.

concepts.concept_title: Equation of State for Ideal Gas
concepts.description: The equation of state for an ideal gas is given by PV = (2/3) N x (1/2) m v^2.
The partial pressure of a gas is given by P = (2/3) (NT) k T.
The mean square speed of molecules is given by v^2 = (3kT)/m.
The root mean square (RMS) speed of molecules is given by v = (v^2)^(1/2).

concepts.concept_title: Relationship between Temperature and RMS Speed
concepts.description: The RMS speed of molecules is directly proportional to the square root of temperature.
The average kinetic energy of a molecule is given by (1/2) m v^2 = (3/2) k T.
The kinetic energy per molecule of an ideal gas is equal to (3/2)kT, where k is Boltzmann's constant and T is the temperature in Kelvin.
The average kinetic energy of the molecules in an ideal gas is a function of the temperature of the gas and is independent of the nature of the gas.
The RMS speed of the molecules in an ideal gas is directly proportional to the square root of the temperature of the gas.
In elastic collisions, the total kinetic energy is conserved, and the velocity of the colliding particles is not affected.
Gases can be compressed by reducing the volume of the container while maintaining a constant temperature, resulting in higher pressure.
When a compressed gas expands, the volume increases, and the pressure decreases.
Isotopes are atoms of the same element with different masses, and their separation can be achieved using techniques such as centrifugation or filtration.
The rate of diffusion of gases is inversely proportional to the square root of their masses.

concepts.concept_title: Rebound speed of a ball after collision
concepts.description: The rebound speed of a ball (gas molecule) after collision is V + u, where V is the speed of the bat and u is the initial speed of the ball.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship between temperature and translational energy
concepts.description: The average translational energy of a molecule is directly proportional to the temperature of the gas (e = kT).

concepts.concept_title: Rotational degrees of freedom and energy
concepts.description: A diatomic molecule has two rotational degrees of freedom, each contributing a term to the total rotational energy.
The translational energy of a single molecule is e = mv^2, where m is the mass and v is the velocity of the molecule.

concepts.concept_title: Kinetic energy equation for a molecule
concepts.description: The kinetic energy of a single molecule is e + e = mv^2 + Iω^2, where I is the moment of inertia and ω is the angular speed.
For a gas in thermal equilibrium at temperature T, the average value of rotational energy is zero.

concepts.concept_title: Rotational energy equation for a diatomic molecule
concepts.description: The rotational energy of a diatomic molecule is e = kBT, where I is the moment of inertia and ω is the angular speed.

concepts.concept_title: Degrees of freedom for a molecule
concepts.description: A molecule has one degree of freedom for motion in a line, two degrees for motion in a plane, and three degrees for motion in space.
A molecule has translational degrees of freedom, which allow it to move in space. Each translational degree of freedom contributes a term (such as ½ mv^2) to the total energy.
In a molecule, the atoms can oscillate along the interatomic axis, contributing a vibrational energy term (such as ½ ky^2) to the total energy.
The average energy of each mode of motion (translational, rotational, or vibrational) is equal to ½ k T, where k is the Boltzmann constant and T is the temperature.
In thermal equilibrium, the total energy is equally distributed in all possible energy modes, with each mode having an average energy equal to ½ k T.

The molar specific heats of a gas are related to the energy modes present in the gas. For example, for a monatomic gas, the molar specific heat at constant pressure (Cp) is equal to (5/2)R, while for a rigid diatomic gas, Cp = (7/2)R.
The ratio of specific heats (gamma) is given by the equation Cp/Cv = (7/5). This is a fundamental relation between the specific heats of a gas.
In thermal equilibrium, the total internal energy (U) of a gas is equal to the total energy present in the gas, given by the equation U = N * k * T.
The law states that each degree of freedom has an average energy of (1/2)kT. Energy from translational and rotational degrees of freedom contribute to Cv and Cp.
The molar specific heat at constant volume (Cv) and constant pressure (Cp) relate to the internal energy and heat capacity.
A monatomic gas has 3 translational degrees of freedom and contributes (3/2)kT to the average energy of a molecule.
Polyatomic molecules have translational and rotational degrees of freedom, and can have vibrational modes contributing to specific heat capacity.
Using the law of equipartition of energy, predicted values of specific heats of gases can be determined.
The law of equipartition of energy states that the average energy of a degree of freedom is (1/2)kT.
Specific heat capacity of solids is given by C = 3R, where R is the gas constant.
Molecules in a gas undergo collisions due to their finite size, affecting their paths.
Mean free path is the average distance a molecule travels before colliding with another molecule.

concepts.concept_title: Collisions Rate and Mean Free Path
concepts.description: The collision rate of molecules is determined by their average relative velocity.

concepts.concept_title: Time between Collisions and Mean Free Path
concepts.description: The time between collisions of a molecule with another molecule is given by τ = 6.1 × 10–10 s.

concepts.concept_title: Diameter of Molecules and Mean Free Path
concepts.description: The mean free path depends inversely on the number density and diameter of the molecules.
The average distance between two successive collisions of a gas molecule.
The rate at which gas molecules collide with each other.
The average time between two successive collisions of a gas molecule.
The study of the behavior of gases using the principles of kinetic theory.
Physical constants that describe the behavior of gases, such as R and k.
An equation that describes the behavior of an ideal gas, relating pressure, volume, and temperature.
The relationship between temperature and the average kinetic energy of a gas molecule.
The specific heat capacity of a gas per mole, determined using the law of equipartition of energy.
A principle stating that the total energy of a system is distributed equally in different energy modes.
The kinetic energy of a gas molecule due to its translational motion.
Physical properties of gases, such as viscosity and heat conductivity, that can be related to molecular size.
The experimental values of specific heats of several gases can be improved by including vibrational modes of motion.
The mean free path is the average distance covered by a molecule between two successive collisions, given by l = (2n pd^2)^(-1), where n is the number density and d the diameter of the molecule.
Pressure in a fluid is not only exerted on the walls, but also exists everywhere in the fluid, making any layer of the fluid inside the container in equilibrium.

At ordinary pressures and temperatures, the intermolecular distance in a gas is 10 times the interatomic distance in solids and liquids, but the mean free path is 100 times the interatomic distance and 1000 times the size of the molecule.

The law of equipartition of energy states that the energy for each degree of freedom in thermal equilibrium is (1/2) kT, with each quadratic term in the total energy expression of a molecule counted as 2 degrees of freedom.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Gravity on Gas Molecules
concepts.description: Molecules of air in a room do not all fall and settle on the ground due to gravity, but instead have a high speed and incessant collisions, resulting in a slight increase in density at lower heights.
The average of a squared quantity is not necessarily the square of the average, requiring careful consideration of examples and conditions.
The fraction of molecular volume to the actual volume occupied by a gas can be estimated, for example, for oxygen gas at STP.

concepts.concept_title: Molar Volume of an Ideal Gas
concepts.description: The molar volume of an ideal gas at STP is 22.4 liters, which can be derived from the ideal gas equation.
The plot of PV/T versus P for an ideal gas shows a linear relationship, with the y-intercept representing the molar volume of the gas at the given temperature.

concepts.concept_title: Comparing Plot of PV/T for Different Gases
concepts.description: By comparing the plots of PV/T for different gases, such as oxygen and hydrogen, the molar volume of each gas at a given temperature can be determined.
The relationship between pressure, volume, temperature, and number of moles of an ideal gas.
The difference between gauge pressure and absolute pressure, and how to calculate absolute pressure from gauge pressure and atmospheric pressure.

concepts.concept_title: Ideal Gas Behavior and Real Gas Corrections
concepts.description: The behavior of ideal gases and corrections to ideal gas behavior, including the van der Waals equation.
The definition and calculation of molar specific heat capacity, including its relationship to the kinetic theory of gases.
The definition and calculation of the root mean square speed of gas molecules, including its relationship to temperature and molecular mass.

concepts.concept_title: Mean Free Path and Collision Frequency
concepts.description: The definitions and calculations of mean free path and collision frequency of gas molecules, including their relationship to molecular mass and density.

concepts.concept_title: Ideal Gas in a Closed Vessel
concepts.description: The behavior of an ideal gas in a closed vessel, including its pressure-volume relationships and thermodynamic properties.

concepts.concept_title: Molar Thermal Energy of a Gas
concepts.description: The definition and calculation of the molar thermal energy of a gas, including its relationship to temperature and molecular properties.

concepts.concept_title: Thermal Energy of a Single Gas Molecule
concepts.description: The definition and calculation of the thermal energy of a single gas molecule, including its relationship to temperature and molecular properties.
A motion where an object moves to and fro about a mean position.
A motion that repeats after a certain interval of time.
A type of oscillatory motion where the object moves to and fro about its mean position.
A measure of the distance of an object from its mean position.
The maximum displacement of an object from its mean position.
The time taken for one complete oscillation.
The number of oscillations per second.
The position of an object about which it oscillates.
The position of an object about which it has no net external force.
A motion that repeats itself at regular intervals of time is called periodic motion.
All periodic motions are not oscillatory, examples include circular motion.
Oscillations are called vibrations when frequency is high, both are periodic motions.
Simple harmonic motion arises when the force on the oscillating body is directly proportional to its displacement from the mean position.
Oscillating bodies eventually come to rest at their equilibrium positions due to dissipative causes.
External periodic agency can force oscillating bodies to remain oscillating.
The smallest interval of time after which the motion is repeated is called its period.
Frequency is a term used to describe the number of oscillations per second.
The collective oscillations of the constituents of a medium manifest themselves as waves.
The time period T is the reciprocal of the frequency ν, and is measured in seconds. Frequency ν is the number of repetitions per unit time, and its unit is s-1.

concepts.concept_title: Reciprocal relation between Frequency and Period
concepts.description: Frequency ν and period T are reciprocals of each other, with T = 1/ν.
The unit of frequency is hertz (Hz), which is equivalent to s-1.
Frequency and period can be calculated from observations, such as the heart rate of a human or the beating of a pendulum.
Displacement is a change in position with time, and can be used to describe various physical phenomena, such as the motion of a particle or the variation of a physical property.

concepts.concept_title: Displacement as a Mathematical Function of Time
concepts.description: Displacement can be represented by a mathematical function of time, which can be periodic and described by a function with a specific form, such as f(t) = A cos ωt.
A function is considered periodic if it repeats its value after a fixed interval of time, known as the period.
The period of a periodic function is the least interval of time after which the function repeats its value.

concepts.concept_title: Period of sin(), cos(), and sin()^n Functions
concepts.description: The period of sin(ωt) is T = 2π/ω, cos(2ωt) is T/2, and cos(4ωt) is T/4.
A co-function is defined as a function that has the same period as another function but is related to it by a phase change.
Examples of periodic functions include sin(ωt) + cos(ωt), sin(ωt) + cos(2ωt) + sin(4ωt), and sin(ωt + cos(2ωt)).
A function is considered non-periodic if it does not repeat its value after any fixed interval of time.
Examples of non-periodic functions include e^(-ωt), log(ωt), and sin(-ωt).
A periodic function can be expressed as a superposition of sine and cosine functions of different time periods with suitable coefficients.
According to the Fourier theorem, any periodic function can be expressed as a superposition of sine and cosine functions of different time periods with suitable coefficients.
Simple harmonic motion (SHM) is a type of periodic motion where the displacement is a sinusoidal function of time.
SHM is a type of periodic motion where the acceleration is proportional to the displacement from the equilibrium position
The equation of SHM is x(t) = A cos ωt, where A is the amplitude and ω is the angular frequency
The period of SHM is the time taken for one complete oscillation, given by T = 2π/ω
The phase constant (φ) is the value of the phase angle at t = 0, and determines the initial position of the particle
The phase of SHM is the time-dependent quantity, ωt + φ, where ω is the angular frequency and φ is the phase constant
The amplitude of SHM is the magnitude of the maximum displacement of the particle
SHM can be represented by various types of graphs, including displacement-time graphs and velocity-time graphs
The angular frequency ω is related to the period T by the equation ω = 2π/T

concepts.concept_title: Uniform Circular Motion and Angular Frequency
concepts.description: Uniform circular motion with angular frequency ω = 2π/T, where T is the period.
Angular frequency of simple harmonic motion is 2π/T times the frequency of oscillation.

concepts.concept_title: Projection of Circular Motion on Diameter as SHM
concepts.description: The projection of uniform circular motion on a diameter of the circle follows simple harmonic motion.
S.I. unit of angular frequency is radians per second.

concepts.concept_title: Relation between Angular Frequency and Frequency of Oscillations
concepts.description: Angular frequency is two times the frequency of oscillations.
Simple harmonic motion equation: x(t) = A cos (ωt + φ), where x is the position, A is the amplitude, ω is the angular frequency, t is time, and φ is the phase.

A reference circle is used to describe the motion of a particle executing simple harmonic motion. The motion of the particle can be projected onto the x and y axes, resulting in simple harmonic motion of the same amplitude but differing by a phase of π/2.
The phase of a particle executing simple harmonic motion is determined by the difference between its position at a given time and its equilibrium position.

In uniform circular motion, the centripetal force is directed towards the center and its magnitude is given by mv^2/A or m(ωA)^2. In simple harmonic motion, the force acting on a particle is different from the centripetal force needed for uniform circular motion.

concepts.concept_title: Instantaneous Velocity and Acceleration in SHM
concepts.description: The instantaneous velocity and acceleration of a particle executing simple harmonic motion can be obtained by differentiating the displacement equation with respect to time.
The natural unit of angle is the radian, defined as the ratio of arc length to radius.
Trigonometric functions can be used with radian or degree units. When an angle is mentioned without units, it is assumed to be in radians.
The acceleration of a particle is the projection of the acceleration of the reference particle.

concepts.concept_title: Velocity of a Particle in Simple Harmonic Motion
concepts.description: The velocity of a particle in SHM is proportional to the displacement from the mean position.
The acceleration of a particle in SHM is proportional to the displacement and directed towards the mean position.

concepts.concept_title: Simple Harmonic Motion in Terms of Acceleration
concepts.description: The SHM can be defined in terms of acceleration as a(t) = -ω2x(t).

concepts.concept_title: Phase Difference Between Velocity and Acceleration
concepts.description: The velocity plot has a phase difference of π/2 with respect to the displacement plot, and the acceleration plot has a phase difference of π.
The force acting on a particle in SHM is proportional to the displacement and directed towards the mean position.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship Between Force and Displacement in SHM
concepts.description: The force acting on a particle in SHM is given by F(t) = -kx(t), where k = mω2.
The acceleration of a particle in simple harmonic motion is given by a = -ω^2x, where ω is the angular frequency and x is the displacement.

A linear harmonic oscillator is a system where the force is proportional to displacement, whereas a non-linear oscillator is a system where the force is proportional to a higher power of displacement.
The period of simple harmonic motion is given by T = 2π/ω, where ω is the angular frequency.
The total energy of a particle in simple harmonic motion is given by E = (1/2)mω^2A^2, where m is the mass, ω is the angular frequency, and A is the amplitude.

concepts.concept_title: Kinetic and Potential Energy in SHM
concepts.description: The kinetic energy of a particle in simple harmonic motion is given by K = (1/2)mω^2A^2 sin^2(ωt), and the potential energy is given by U = (1/2)kx^2, where m is the mass, ω is the angular frequency, A is the amplitude, and k is the spring constant.
A conservative force is a force that can be derived from a potential energy function, such as the spring force F = -kx.

concepts.concept_title: Equation of Simple Harmonic Motion in Terms of Displacement
concepts.description: The equation of motion of a particle in simple harmonic motion is given by x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ), where A is the amplitude, ω is the angular frequency, and φ is the phase angle.
SHM is a type of periodic motion where the acceleration of the particle is proportional to its displacement from the mean position.
The potential energy in SHM is dependent on the displacement of the particle from its mean position, with energy minimum at the mean position and maximum at the extreme displacements.
