The total mechanical energy in SHM is constant and independent of time, and is the sum of kinetic and potential energies.
A motion is periodic if it repeats after a fixed interval of time, such as a period of SHM.
The angular frequency of SHM is given by the equation ω = √(k/m), where k is the spring constant and m is the mass of the particle.

concepts.concept_title: Displacement as a Function of Time in SHM
concepts.description: For SHM, displacement is given by the equation x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ), where A is the amplitude and φ is the phase angle.
The total energy of a system remains constant if no external force does work on the system.
A type of periodic motion where the acceleration of the object is directly proportional to the displacement from its mean position.
The torque that opposes the displacement of an object from its mean position, causing it to oscillate.
The acceleration of an object moving in a circular path, consisting of a radial component towards the center and a tangential component along the path.
The total energy of a simple pendulum at its mean position is equal to the sum of its total energy at any other position.
Periodic motion is a type of motion that repeats itself. This definition is crucial for understanding simple harmonic motion.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship between Time Period and Frequency
concepts.description: The period T is related to the frequency v by the equation T = 1/v.
The unit of frequency is hertz (Hz), which represents one oscillation per second.
The displacement x(t) of a particle from its equilibrium position in simple harmonic motion is given by the equation x(t) = A cos (ωt + φ ).
The angular frequency ω is related to the period and frequency of the motion by the equation ω = 2π/T = 2π/T.
Simple harmonic motion can be viewed as the projection of uniform circular motion on the diameter of the circle in which the latter motion occurs.
The particle velocity and acceleration during simple harmonic motion as functions of time are given by v(t) and a(t) = -ω2x(t) respectively.
The force acting in simple harmonic motion is proportional to the displacement and is always directed towards the centre of motion.
A particle executing simple harmonic motion has, at any time, kinetic energy K = ½ mv2 and potential energy U = ½ kx2.

concepts.concept_title: Time Period of a Simple Pendulum
concepts.description: The time period of a simple pendulum is given by the equation T = 2π √(L/g).
For small angles, the function sinθ can be approximated by θ.
Mechanical energy of a system is the sum of kinetic and potential energy and remains constant over time.
A mass oscillating under Hooke's law restoring force exhibits simple harmonic motion with a specific period and angular frequency.
The period of a simple harmonic motion is given by T = 2π√(m/k) and is independent of amplitude and energy.

concepts.concept_title: Simple Harmonic Motion of a Pendulum
concepts.description: The period of a simple pendulum is given by T = 2π√(L/g) and is a specific case of simple harmonic motion.
Simple harmonic motion is characterized by a fixed amplitude and frequency, and the motion repeats itself after a specific period.
Simple harmonic motion requires a restoring force proportional to displacement and a fixed amplitude.
Periodic motion is not necessarily simple harmonic motion and requires a repeating pattern of motion.
Damped simple harmonic motion is characterized by a decay in amplitude over time due to energy losses.
Simple harmonic motion can be expressed in various forms, including x = A cos ωt + B sin ωt and x = A cos (ωt + α).
Examples of SHM include the rotation of a body about a fixed axis, oscillating mercury column, and a ball bearing rolling down a curved surface.

concepts.concept_title: Periodic but not Simple Harmonic Motion
concepts.description: Periodic but not SHM includes the motion of a polyatomic molecule, the rotation of the Earth about its axis, and vibrations of a polyatomic molecule.
SHM can be represented as a sinusoidal waveform, with the particle moving back and forth about its equilibrium position.
There are three types of periodic functions: (1) simple harmonic function, (2) periodic but not simple harmonic function, and (3) non-periodic function.

concepts.concept_title: Velocity, Acceleration, and Force in SHM
concepts.description: In SHM, the velocity, acceleration, and force on the particle are all periodic functions that oscillate about their equilibrium values.
The acceleration and displacement of a particle in SHM are related by the equation a = –ω^2x.
The displacement function in SHM is given by x(t) = A cos (ωt + φ), where A is the amplitude, ω is the angular frequency, and φ is the initial phase angle.

concepts.concept_title: Weight of a Body in SHM
concepts.description: The weight of a body in SHM is related to the period of oscillation by the equation W = 2π^2m/A^2T^2.
The frequency of oscillations in a mass-spring system is given by f = (1/2π)√(k/m).
The maximum acceleration in SHM is given by a_max = ω^2A.
The maximum speed in SHM is given by v_max = ωA.
SHM functions have different initial phases based on the initial positions, but frequency and amplitude remain constant.
x-projection of the radius vector of a revolving particle in circular motion has a simple harmonic motion.
For a simple harmonic motion, the reference circle can be plotted by indicating the initial position, radius, and angular speed.
The maximum extension of a spring is different in two cases: when attached to a mass with one end free and one end fixed, and when attached to two masses at both ends.

concepts.concept_title: Period of oscillation of a spring
concepts.description: The period of oscillation of a spring-mass system depends on the force constant and the mass attached to it.

concepts.concept_title: Maximum speed of a moving piston
concepts.description: The maximum speed of a piston moving in simple harmonic motion is equal to the product of its angular frequency and amplitude.

concepts.concept_title: Time period of a simple pendulum on the moon
concepts.description: The time period of a simple pendulum on the moon can be calculated using the formula for the time period on Earth and the ratio of gravitational accelerations.

concepts.concept_title: Time period of a simple pendulum in a rotating car
concepts.description: The time period of a simple pendulum in a rotating car is affected by the car's angular speed and the pendulum's mass and length.
A floating object in a liquid oscillates in simple harmonic motion when slightly depressed and released.

concepts.concept_title: Time period of a floating object
concepts.description: The time period of a floating object oscillating in simple harmonic motion depends on the densities of the object and the liquid, as well as the acceleration due to gravity.
In a U-tube manometer, when the suction pump is removed, the column of mercury executes simple harmonic motion due to the constant pressure difference between the two columns.
A type of wave that requires a medium for propagation and a type of wave that does not require a medium.
The characteristics of mechanical waves, such as waves on a string, water waves and sound waves.
A moving disturbance created in a medium without the actual physical transfer or flow of matter.
Waves transport energy from one point to another through the pattern of disturbance.
Mechanical waves require a medium to propagate, whereas non-mechanical waves do not.
Examples of different types of waves, including sound waves, water waves, and light waves.
Waves on a string, water waves, and sound waves are examples of mechanical waves.
Waves are used for communication and the pattern of disturbance has information that propagate from one point to another.
Examples of different types of communication, including speech, sound waves, and electromagnetic waves.
Waves transport energy and the pattern of disturbance does not involve the actual physical transfer or flow of matter.
Waves that require a material medium for their propagation.
Distinction between travelling waves and standing waves.
Importance of elastic properties of a medium in determining wave propagation.
Dependence of speed of mechanical waves on the properties of the medium.
Type of waves that do not require a material medium for their propagation.
Common speed of electromagnetic waves in vacuum, c.
Value of speed of electromagnetic waves in vacuum, c = 299, 792, 458 m/s.
Type of waves associated with constituents of matter: electrons, protons, neutrons, atoms, and molecules.
A wave is called transverse if the constituents of the medium oscillate perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation.
A wave is called longitudinal if the constituents of the medium oscillate along the direction of wave propagation.
Waves can be described by their characteristic properties, which can be studied in the subsequent sections of the chapter.
An example of a transverse wave is a wave traveling along a stretched string, where elements of the string oscillate up and down.
In a transverse wave, elements of the string oscillate about their equilibrium position perpendicular to the direction of wave motion.
An example of a longitudinal wave is the propagation of sound waves in a long pipe filled with air, where a pulse of condensations and rarefactions is generated.
In a crystalline solid, atoms or group of atoms are arranged in a periodic lattice, and displacing one atom leads to restoring forces, similar to a spring.

Waves are classified into two main types: transverse and longitudinal waves. Transverse waves are characterized by particle motion normal to the direction of propagation, while longitudinal waves involve compressive strain along the direction of propagation.
Transverse waves require a medium that can sustain shearing stress, such as solids. They propagate only in media that allow shearing strain.
Longitudinal waves can propagate in all elastic media, but air can sustain only longitudinal waves. In a material like steel, both transverse and longitudinal waves can propagate.

A progressive wave can be described using a function of position (x) and time (t). The function should give the shape of the wave at every instant and describe the motion of constituents at every location.
A wave that propagates through a medium is termed a travelling wave. The displacement of its constituents is given by the equation y(x,t)=asin(kx-wt+φ).
The wave function is expressed as y(x,t) = asin(kx - wt+φ) where a is the amplitude, k is the wave number, and φ is the initial phase angle.

The equation y(x,t) = asin(kx - wt+φ) represents a travelling wave propagating in the positive direction of the x-axis. The shape of the wave at any fixed instant is a sine wave, and the displacement of its constituents varies sinusoidally with time.
The angular wave number 'k' determines the shape of the wave at any fixed instant and is related to the wavelength 'λ' as k = 2π/λ.
The angular frequency 'ω' is the rate of change of the phase angle with time and is related to the frequency 'f' as ω = 2πf.
The amplitude 'a' of a wave is its maximum displacement from the equilibrium position.
The phase angle 'φ' is a measure of the initial position of the wave and is an important characteristic of a wave.
The speed of a wave is determined by the angular frequency 'ω' and the wave number 'k' and is related to the wavelength 'λ' as v = ω/k = λf.
The direction of propagation of a wave can be determined by the sign of the wave number 'k' and is given by the direction of the group velocity.
A wave equation is a mathematical relationship that describes the behavior of a wave and can be used to determine its properties.

concepts.concept_title: Displacement and Amplitude of a Wave
concepts.description: Displacement 'y' of a wave varies between a and –a, with 'a' being the maximum displacement or amplitude.

concepts.concept_title: Crest and Trough of a Wave
concepts.description: Crest is the point of maximum positive displacement and trough is the point of maximum negative displacement.
The quantity (kx – ωt + φ) as the argument of the sine function in equation of wave is called the phase of the wave.
The phase at x = 0 and t = 0 is called the initial phase.
The minimum distance between two points having the same phase is called the wavelength of the wave.
For a wave, the angular wave number is represented by k, where k = 2π / λ.
The angular frequency of a wave is represented by ω, where ω = 2πν.
The frequency of a wave is represented by v, where v = (1 / T) and T is the time period of the wave.

concepts.concept_title: Definition of Angular Frequency and Linear Frequency
concepts.description: The angular frequency (ω) is defined as the rate of change of phase per unit time, and linear frequency (f) is the number of oscillations per second.
The SI unit of angular frequency is radian per second (rad/s).

concepts.concept_title: Relation between Wavelength and Angular Wave Number
concepts.description: The wavelength (λ) and angular wave number (k) are related through the equation λ = 2π/k.
The angular wave number (k) is defined as the total phase difference that can be accommodated per unit length.

concepts.concept_title: Relation between Wavelength and Period of a Wave
concepts.description: The wavelength (λ) and period (T) of a wave are related through the equation λ = vT, where v is the speed of the wave.

concepts.concept_title: Displacement Function of a Longitudinal Wave
concepts.description: The displacement function of a longitudinal wave is given by s(x, t) = a sin (kx – ωt + φ).

concepts.concept_title: Relation between Angular Wave Number and Period of a Wave
concepts.description: The angular wave number (k) and period (T) of a wave are related through the equation k = 2π / λ = 2π / vT.
The speed of a wave is the rate at which the entire wave pattern shifts, given by v = Δx / Δt.

concepts.concept_title: Motion of a Crest of a Wave
concepts.description: The motion of a crest of a wave is the same as the motion of the entire wave pattern.
The fixed phase point on a wave is given by the equation kx - ωt = constant, where k is the wave number and ω is the angular frequency.

concepts.concept_title: Period and Frequency of Progressive Waves
concepts.description: The period (T) of a wave is related to the angular frequency (ω) by T = 2π/ω and the frequency (v) is related to T by v = 1/T.

concepts.concept_title: Relating ω to T and k to λ
concepts.description: The angular frequency ω is related to period T and the wave number k is related to wavelength λ.
In the time required for one full oscillation by any constituent of the medium, the wave pattern travels a distance equal to the wavelength of the wave.
The speed of a mechanical wave is determined by the inertial and elastic properties of the medium.

concepts.concept_title: Speed of a Transverse Wave on a String
concepts.description: The speed of a transverse wave on a string is determined by the tension T in the string and the mass per unit length µ.
The speed v of a wave is related to frequency v and wavelength λ by the equation v = λv.
Wave speed on a string can be derived using dimensional analysis. The wave speed is given by v = C (T/µ).
The bulk modulus of a medium determines the stress under compressional strain and is given by B = - D P / (D V / V).
The speed of longitudinal waves in a medium is given by v = C (B/ρ).
Dimensional analysis shows that the quantity T/µ has the relevant dimension [L^2T^-2].
The quantity B/ρ has the relevant dimension [L^2T^-2].

concepts.concept_title: Speed of Longitudinal Waves in Solids and Liquids
concepts.description: Solids and liquids have higher speeds of sound than gases due to higher mass densities and bulk moduli.

The principle of superposition states that the actual disturbance in a medium is the algebraic sum of the individual displacements of the individual wave pulses. This means if two waves are superimposed, the displacement of the resultant wave is the sum of the displacements of the individual waves.

Constructive interference occurs when the amplitudes of two waves add up, resulting in a larger amplitude. Destructive interference occurs when the amplitudes of two waves subtract, resulting in a smaller amplitude or zero displacement.

When a wave or pulse meets a boundary, it can be reflected or transmitted into the second medium. The reflection at a rigid boundary causes a phase change of π or 180°, while the reflection at an open boundary causes no phase change.
Snell's law states that the incident and refracted waves obey the law of refraction, but the incident and reflected waves obey the usual laws for reflection at a rigid or open boundary.
When a wave or pulse is reflected at a rigid boundary, it suffers a phase change of π or 180°, while at an open boundary it experiences no phase change.
A harmonic travelling wave is a wave that has a specific frequency and wavelength, and can be described by the equation y(x,t) = a sin(kx - ωt + φ).
Waves can propagate in an unbounded medium, such as a stretched string, but can also be affected by boundaries, such as a rigid or open boundary.
According to experiments, there are only two kinds of electric charges, which repel each other (like charges) and attract each other (unlike charges).
The property that differentiates the two kinds of electric charges.

concepts.concept_title: Behavior of Like and Unlike Charges
concepts.description: Like charges repel each other, unlike charges attract each other.
Rubbing two insulating surfaces together generates static electricity, causing one surface to acquire a positive charge and the other a negative charge.
On rubbing, objects like glass, plastic rods, silk, and fur acquire an electric charge.

concepts.concept_title: Attraction and Repulsion of Like and Unlike Charges
concepts.description: Like charges repel each other, unlike charges attract each other
Electric charges can be positive or negative, neutralize each other

concepts.concept_title: Convention of Positive and Negative Charges
concepts.description: Glass rods are considered positive, plastic rods are considered negative by convention
A neutral body can acquire charge by gaining or losing electrons
Charge is transferred when a body comes into contact with another

concepts.concept_title: Types of Materials - Conductors and Insulators
concepts.description: Conductors allow free movement of electric charges, insulators do not
Materials that allow electricity to flow through them are called conductors and materials that do not allow electricity to flow through them are called insulators.
Electric charges can be added together like real numbers to get the total charge of a system.
Electric charge has magnitude but no direction, similar to mass.
Electric charge is neither created nor destroyed in a closed system, it is conserved.
Proper signs must be used while adding charges in a system, as a charge can be either positive or negative.
Total charge in an isolated system is conserved and cannot be created or destroyed.
Electric charge is always an integral multiple of a basic unit of charge e, which is the charge carried by an electron or a proton.
The total charge on a body is the sum of charges carried by its constituent particles, and it is always an integral multiple of e.
The SI unit of charge is the coulomb (C), which is defined in terms of the unit of electric current.

concepts.concept_title: Value of Basic Unit of Charge
concepts.description: The value of the basic unit of charge e is 1.602192 × 10^-19 C.
Smaller units of charge are microcoulomb (1 μC = 10^-6 C) and millicoulomb (1 mC = 10^-3 C).
The only basic charges in the universe are protons and electrons, and all observable charges are integral multiples of e.
Charge is not continuous but appears to be continuous at the macroscopic level due to the large number of electrons.
The magnitude of charge is enormous compared to the electronic charge at the macroscopic level.
Charge quantization is significant at the microscopic level but can be ignored at the macroscopic level.
Estimation of the time required to collect a specific charge by electron transfer.
Estimation of the number of electrons in a specific material, such as a piece of copper.
Calculation of the total positive and negative charge in a material, such as a cup of water.
Treatment of charged bodies as point charges and Coulomb’s law for the force between two point charges.

Coulomb's Law states that the magnitude of the force between two point charges is directly proportional to the product of the magnitudes of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
Coulomb's method of charging spheres involves putting a charged sphere in contact with an uncharged sphere and measuring the transfer of charge.
The inverse square law states that the force between two points is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

concepts.concept_title: Coulomb's Law and Unit of Charge
concepts.description: Coulomb's Law can be used to define a unit of charge, with the value of k determining the size of the unit.
Coulomb used a torsion balance to measure the force between two charged metallic spheres at different distances, which led to the establishment of Coulomb's Law.

Coulomb's law states that the magnitude of the electrostatic force between two point charges is directly proportional to the product of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
Permittivity of free space is a physical constant that is defined as the measure of the resistance of free space to the formation of an electric field.
Coulomb's law can be expressed using vector notation, which takes into account the direction of the force between two charges.
The sum of two charges is equal to the sum of their individual magnitudes, and this is a fundamental principle of electrostatics.

concepts.concept_title: Unit Vectors and Direction of Force
concepts.description: Unit vectors are used to specify the direction of force between two charges, depending on the signs of the charges.

concepts.concept_title: Newton's Third Law and Coulomb's Law
concepts.description: Coulomb's law is consistent with Newton's third law of motion, which states that every action has an equal and opposite reaction.

concepts.concept_title: Coulomb's Law for Two Point Charges
concepts.description: Coulomb's law states that the electrostatic force between two point charges is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. The force is given by the equation F = k * (q1 * q2) / r^2, where k is Coulomb's constant.

concepts.concept_title: Comparison of Electric and Gravitational Forces
concepts.description: The ratio of electric force to gravitational force between two point charges is given by F_e/F_g = e^2 / (4*pi*e*G*m*m), where e is the elementary charge and m is the mass of the particles.
The electric force between two particles with charges q1 and q2 is given by F_e = k * (q1 * q2) / r^2, where k is Coulomb's constant and r is the distance between the particles.
The acceleration of a particle due to electric force is given by a = F_e / m, where F_e is the electric force and m is the mass of the particle.

concepts.concept_title: Sign of Electric and Gravitational Forces
concepts.description: The signs of electric and gravitational forces are different for like charges and opposite charges.

concepts.concept_title: Ratio of Electric and Gravitational Forces
concepts.description: The ratio of electric and gravitational forces between two particles can be approximated by F_e / F_g ≈ e^2 / (4*pi*e*G*m^2) ≈ 10^36.
The electrostatic force between two charges is given by Coulomb's law: 1 qq' / (4πεo r^2) where q and q' are the magnitudes of the charges and εo is the electric constant.
The total force on a charge due to multiple charges is the vector sum of the individual forces on that charge due to each of the other charges.
The force on a charge due to a set of multiple charges can be found by applying Coulomb's law separately to each pair of charges and summing the resulting forces.

concepts.concept_title: Force on a Charge due to Multiple Charges
concepts.description: The force on a charge due to a set of multiple charges is the vector sum of the individual forces on that charge due to each of the other charges.

concepts.concept_title: Parallelogram Law of Addition for Electrostatic Forces
concepts.description: Forces of electrostatic origin do not add according to the parallelogram law of addition, unlike forces of mechanical origin.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Force due to Multiple Charges
concepts.description: The electric force due to multiple charges is given by adding the individual electric forces due to each charge, taking into account the direction of the forces as specified by the vector addition.

The principle states that in a system of charges, the force on one charge due to another is unaffected by the presence of other charges. The total force on a charge is the vector sum of the forces due to each individual charge.

concepts.concept_title: Coulomb's Law for System of Charges
concepts.description: The force between two charges in a system of charges is given by Coulomb's Law, which states that F = (1/4πε) × (q1 × q2)/r^2, where q1 and q2 are the charges and r is the distance between them.

concepts.concept_title: Vector Sum of Forces for Multiple Charges
concepts.description: The total force on a charge in a system of charges is obtained by the vector sum of the forces due to each individual charge using the parallelogram law of addition of vectors.
In a system of charges, the forces on a charge can be simplified using symmetry, where equal and opposite forces cancel each other out, resulting in a net zero force.

concepts.concept_title: Force on a Charge at the Centroid of an Equilateral Triangle
concepts.description: The force on a charge at the centroid of an equilateral triangle due to three charges at its vertices is given by F = (4πε × Q × q)/l^2, where Q and q are the charges and l is the side length of the triangle.

concepts.concept_title: Resultant Force in a System of Three Charges with Equal Positive and Negative Charges
concepts.description: In a system of three charges with two equal positive charges and one equal negative charge, the resultant force on each charge is zero due to symmetry.
In electrostatics, unit vectors are used to represent the direction of forces, such as rˆ for a force along a line connecting two charges.

concepts.concept_title: Coulomb's Law Consistency with Newton's Third Law
concepts.description: Coulomb's law is consistent with Newton's third law, resulting in the sum of forces on three charges being zero.
The concept of electric field is introduced as a distributed quantity that varies with position, describing the effect of a charge.
The electric field E(r) is given by 1/4πε₀ * (Q / r²) r̂, where r̂ is a unit vector from the origin.

concepts.concept_title: Force on a Charge in Electric Field
concepts.description: The force F on a charge q due to electric field E is given by F = qE, where q is the charge and E is the electric field.
The SI unit of electric field is N/C.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field as a Force on a Unit Charge
concepts.description: The electric field at a point is numerically equal to the force experienced by a unit positive charge.
A source charge is a charge that produces an electric field, while a test charge is a charge that experiences the effect of the electric field.

The electric field is defined as the ratio of electric force (F) to test charge (q). It is an operational definition based on the force experienced by a charged object placed in the electric field of another charge.
The electric field is independent of the test charge (q) and depends only on the position of the charge in the space. It can be observed at every point in three-dimensional space.

The electric field direction is radially outward from a positive charge and radially inward from a negative charge. The magnitude of the electric field depends only on the distance from the charge and not on the charge itself.
At equal distances from a point charge, the magnitude of the electric field is the same, exhibiting spherical symmetry.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field Due to a System of Charges
concepts.description: The electric field at a point in space due to a system of charges is the sum of the electric fields due to each individual charge, calculated using Coulomb's law and the superposition principle.
key_terms: electric field due to a system of charges

concepts.concept_title: Expression for Electric Field Due to a Point Charge
concepts.description: The electric field E at a distance r from a point charge q is given by E = (1/4πε0) × (q / r^2) × r̂, where r̂ is a unit vector in the direction from the charge to the point.
key_terms: electric field expression for a point charge

concepts.description: The superposition principle states that the electric field at a point due to multiple charges is the vector sum of the electric fields due to individual charges.
Electric field is a vector quantity that varies from one point to another point in space.
The electric field at a point is given by the equation E(r) = 1/r^2 (q * r^) and can be represented by a vector equation.
The electric field at a point in space around a system of charges tells you the force a unit positive test charge would experience if placed at that point.
Electric field is a vector field since force is a vector quantity.

concepts.concept_title: Concept of Field in Time-Dependent Phenomena
concepts.description: The concept of electric field is useful in time-dependent electromagnetic phenomena where information cannot travel instantaneously.
Electromagnetic waves propagate with the speed of light "c".
The electric field is independent of the test charge used to determine it.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field due to a Point Charge
concepts.description: Electric field is a vector quantity represented by vectors pointing along the direction of the electric field. Its magnitude is proportional to the strength of the field.
Electric field can be represented pictorially by drawing vector arrows pointing along the direction of the electric field. The density of field lines represents the magnitude of the field.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field Lines and their Interpretation
concepts.description: Electric field lines are a graphical representation of the electric field. The density of field lines is proportional to the magnitude of the field.

concepts.concept_title: Direction and Magnitude of Total Electric Field
concepts.description: The direction and magnitude of the total electric field at a point are determined by the superposition of electric fields due to individual charges.
The strength of an electric field is inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the charge.

concepts.concept_title: Direction of Electric Field at Different Points
concepts.description: The direction of an electric field is determined by the direction of the lines of force.
The electric field is proportional to the number of field lines per unit solid angle.

concepts.concept_title: Relation Between Area and Solid Angle
concepts.description: The solid angle of an area is inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the point.

concepts.concept_title: Lines of Force and Electric Field
concepts.description: Lines of force, also known as field lines, are a way to visualize the electric field around a charge.
The tangent to a field line at a point is in the direction of the electric field at that point.
Electric field lines start from positive charges, end at negative charges, and can be taken as continuous curves without breaks.
Electric field lines follow four important properties: (i) start from positive charges, (ii) end at negative charges, (iii) can't cross each other, and (iv) don't form closed loops.
Electric flux is defined as the number of electric field lines crossing a unit area placed normal to the field at a point.
The direction of electric field lines is perpendicular to their line of action and is a tangent to the curve formed by the field lines.

concepts.concept_title: Proportionality of Electric Field to Area
concepts.description: The number of electric field lines crossing a unit area is proportional to the electric field strength at that point.
The direction of an area element is along its normal, but the normal can point in two directions. A convention is used to resolve this ambiguity.

concepts.concept_title: Area Element Vector for Curved Surface
concepts.description: The area element of a curved surface can be treated as a planar area element by dividing the surface into small area elements and associating a vector with each.
For a closed surface, the convention is to choose the outward normal as the direction of the area element vector.
Electric flux Df through an area element DS is defined by Df = E.DS = E DS cosq, which is proportional to the number of field lines cutting the area element.

concepts.concept_title: Component of Electric Field along Normal
concepts.description: The component of electric field along the normal to the area element is given by E DS cosq, which is also the magnitude of electric flux.
The total electric flux f through a surface S is given by f ~ S E.DS, which is the sum of the electric flux at each area element.
The electric field due to a dipole is not zero even if the total charge is zero, and it falls off faster than 1/r^2 at large distances.
An electric dipole is a pair of equal and opposite point charges separated by a distance, with the direction from negative to positive considered the direction of the dipole.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field for Points on the Dipole Axis
concepts.description: The electric field due to a dipole at a point on its axis is given by E = p / (4πε (r^2 - a^2)^2) for r >> a.
At large distances (r >> a), the electric field due to a dipole falls off as 1/r^3.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field for Points on the Equatorial Plane
concepts.description: The total electric field at a point on the equatorial plane is given by E = – (E + E) cosθ pˆ, where θ is the angle between the dipole axis and the electric field.
The dipole moment vector p is the sum of the position vectors of the two charges and is given by p = q × 2a.
The dipole moment vector p of an electric dipole is defined as p = q × 2a, where q is the charge and a is the separation between the pair of charges.

concepts.concept_title: Dipole Field at Large Distances on Axis
concepts.description: The electric field of a dipole at large distances is given by E = 4p / εr^3, along the dipole axis.

concepts.concept_title: Dipole Field at Large Distances on Equatorial Plane
concepts.description: The electric field of a dipole at large distances is given by E = -4p / εr^3, at a point on the equatorial plane.
A molecule develops a dipole moment when the centres of positive charges and negative charges do not coincide.
A point dipole is a dipole with negligible size and is defined as the limit when the dipole size a approaches zero and the charge q approaches infinity.
For a point dipole, the electric field is given by E = 1 / (4πεr^2) ( p · r / r^3) at a near point.
The electric field at a far-away point on the axis of a dipole is given by the formula E = 4πε₀r^₃ p / r^₂, where p is the dipole moment.
The electric field at a distance r from the centre on the axis of the dipole has a magnitude E = 4πε₀r^₃ p / a³, where p is the dipole moment.
The electric field at a point on the normal to the axis of the dipole is given by E = 4πε₀p / (2πϵ₀r^(2)) = 2p E₀ / r^(2), where p is the dipole moment and E₀ is the external field.
The net force on a dipole in a uniform external field is zero, but there is a torque due to the separation of the charges.
The magnitude of the torque on a dipole is given by τ = p E sinq, where p is the dipole moment and E is the external field.
A dipole will align itself with a uniform external field in the direction of the field.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Dipole in a Uniform Electric Field
concepts.description: A dipole in a uniform electric field experiences no net torque but may experience a net force. The force depends on the orientation of p with respect to E.

concepts.concept_title: Force on a Dipole in a Non-uniform Electric Field
concepts.description: A dipole in a non-uniform electric field experiences a net force due to the non-uniformity of the field. The force depends on the orientation of p with respect to E.

concepts.concept_title: Induction of Dipole Moment in a Conductor
concepts.description: When a charged object Polarises a conductor, it induces a net dipole moment in the conductor in the direction of the electric field.
A continuous charge distribution is more feasible to work with than discrete charges for many purposes. It is defined as a surface, line, or volume charge density.
Surface charge density is defined as the charge on an area element divided by the area itself. It represents the macroscopic surface charge density.
A macroscopic charge density is defined as the total charge contained in a macroscopically small volume element divided by the volume of the element. It has units of C/m^3.
A continuous charge distribution is analogous to a continuous mass distribution in mechanics, where a large number of discrete charges are considered as a continuous fluid.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field Due to a Continuous Charge Distribution
concepts.description: The electric field due to a continuous charge distribution can be obtained by summing the electric fields due to small volume elements, using Coulomb's law and the superposition principle.
The electric flux through a sphere enclosing a point charge q at its centre is equal to q/ε0, where ε0 is the electric constant.
The electric flux through a sphere due to a point charge is 4πεr^2.

concepts.concept_title: Total Flux through Multiple Area Elements
concepts.description: The total flux is obtained by adding the flux through all individual area elements.
The electric flux through a closed surface is equal to the charge enclosed by the surface.

concepts.concept_title: Zero Flux through a Closed Surface with No Charge
concepts.description: If there is no charge enclosed by the surface, the total electric flux is zero.

concepts.concept_title: Gauss's Law for a Uniform Electric Field
concepts.description: The total electric flux through a closed surface in a uniform electric field is zero.
Gauss's law is a general result of electrostatics, applicable to any closed surface.
The law applies to any closed surface and includes the sum of all charges enclosed.
A surface chosen for the application of Gauss's law to calculate electrostatic field and total charge inside a region.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field due to a System of Discrete Charges
concepts.description: The electric field due to a system of discrete charges is not well defined at the location of any charge and grows without bound as the distance to the charge decreases.

concepts.concept_title: Gauss's Law for a Symmetric System
concepts.description: Gauss's law is often useful for easy calculation of electrostatic field for a system with symmetry by choosing a suitable Gaussian surface.

concepts.concept_title: Inverse Square Dependence of Coulomb's Law
concepts.description: Coulomb's law is based on the inverse square dependence on distance, and any violation of Gauss's law indicates a departure from the inverse square law.

concepts.concept_title: Flux through a Face of a Cube
concepts.description: The electric flux through a face of a cube is calculated using the formula f = E.DS, where E is the electric field and DS is the area of the face.

The net electric flux through a cube is the sum of the fluxes through its faces, calculated using the formula ∑ f = EA - EB = 2A(E - E), where A is the area of each face and E and E are the electric fields at the left and right faces, respectively.

concepts.concept_title: Charge inside a Volume using Gauss's Law
concepts.description: The total charge q inside a volume can be calculated using Gauss's law, q = fe, where f is the net electric flux through a Gaussian surface enclosing the volume.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field and Flux through a Cylinder
concepts.description: The electric flux and charge inside a cylinder can be calculated using Gauss's law, given a uniform electric field E = 200 ˆi N/C for x > 0 and E = -200 ˆi N/C for x < 0.
The net charge inside a cylinder can be calculated using Gauss's law, q = fe, where f is the net electric flux through a Gaussian surface enclosing the cylinder.

Gauss's law relates the distribution of electric charge to the resulting electric field. It states that the total electric flux through a closed surface is proportional to the charge enclosed within the surface.
Symmetry in a charge distribution implies that the electric field must have the same magnitude at equivalent points and must be radial (outward or inward).

concepts.concept_title: Radial Electric Field due to Infinite Straight Wire
concepts.description: The electric field due to an infinite straight wire is radial (outward or inward) and depends only on the radial distance r. It is perpendicular to the surface of a cylindrical Gaussian surface.

concepts.concept_title: Flux Through a Cylindrical Gaussian Surface
concepts.description: The flux through the curved part of a cylindrical Gaussian surface is proportional to the surface area and the strength of the electric field. Flux through the two ends of the surface is zero.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field Magnitude and Charge Distribution
concepts.description: The magnitude of the electric field depends on the charge distribution and can be calculated using Gauss's law for symmetric configurations.
Gauss's law can be used to calculate the charge within a closed surface by relating the electric flux to the enclosed charge.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field of an Infinitely Long Wire
concepts.description: The electric field due to an infinitely long straight wire is given by E = 2pρ/r0, where ρ is the linear charge density and r0 is the distance from the wire.
The modulus of a vector is its magnitude without any consideration of its direction, represented by a non-negative value.

concepts.concept_title: Gaussian Surface for an Electric Field
concepts.description: A Gaussian surface is used to calculate electric flux through a closed surface, enclosed charge, and electric field strength.

concepts.concept_title: Field due to a Uniformly Charged Infinite Plane Sheet
concepts.description: The electric field due to an infinite plane sheet with uniform surface charge density s is given by E = s/2e0, independent of the x-coordinate.
An electric field can be represented as a vector, E, with direction and magnitude depending on the charge distribution and location.

concepts.concept_title: Gauss's Law Application in Electric Field
concepts.description: Gauss's law relates electric flux, enclosed charge, and the electric field strength, and is used to find electric field distributions.
A Gaussian surface with spherical symmetry can only depend on the radial distance from the center to the point.

concepts.concept_title: Field Outside Spherical Shell Using Gauss's Law
concepts.description: The electric field at a point outside a uniformly charged spherical shell is radial, and its magnitude decreases with increasing distance.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field due to Concentrated Charge at Center
concepts.description: For points outside a uniformly charged shell, the electric field due to the shell is the same as if all charge were concentrated at its center.

concepts.concept_title: Field Inside Spherical Shell Using Gauss's Law
concepts.description: The electric field at a point inside a uniformly charged spherical shell is zero.
The electric field at a point outside a spherical shell with a center charge is influenced by both the shell and the center charge.

concepts.concept_title: Dependence of Electric Field on Radial Distance
concepts.description: The magnitude of the electric field depends only on the radial distance from the origin to the point where the field is measured.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field at a Point on a Spherical Surface
concepts.description: The electric field at any point on a spherical surface has the same direction as the normal to the surface and the same magnitude.

concepts.concept_title: Charge Enclosed by a Gaussian Surface
concepts.description: The charge enclosed by a Gaussian surface is the sum of the positive nuclear charge and the negative charge within the surface.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field Inside a Spherical Gaussian Surface
concepts.description: The electric field inside a spherical Gaussian surface is given by E(r) = Ze / (4πεr^2R^3), where r < R.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field Outside a Spherical Gaussian Surface
concepts.description: The electric field outside a spherical Gaussian surface is zero, i.e., E(r) = 0, where r > R.
Electric charge has three basic properties: quantisation, additivity and conservation.
The total charge of a body is always an integral multiple of a basic quantum of charge (e) i.e., q = n e, where n = 0, ±1, ±2, ±3, ....
The total charge of a system is the algebraic sum of all individual charges in the system.
The total charge of an isolated system remains unchanged with time.
The mutual electrostatic force between two point charges is given by F = k * |q1| * |q2| / r^2, where k is Coulomb's constant.
The electrostatic force between two point charges is proportional to the product of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
The constant of proportionality, denoted as k, in Coulomb's Law is determined experimentally.

concepts.concept_title: Units of Charge and Coulomb's Constant
concepts.description: The unit of charge is coulomb (C) and the units of Coulomb's constant are N m^2 C^-2.

concepts.concept_title: Ratio of Electric and Gravitational Forces
concepts.description: The ratio of electric force to gravitational force between a proton and an electron is calculated.
The principle states that the forces with which two charges attract or repel each other are not affected by the presence of a third or more additional charge.
The electric field at a point due to a charge configuration is defined as the force on a small positive test charge divided by the magnitude of the test charge.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field Due to a Point Charge
concepts.description: The magnitude of the electric field due to a point charge is given by |q|/4πr^2.
The electric field due to a configuration of charges is the vector sum of the electric fields due to each charge.
Electric field lines are curves that give the direction of the electric field at each point.
Electric field lines are continuous curves without breaks, cannot cross each other, and start at positive charges and end at negative charges.
The dipole moment of a pair of equal and opposite charges separated by a distance is defined.
The electric field due to a dipole at a distance in its equatorial plane is given by 2p/a^3.

concepts.concept_title: Dipole Electric Field on the Axis
concepts.description: The electric field due to a dipole at a distance on its axis is given by p/4πε0r^3.

concepts.concept_title: Torque on a Dipole in Uniform Field
concepts.description: A dipole in a uniform electric field experiences a torque proportional to the product of dipole moment and electric field, but no net force.
The vector area element DS is defined as the product of area element DS and unit normal vector nˆ.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Flux through a Small Area Element
concepts.description: The electric flux Df through a small area element DS is given by Df = E.DS.
The flux of electric field through any closed surface S is 1/e times the total charge enclosed by S.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field due to a Thin Infinitely Long Straight Wire
concepts.description: The electric field E due to a thin infinitely long straight wire is given by E = nˆ / (2πεr).

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field due to an Infinite Thin Plane Sheet
concepts.description: The electric field E due to an infinite thin plane sheet is given by E = nˆ / (2ε₀).

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field due to a Thin Spherical Shell
concepts.description: The electric field E outside a thin spherical shell is given by E = rˆ / (4πε₀r²) for r > R, and E = 0 for r < R.
Charge density is defined as charge per unit length (linear), area (surface), or volume (volume).
Physical quantities have dimensions such as length, time, electric field, etc., and can be measured in various units.
The strong force has a small range of about 10⁻¹⁴ m, which is responsible for holding protons together in the nucleus.

concepts.concept_title: Comparison of Coulomb and Gravitational Forces
concepts.description: Coulomb force can be attractive or repulsive, whereas gravitational force is always attractive, leading to cancellation of electric forces.
The constant of proportionality k in Coulomb's law has a value of approximately 9 × 10^9 N m^2 C^-2 in SI units.
The unit of charge is defined as 1 C = 1 A s, and is related to the magnetic effect of current (Ampere's law).

concepts.concept_title: Large Size of Electric Charge Unit
concepts.description: The unit of electric charge is too big in terms of electric effects because it is defined in terms of magnetic forces.
Electric charge is a scalar with no direction associated with it, and has an additive property.
Electric charge is invariant under transformation of frames of reference in relative motion.
The total charge of an isolated system is conserved and is independent of the scalar nature of charge.
Electric charge is quantised, and there is no analogous law on quantisation of mass.
The force on one charge due to another charge is unaffected by the presence of other charges, and there are no additional forces that arise when there are more than two charges.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field Due to Discrete Charge Configuration
concepts.description: The electric field due to a discrete charge configuration is not defined at the locations of the discrete charges.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field Due to Continuous Volume Charge Distribution
concepts.description: The electric field due to a continuous volume charge distribution is defined at any point in the distribution.
The electric field due to a surface charge distribution is discontinuous across the surface.

concepts.concept_title: Field Due to Zero Net Charge Charge Configuration
concepts.description: The electric field due to a charge configuration with zero net charge is not zero, and falls off faster than 1/r^2 for large distances.
Electric charge comes in discrete values due to the quantization of charge.
Charge is conserved in an isolated system, meaning the total charge remains constant over time.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Force on a Single Charge
concepts.description: The electric force on a charge due to another charge is given by Coulomb's Law.
Electric field and electric potential are fundamental concepts in electrostatics.

concepts.concept_title: Torque on a Dipole in a Uniform Field
concepts.description: The torque on a dipole in a uniform electric field is given by the product of the dipole moment and the electric field.
Electric field lines are continuous and never intersect each other.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Dipole Moment and System Charge
concepts.description: The total charge of a system of charges is the sum of all individual charges, and the electric dipole moment is given by the product of charge and separation.
The electric flux through a surface is a measure of the amount of electric field that passes through that surface.
The total electric flux through a closed surface is proportional to the charge enclosed within that surface.
The electric flux passing through a plane surface is given by the dot product of the unit vector normal to the plane and the electric field.
If the electric field is uniform and constant within a closed surface, the total electric flux through the surface is equal to the product of the electric field and the area of the surface.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field and Flux through a Cube
concepts.description: The total electric flux through each face of a cube is equal, and the net flux through the cube is the sum of the flux through each face.

concepts.concept_title: Charge inside a Closed Surface - Gauss's Law
concepts.description: The net charge inside a closed surface can be determined by the total electric flux through the surface using Gauss's Law.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Flux through a Gaussian Surface
concepts.description: The electric flux through a Gaussian surface is proportional to the charge enclosed within the surface.
The surface charge density of a conductor can be related to the electric field at a distance from the conductor.
The linear charge density of a line charge can be related to the electric field at a distance from the line charge.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Flux through a Conductor - Gauss's Law
concepts.description: The electric flux through a conducting surface can be determined using Gauss's Law and the enclosed charge.

concepts.concept_title: Net Charge Inside a Conductor - Gauss's Law
concepts.description: The net charge inside a conductor can be determined using Gauss's Law and the electric flux through the conductor.
The work done by an external force in moving a charge from one point to another is equal to the potential energy difference between the points.
A force is conservative if the work done by it in moving a charge from one point to another depends only on the initial and final points, and not on the path taken.
The work done by an electrostatic field in moving a charge from one point to another depends only on the initial and final points, and not on the path taken.

concepts.concept_title: Potential Energy is Undetermined to Within an Additive Constant
concepts.description: The actual value of potential energy is not physically significant; it is only the difference of potential energy that is significant.
The potential energy of a charge q at any point is the work done by an external force in bringing the charge from infinity to that point.
Electric field and potential are related; potential is work done per unit test charge to bring from infinity to a point.
Potential difference is physically significant, not actual value of potential. It's the difference in potentials between two points.
Electrostatic potential at a point is work done in bringing a unit positive charge from infinity to that point.
Potential energy of a system is dependent on the initial and final positions, not on the path taken.
To find work done per unit test charge, we need to consider an infinitesimal test charge.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done on a Test Charge
concepts.description: The work done on a test charge is equal to the force at every point times the displacement.
key_terms: work done on a test charge

concepts.concept_title: Potential due to a Point Charge
concepts.description: The potential at a point P due to a charge Q is V(r) = (1/4πε0) (Q/r), where r is the distance between the charge and point P. The potential is negative for a negative charge.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done in Bringing a Charge from Infinity
concepts.description: The work done in bringing a charge q from infinity to a point P is given by W = qV, where V is the potential at point P. The work done is path independent.

concepts.concept_title: Variation of Potential and Electric Field with Distance
concepts.description: The electrostatic potential and electric field vary with distance according to V  1/r and E  1/r2, respectively.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Potential due to an Electric Dipole
concepts.description: The electric potential due to a dipole at a point with position vector r depends on the magnitude of r and the angle between r and the dipole moment vector p.
The potential at infinity is defined as zero, and this choice is consistent with the equation for the electric potential of a point charge.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Dipole Potential due to q and -q
concepts.description: The potential at a point due to a dipole is the sum of potentials due to its constituent charges q and -q, related by superposition principle.
For a dipole at the origin, the electric potential at a point is given by V = 4πεr^(-2), where r is the distance from the dipole and ε is the permittivity of the medium.
The electric potential of a dipole depends on the angle θ between the position vector and the dipole moment vector p.

concepts.concept_title: Difference between Dipole and Single Charge Potential
concepts.description: The electric potential of a dipole falls off as 1/r^2 at large distances, whereas a single charge potential falls off as 1/r.
The electric potential at a point due to a system of charges is the sum of potentials due to each charge, considering the superposition principle.

concepts.concept_title: Potential due to a Point Charge
concepts.description: The potential at a point P due to a point charge is given by V = 1/4πε0 × q / r, where r is the distance between the charge and the point.
The potential at a point due to a system of charges is the sum of the potentials due to individual charges, given by V = V1 + V2 + ... + Vn.

concepts.concept_title: Potential at a Point Due to Multiple Charges
concepts.description: The potential at a point P due to multiple charges is given by V = ∑ (1/4πε0 × q / r), where r is the distance between each charge and the point, and the sum is over all charges.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Potential Due to Continuous Charge Distribution
concepts.description: The potential due to a continuous charge distribution can be found by summing the contributions from small volume elements, each carrying a charge density ρ(r).

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field Outside a Uniformly Charged Spherical Shell
concepts.description: The electric field outside a uniformly charged spherical shell is given by E = 1/4πε0 × q / r, where q is the total charge on the shell and r is the distance from the center of the shell.

concepts.concept_title: Potential Outside a Uniformly Charged Spherical Shell
concepts.description: The potential outside a uniformly charged spherical shell is given by V = 1/4πε0 × q / r, where q is the total charge on the shell and r is the distance from the center of the shell.

concepts.concept_title: Potential Inside a Uniformly Charged Spherical Shell
concepts.description: The potential inside a uniformly charged spherical shell is constant and equal to the value at the surface, given by V = 1/4πε0 × q / R, where q is the total charge on the shell and R is its radius.

concepts.concept_title: Zero Potential on the Line Joining Two Charges
concepts.description: The electric potential is zero at certain points on the line joining two charges, such as the point where the distance from each charge is equal or the point where the potential due to one charge is equal in magnitude but opposite in sign to the potential due to the other charge.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Potential Difference Between Two Points
concepts.description: The electric potential difference between two points is the change in electric potential energy per unit charge. It's a scalar quantity and can be positive, negative, or zero.

The sign of potential difference depends on the relative values of the electric potentials at two points. A positive potential difference indicates that the electric potential at the second point is higher than at the first point.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done by Electric Field on a Charge
concepts.description: The work done by an electric field on a charge depends on the electric field and the direction of the charge. A positive charge experiences a force opposite to the electric field, resulting in positive work done on the field, while a negative charge experiences a force in the direction of the electric field, resulting in negative work done on the field.

concepts.concept_title: Potential Energy of a Charge Between Two Points
concepts.description: The potential energy of a charge between two points is the energy associated with the electric field and the charge. A charge moves from a point of higher potential energy to a point of lower potential energy, resulting in a decrease in potential energy.

concepts.concept_title: Equipotential Surfaces of a Single Point Charge
concepts.description: The equipotential surfaces of a single point charge are concentric spherical surfaces centered at the charge. These surfaces have a constant value of electric potential at all points on the surface.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field and Equipotential Surface Relationship
concepts.description: The electric field at any point is normal to the equipotential surface passing through that point. This means that the electric field direction and the equipotential surface are perpendicular to each other.
A surface where every point has the same electric potential. Electric field lines are normal to these surfaces.
Electric field direction is normal to equipotential surfaces. Electric field magnitude equals the negative rate of change of potential with perpendicular distance.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done in Moving a Charge
concepts.description: The work done in moving a unit positive charge against the electric field is given by the difference in electric potential. Equivalently, the magnitude of the electric field times the perpendicular distance.
Calculating the work done in building up a configuration of charges gives the electrostatic potential energy of the system.
Potential at a point due to a point charge q is given by V = 1/4πε₀ r where r is the distance from the charge to the point.
Potential decreases steepest in the direction of the electric field. Its magnitude equals the change in potential per unit displacement normal to the equipotential surface.

concepts.concept_title: Equipotential Surfaces for Uniform Electric Field
concepts.description: For a uniform electric field, equipotential surfaces are planes normal to the direction of the field.

concepts.concept_title: Potential Energy of a System of Two Charges
concepts.description: The potential energy of a system of two charges is directly proportional to the product of the charges and inversely to the distance between them.

concepts.concept_title: Path Independence of Work for Electrostatic Force
concepts.description: Work done in assembling charges is path-independent due to the conservative nature of electrostatic force.

concepts.concept_title: Sign of Potential Energy for Like and Unlike Charges
concepts.description: Potential energy is positive for like charges and negative for unlike charges.

concepts.concept_title: Potential Energy of a System of Multiple Charges
concepts.description: The potential energy of a system of multiple charges is the sum of the potential energies of pairs of charges.

concepts.concept_title: Expression for Potential Energy of a System of Three Charges
concepts.description: The potential energy of a system of three charges is given by a specific expression that includes the charges and their distances.
The work done in moving charges depends only on the final arrangement of the charges, not on the order they are assembled.

concepts.concept_title: Electrostatic Potential Energy of a Single Charge
concepts.description: The potential energy of a charge q in a given electric field is equal to the charge multiplied by the electrostatic potential at the point.

concepts.concept_title: Electrostatic Potential Energy in an External Field
concepts.description: A charge q in an electric field has a potential energy equal to the charge multiplied by the electrostatic potential at the point.

concepts.concept_title: External Potential Energy of a Charge
concepts.description: Potential energy of a charge in an external electric field is given by the work done in bringing the charge from infinity to the point. External electric field and corresponding potential V vary from point to point.

concepts.concept_title: Potential Energy of a Charge in an External Field
concepts.description: Potential energy of a charge q at a point P in an external field is given by qV(r), where V(r) is the external potential at the point P.

concepts.concept_title: Electron Volt (eV) as a Unit of Energy
concepts.description: 1 electron volt (eV) is defined as the energy gained by an electron with charge q = e = 1.6×10–19 C when accelerated by a potential difference of 1 volt.

concepts.concept_title: Potential Energy of a System of Two Charges in an External Field
concepts.description: The potential energy of a system of two charges q1 and q2 located at r1 and r2, respectively, in an external field is the sum of the potential energy of each charge individually in the external field.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done on Charge Against External Field
concepts.description: Work done on a charge against an external electric field is given by W = qV(r) + 1/2 * (q*q)/4πε0 * (1/r1 + 1/r2).

concepts.concept_title: Potential Energy of a System of Charges
concepts.description: Potential energy of a system of charges is the total work done in assembling the configuration and is given by U = q1V(r1) + q2V(r2) + 1/2 * (q1*q2)/4πε0 * (1/r1 + 1/r2).
Work required to separate two charges infinitely away from each other is given by W = U - Ui, where U is the final potential energy and Ui is the initial potential energy.
A dipole experiences no net force but experiences a torque ttttt given by τ = p × E in a uniform electric field.
Potential energy of a dipole in a uniform external field is given by U = -pE cos(θ), where θ is the inclination of the dipole.
The potential energy expression for a dipole is given by U(q) = –p.E.
The potential difference between two points is equal to the work done in moving a unit positive charge from one point to the other against the electric field.

concepts.concept_title: Electrical Work Done and Potential Energy
concepts.description: The work done against an external electric field in moving a dipole is equal to the change in its potential energy.
Polarization of a substance is the aligning of its permanent electric dipole moment in the direction of an external electric field.

concepts.concept_title: Heat Released by a Substance in Aligning its Dipoles
concepts.description: The heat released by a substance in aligning its dipoles along the direction of an external electric field is equal to the change in its potential energy.
Conductors contain mobile charge carriers, while insulators do not. Conductors have free electrons, which are not free to leave the conductor.
In metallic conductors, the charge carriers are free electrons, while in electrolytic conductors, they are both positive and negative ions.
Inside a conductor, the electrostatic field is zero due to free electrons distributing themselves to cancel out any electric field.
At the surface of a charged conductor, the electrostatic field is normal to the surface, with no tangential component.

concepts.concept_title: Excess Charge Resides on Conductor Surface
concepts.description: In the static situation, excess charge on a conductor resides on the surface, with no net charge inside the conductor.
Electrostatic potential is constant throughout the volume of a conductor.
In a system of conductors, each conductor is characterized by a constant value of potential, which may differ from one conductor to another.

concepts.concept_title: Gauss's Law for Flux through Pill Box
concepts.description: The total flux through the pill box is equal to ± EdS for s > 0 or s < 0.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field at Surface of Charged Conductor
concepts.description: The electric field at the surface of a charged conductor is normal to the surface with magnitude E = e nˆ / s.

concepts.concept_title: Equation for Electric Field at Surface
concepts.description: The equation for electric field at the surface is E = (2.35) where s is the surface charge density and nˆ is a unit vector normal to the surface.
The electric field inside a cavity of a conductor is zero, and all charges reside only on the outer surface of the conductor.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field Inside Cavity of Conductor is Zero
concepts.description: The electric field inside a cavity of a conductor is zero, regardless of the size, shape, charge, and external fields.

concepts.concept_title: All Charges Reside on Outer Surface
concepts.description: When a conductor is charged or charges are induced on a conductor by an external field, all charges reside only on the outer surface of the conductor.
When two different materials come into contact and move, they transfer electrons and become charged due to friction.
An external electric field induces dipole moment in polar or non-polar molecules, resulting in a collective effect of net charges on the surface of the dielectric.

concepts.concept_title: Net Electrostatic Field in Dielectrics and Conductors
concepts.description: In a dielectric, the external electric field induces a net electrostatic field that opposes the external field, reducing its intensity. In a conductor, the free movement of charges cancels the external electric field.

Dielectrics have no free charge carriers and their polarisation opposes the external electric field, whereas conductors have free charge carriers that move and adjust the charge distribution to cancel the external electric field.
Molecules can have a permanent or intrinsic dipole moment, and they can also be induced to have a dipole moment when exposed to an external electric field.
A polar molecule has separated centers of positive and negative charges even in the absence of an external field. It has a permanent dipole moment.
A non-polar molecule has its positive and negative charges equally distributed and does not have a permanent dipole moment.
In an external electric field, a non-polar molecule becomes polarized and develops an induced dipole moment.
Polarisation is the net dipole moment per unit volume of a dielectric in the presence of an external electric field.
Electric susceptibility is a constant characteristic of a dielectric that represents its ability to become polarized in an external electric field.

concepts.concept_title: Relation Between Polarisation and Electric Field
concepts.description: For linear isotropic dielectrics, the polarisation (P) is directly proportional to the strength of the external electric field (E), given by the equation P = ecE.
Dielectric polarisation is the process by which a dielectric material becomes polarised due to an external electric field, resulting in the alignment of molecular dipoles.
Induced charges are the charges that appear on the surface of a dielectric material due to an external electric field, resulting in a reduction of the total electric field.
A polarised dielectric is equivalent to two charged surfaces with induced surface charge densities, resulting in a reduction of the total electric field.
Surface charge density is the charge per unit area on the surface of a dielectric material due to an external electric field.
A capacitor is a system of two conductors separated by an insulator, with charges on the conductors that result in an electric field between them.
The electric field in the region between conductors of a capacitor is proportional to the charge, while the potential difference is the work done per unit positive charge against the electric field.
Capacitance is the ratio of the charge on a capacitor to its potential difference, and is a measure of the ability of a capacitor to store charge.
The SI unit of capacitance is 1 farad, denoted by 'F', equal to 1 coulomb volt-1.

concepts.concept_title: Capacitor with Fixed and Variable Capacitance
concepts.description: A capacitor with fixed capacitance is represented as ---||---, while a capacitor with variable capacitance is represented as .

concepts.concept_title: Relationship between Charge, Voltage, and Capacitance
concepts.description: For a capacitor with large capacitance, a given charge can be held at a relatively small voltage, implying a practical importance.
A capacitor has a limit to the amount of charge it can store without significant leaking, which is determined by its capacitance and the electric field it can withstand.
The maximum electric field a dielectric medium can withstand without break-down is called its dielectric strength.
A parallel plate capacitor consists of two large plane parallel conducting plates separated by a small distance, and is represented in figure 2.25.

concepts.concept_title: Surface Charge Density and Electric Field
concepts.description: The surface charge density of a plate is given by Q/A, where Q is the charge on the plate and A is its area. The electric field due to an infinite plane sheet of uniform surface charge density is given by E = 2 ρ 0 ρ / 2.
The electric field between two parallel plates is uniform and directed from the positive to the negative plate.
The direction of electric field lines is from the positive to the negative plate.
Near the outer boundaries of the plates, the electric field lines bend outward due to the 'fringing of the field'.

concepts.concept_title: Uniform Electric Field and Potential Difference
concepts.description: For a uniform electric field, the potential difference is the electric field times the distance between the plates.

concepts.concept_title: Capacitance of a Parallel Plate Capacitor
concepts.description: The capacitance C of a parallel plate capacitor is given by Q/eA.
The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor is modified by the presence of a dielectric, and is given by Q/KsA.
K is a constant characteristic of the dielectric, and it is a measure of how much dielectric constant of a dielectric material changes under the influence of external field.
The capacitance C of a capacitor with dielectric between the plates is given by Q / V = (ε A e^(K-1)) / d.
The dielectric constant K of a substance is the factor by which the capacitance increases from its vacuum value.

concepts.concept_title: Formula for capacitance increase due to dielectric
concepts.description: The capacitance of a capacitor increases by a factor of K + 1 for a dielectric with dielectric constant K.
When capacitors are connected in series, the charge on each capacitor is the same but the potential drop across each capacitor is different.
The charges on the two plates of each capacitor in a series combination are (±Q) and are the same on each capacitor.
The total potential drop across a series combination of capacitors is the sum of the individual potential drops across each capacitor.
The effective capacitance of a series combination of capacitors is 1 / Σ (1 / C) where C are the individual capacitances.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship between electric field and capacitance
concepts.description: When a dielectric is inserted between the plates of a capacitor, the electric field in the dielectric decreases by a factor of K.

concepts.concept_title: Capacitance formula for slab of dielectric
concepts.description: The capacitance of a capacitor with a slab of dielectric of thickness (3/4)d between the plates is given by C = C / (K + 1)^(1/3) where C is the vacuum capacitance.

When capacitors are connected in series, the charge on each capacitor is the same, but the potential difference across each capacitor is different. The total potential difference is the sum of the potential differences across each capacitor.
The equivalent capacitance of a series combination of capacitors is given by the formula 1/C ≡ 1/C1 + 1/C2 + … + 1/Cn.
The general formula for the potential difference across a series combination of n capacitors is V = V1 + V2 + … + Vn = Q/C1 + Q/C2 + … + Q/Cn.

concepts.concept_title: General Formula for Equivalent Capacitance in Series
concepts.description: The general formula for the equivalent capacitance of a series combination of n capacitors is 1/C ≡ 1/C1 + 1/C2 + … + 1/Cn.
When capacitors are connected in parallel, the potential difference across each capacitor is the same, but the charge on each capacitor is different.
The equivalent capacitance of a parallel combination of capacitors is given by the formula C ≡ C1 + C2 + … + Cn.
The general formula for the charge on a parallel combination of n capacitors is Q = Q1 + Q2 + … + Qn.

concepts.concept_title: General Formula for Equivalent Capacitance in Parallel
concepts.description: The general formula for the equivalent capacitance of a parallel combination of n capacitors is C ≡ C1 + C2 + … + Cn.

concepts.concept_title: Electrostatic energy stored in a capacitor
concepts.description: The electrostatic energy stored by a capacitor is given by U = (1/2) CV^2, where C is the capacitance and V is the potential difference across the capacitor.

concepts.concept_title: Electrostatic energy stored in multiple capacitors
concepts.description: When two or more capacitors are connected, the total electrostatic energy is the sum of the energies stored by each capacitor.
Work done by an external force in bringing a charge from a point R to a point P is equal to the difference in potential energy of the charge between the final and initial points.

concepts.concept_title: Definition of potential at a point
concepts.description: Potential at a point is the work done per unit charge by an external agency in bringing a charge from infinity to that point.
The potential at a point is arbitrary to within an additive constant, since only potential differences are physically significant.

concepts.concept_title: Potential due to a point charge
concepts.description: The potential at a point due to a point charge is given by V(r) = kQ/r, where k is Coulomb's constant and r is the distance from the charge.
The potential at a point due to a dipole is given by V(r) = k · rˆ/4πεr^2, where p is the dipole moment and r is the distance from the dipole.
The potential at a point due to a charge configuration is given by the superposition principle, which states that the potential is the sum of the potentials due to each charge.
An equipotential surface is a surface over which the potential has a constant value.
The electric field at a point is perpendicular to the equipotential surface through the point and is in the direction of the steepest decrease of potential.
The potential energy of two charges q1 and q2 separated by a distance r is given by U = k * q1 * q2 / r where k is Coulomb's constant.

concepts.concept_title: Potential Energy of a Charge in an External Potential
concepts.description: The potential energy of a charge q in an external potential V(r) is qV(r), where V(r) is the potential at a distance r from the charge.

concepts.concept_title: Potential Energy of a Dipole Moment in an Electric Field
concepts.description: The potential energy of a dipole moment p in a uniform electric field E is -p.E, where E is the electric field strength.
