The electric field E is zero in the interior of a conductor and normal to the surface of a charged conductor, given by E = σ/n0 where σ is the surface charge density and n0 is the unit vector along the outward normal.
The potential is constant within and on the surface of a conductor.
The capacity of a capacitor is given by C = Q/V, where Q is the charge on the conductor and V is the potential difference between the conductors.
The unit of capacitance is farad (F), where 1 F = 1 C/V.

concepts.concept_title: Capacitance of a Parallel Plate Capacitor
concepts.description: The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor is given by C = A/ε0d, where A is the area of each plate and d is the separation between them.
The capacitance of a capacitor increases when filled with a dielectric, given by C = KC0, where K is the dielectric constant.

concepts.concept_title: Capacitance in Series and Parallel Combinations
concepts.description: For capacitors in series, 1/Ctot = 1/C1 + 1/C2 + 1/C3; for capacitors in parallel, Ctot = C1 + C2 + C3.
The energy stored in a capacitor is given by U = QV = CV2/2.
The electric energy density is given by (1/2)εE2, where ε is the permittivity of the medium and E is the electric field strength.
A force that opposes the net Coulomb force on a charge.

concepts.concept_title: Confined Electric Field within Small Region
concepts.description: A capacitor designed to confine electric field lines within a small region, resulting in a small potential difference between conductors.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field across Spherical Charged Shell
concepts.description: The electric field is discontinuous across the surface of a spherical charged shell, being zero inside and nonzero outside.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Potential across Spherical Charged Shell
concepts.description: The electric potential is continuous across the surface of a spherical charged shell, taking the value of q/4πεR at the surface.
The torque on a dipole causes it to oscillate about E, with the dipole eventually aligning with E if a dissipative mechanism is present.
The potential energy expression qV(r) cannot include the potential due to the charge q itself, which is undefined.
A cavity inside a conductor is shielded from external electrical influences, but not from the fields generated by charges within the conductor.

concepts.concept_title: Zero Electric Potential along Line of Charges
concepts.description: There exist points along the line joining two charges where the electric potential is zero.

concepts.concept_title: Potential at the Centre of a Regular Hexagon
concepts.description: The potential at the centre of a regular hexagon with charges at its vertices is nonzero and can be calculated.

concepts.concept_title: Equipotential Surface and Electric Field Direction
concepts.description: An equipotential surface of a charge distribution has a well-defined direction of electric field at every point.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field inside and outside a Spherical Conductor
concepts.description: The electric field inside a spherical conductor is zero, while just outside the sphere, the electric field is given by q/4πεR.

concepts.concept_title: Capacitance of a Parallel Plate Capacitor
concepts.description: The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor depends on the distance between the plates, the dielectric constant, and the plate area.
When capacitors are connected in series, the total capacitance is given by the reciprocal of the sum of the reciprocals of the individual capacitances.
The potential difference across each capacitor in a series combination is proportional to the capacitance.

concepts.concept_title: Capacitance of a Parallel Plate Capacitor with Dielectric
concepts.description: When a dielectric material is placed between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor, its capacitance increases by a factor of the dielectric constant.

concepts.concept_title: Equivalent Capacitance and Charge on Parallel Plate Capacitors
concepts.description: In a parallel combination of capacitors, the total capacitance is the sum of the individual capacitances, and the charge on each capacitor is proportional to its capacitance.

concepts.concept_title: Capacitance of a Parallel Plate Capacitor with Reduced Distance and Dielectric
concepts.description: When the distance between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor is reduced and a dielectric material is introduced, its capacitance increases.
key_terms: parallel plate capacitor with reduced distance and dielectric

concepts.description: Capacitance (C) of a capacitor is calculated using the formula: C = ε0A/d, where ε0 is the permittivity of free space, A is the plate area, and d is the distance between the plates.

concepts.concept_title: Capacitance of a Parallel Plate Capacitor
concepts.description: For a parallel plate capacitor, the capacitance (C) is given by the formula: C = ε0A/d, where ε0 is the permittivity of free space, A is the plate area, and d is the distance between the plates.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Dielectric Constant on Capacitance
concepts.description: The capacitance of a capacitor increases with the increase in dielectric constant (K).

concepts.concept_title: Charging of a Capacitor with a Battery
concepts.description: When a capacitor is connected to a battery, the charge (Q) stored on each plate is given by Q = CV, where C is the capacitance and V is the potential difference across the capacitor.

concepts.concept_title: Electrostatic Energy Stored in a Capacitor
concepts.description: The electrostatic energy (U) stored in a capacitor is given by the formula: U = (1/2)CV^2, where C is the capacitance and V is the potential difference across the capacitor.
Electric current is defined as the net amount of positive charge flowing in a given time interval across a small area held normal to the direction of flow of charges.
The electric current is mathematically expressed as the quotient of the net amount of charge flowing across a cross-sectional area in a time interval and the duration of the time interval.
The SI unit of electric current is defined as the order of magnitude of currents in domestic appliances and is typically measured in amperes.
The equation I = (ΔQ / Δt) can be rearranged to express the net charge (ΔQ) flowing across a cross-sectional area of a conductor in a time interval (Δt) as ΔQ = IΔt.

concepts.concept_title: Current as a Function of Time
concepts.description: The current can be expressed as a function of time (I(t)) as the limit of the ratio of the net charge (ΔQ) flowing across a cross-sectional area of a conductor in a time interval (Δt) to the duration of the time interval (Δt).
The direction of electric current is opposite to the direction of flow of positive charges.
In some materials, all electrons are bound, while in others, some are free to move within the bulk material.
In solid conductors, electric current is carried by negatively charged electrons, while in electrolytic solutions, both positive and negative charges can move.
At a given time, there is no preferential direction for the velocities of electrons, resulting in no net electric current in the absence of an electric field.
An electric field causes electrons to accelerate and move towards the positive charge, resulting in electric current.
A steady electric field and continuous replenishment of charges result in a continuous electric current.
Resistivity (r) is a property of the material of a conductor, not its dimensions, and it measures opposition to electric current flow.
Ohm's Law states that the potential difference (V) across a conductor is equal to the product of the current (I) and resistance (R), given by V = IR.
Current density (j) is the current per unit area of a conductor and is given by j = I/A, where I is the current and A is the cross-sectional area.
The electric field (E) and potential difference (V) across a conductor are related by the equation E = j r, where j is the current density and r is the resistivity.
Conductivity (s) is the reciprocal of resistivity and is given by s = 1/r.
The drift velocity of electrons is zero in the absence of an electric field, but it is given by the equation v = a t, where a is the acceleration and t is the time elapsed.
Electrons are accelerated by an electric field given by the equation a = -e E/m, where e is the charge, E is the electric field, and m is the mass of an electron.
The average velocity of electrons is the average of all the velocities obtained by integrating the acceleration over a time interval.
The average time between successive collisions is known as relaxation time. Despite electrons being accelerated, their average velocity is independent of time, leading to drift velocity.
Current density is defined as the charge transported across a unit area per unit time. It is inversely proportional to drift velocity and proportional to charge density.

Ohm's law states that current density is proportional to electric field and conductivity. Conductivity is directly proportional to the density of free electrons and inversely proportional to relaxation time.
Drift speed of conduction electrons can be estimated using the relaxation time and charge density of the material.
The drift speed of conduction electrons is given by v = (I/neA), where e is the charge of an electron, n is the density of conduction electrons, A is the cross-sectional area, and I is the current.
The density of conduction electrons is equal to the number of atoms per cubic meter, assuming one conduction electron per copper atom.
The thermal speed of a copper atom of mass M at temperature T is typically of the order of kT/M, where k is the Boltzmann constant.
The speed of an electric field along a conductor is equal to the speed of an electromagnetic wave, which is 3.0 x 10^8 m/s.
Electric fields establish throughout the circuit almost instantly, causing local forces that result in electron drift.
Electrons acquire a steady average drift speed due to the balance between the force experienced by electrons in the electric field and the collisions with positive ions.
A large current can be obtained in a conductor despite the small drift speed of electrons due to the large number of electrons available.
When electrons drift from lower to higher potential, it does not mean that all 'free' electrons are moving in the same direction.

In the absence of an electric field, the paths of electrons are not straight lines between successive collisions. In the presence of an electric field, the paths of electrons can deviate due to the force exerted by the electric field.
The drift velocity of electrons in a conductor is a result of electric field and results in electron movement.
Electrons in a conductor collide with positive ions, which slows down their speed and results in a drift speed.
The number density of electrons in a conductor is extremely high, approximately 10^29 m^-3.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship between Drift Velocity and Electric Field
concepts.description: The drift velocity of electrons is directly proportional to the electric field, with the proportionality constant being mobility.
Mobility is defined as the magnitude of drift velocity per unit electric field.
The average collision time is inversely proportional to the mobility of electrons.
There are materials that do not obey Ohm's law, including semi-conductors and certain types of conductors.

concepts.concept_title: Classification of Materials Based on Resistivity
concepts.description: Materials are classified as conductors, semiconductors, and insulators based on their resistivities.
Metals have low resistivities in the range of 10^-8 Wm to 10^-6 Wm.
Insulators like ceramic, rubber, and plastics have resistivities 10^18 times greater than metals.

concepts.concept_title: Temperature Dependence of Resistivity of Metals
concepts.description: The resistivity of metals is approximately given by the equation r = r_0 [1 + a (T - T_0)] over a limited range of temperatures.
The dimension of temperature co-efficient of resistivity (a) is (Temperature)^-1.

concepts.concept_title: Graph of Resistivity vs Temperature for Metals
concepts.description: A graph of resistivity plotted against temperature for metals would be a straight line over a limited range of temperatures.
Some materials like Nichrome exhibit a very weak dependence of resistivity with temperature.

concepts.concept_title: Temperature Dependence of Resistivity of Semiconductors
concepts.description: The resistivities of semiconductors decrease with increasing temperatures.

concepts.concept_title: Equation for Resistivity of a Material
concepts.description: The resistivity of a material is given by r = ne^2 t / m.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Temperature on Average Time of Collisions
concepts.description: The average time of collisions t decreases with temperature.
Resistance increases with temperature for metals, but decreases for insulators and semiconductors.

concepts.concept_title: Resistance of Nichrome at High Temperature
concepts.description: Nichrome's resistance increases with temperature, and its temperature coefficient of resistance is 1.70 × 10–4 °C–1.
The steady temperature of a heating element is reached when the heating effect of the current equals the heat loss to the surroundings.
A platinum resistance thermometer works based on the variation of platinum's resistance with temperature.
The potential difference across a conductor is the difference between the electric potential at its ends.

concepts.concept_title: Change in Potential Energy of Charge
concepts.description: When a charge moves from one point to another, its potential energy changes, and the change is equal to the negative product of the charge and the potential difference.
Total energy of a charge remains constant; only its form changes. This implies a relationship between kinetic energy and potential energy.
A relationship between changes in kinetic energy and potential energy, reflecting conservation of energy.
The power lost in a resistor is directly proportional to the square of the current through it; it also depends on the resistance.

concepts.concept_title: I2 R Loss in a Conductor
concepts.description: The power wasted in connecting wires is inversely proportional to the square of the voltage applied.
An external source supplies power to maintain a steady current through a conductor. Power losses occur in the resistance of connecting wires and devices.
Power loss in transmission lines is inversely proportional to the square of the applied voltage.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship Between Voltage, Current, and Power
concepts.description: The power delivered to a device depends on the voltage and current through it, according to the equations P = VI and P = V^2/R.
High voltage danger due to enormous current values in long-distance transmission lines.
Transformer is a device that lowers high voltage to a safe value suitable for use.
An electrolytic cell has two electrodes (positive and negative) immersed in an electrolyte solution, maintaining a steady current by exchanging charges.
emf is the potential difference between the positive and negative electrodes in an open circuit, denoted by e = V + V.
Internal resistance is the finite resistance of the electrolyte through which a current flows.
The potential difference between two points in a circuit is the energy per unit charge required to move a charge from one point to another.
Electric current in a circuit is the flow of charge, which is zero if the internal resistance is infinite or the external resistance is infinite.
Kirchhoff's laws relate the potential differences and currents in a circuit, but are not explicitly mentioned in this chapter.
The sign convention for potential difference between two points is determined by the direction of current flow.
Internal resistance of cells can be neglected in practical calculations when the current is such that electro motive force (emf) is much greater than the product of current and internal resistance.
According to Ohm's law, the potential difference across a resistor is equal to the product of current and resistance, given by V = IR.
The maximum current that can be drawn from a cell is given by I = emf/internal resistance, but in most cells the maximum allowed current is much lower to prevent permanent damage.
In a series combination of cells, the equivalent emf is the sum of individual emf's and the internal resistance is the sum of individual internal resistances.
The equivalent emf of a series combination of n cells is the sum of their individual emf's, and the internal resistance is the sum of their individual internal resistances.

concepts.concept_title: Equivalent internal resistance of series combination of cells
concepts.description: When cells are connected in series, the equivalent internal resistance is the sum of their internal resistances, assuming current leaves each cell from the positive electrode.

concepts.concept_title: Combination of internal resistances in parallel combination
concepts.description: The combination of internal resistances in a parallel circuit is related to the inverse sum of internal resistances, assuming charge flows in and out equally.
The potential difference across terminals of a cell in a parallel combination is related to the electromotive force and current flowing through it.

concepts.concept_title: Equivalence of internal resistance in parallel combination
concepts.description: The internal resistance of a parallel combination of cells is given by the sum of inverse internal resistances, assuming cells are joined in parallel.

concepts.concept_title: Combined internal resistance in multi-cell parallel circuit
concepts.description: The combined internal resistance of a parallel circuit with multiple cells is given by the sum of inverse internal resistances, assuming the cells are connected in parallel.

concepts.concept_title: Equivalence of electromotive force in parallel combination
concepts.description: The electromotive force of a parallel combination of cells is the sum of electromotive forces of individual cells, assuming the cells are connected in parallel.
At any junction, the sum of currents entering the junction is equal to the sum of currents leaving the junction.
The algebraic sum of potential changes around any closed loop involving resistors and cells is zero.

concepts.concept_title: Determining Potential Difference across a Cell
concepts.description: Potential difference V = V (P) – V (N) = e – I r, where e is the electromotive force, I is the current, and r is the internal resistance.

concepts.concept_title: Determining Potential Difference when Current Flows from Negative Terminal to Positive Terminal
concepts.description: Potential difference V = e + I r, where e is the electromotive force, I is the current, and r is the internal resistance.

concepts.concept_title: Algebraic Sum of Changes in Potential around a Closed Loop
concepts.description: The algebraic sum of changes in potential around a closed loop is zero.
The total current entering a junction is equal to the total current leaving the junction.
A network with symmetrical paths and junctions can be reduced to a simpler equivalent circuit using Kirchhoff's rules.
The sum of the potential differences across the resistors in a closed loop is equal to the emf of the battery in that loop.

concepts.concept_title: Application of Kirchhoff's Rules to a Network
concepts.description: Kirchhoff's rules can be applied to a network by analyzing junctions and closed loops to determine the unknown currents and voltages.
A system of simultaneous equations can be formed from Kirchhoff's rules to solve for the unknown currents and voltages in a network.
Kirchhoff's second rule states that for any closed loop in a circuit, the sum of the voltage changes across all resistors in the loop is zero.
Kirchhoff's junction rule states that at any junction in a circuit, the sum of the currents entering the junction is equal to the sum of the currents leaving the junction.
A Wheatstone bridge is a type of electrical circuit that consists of four resistors and a galvanometer, used for measuring unknown resistances.
The balance condition for a Wheatstone bridge is given by R2/R1 = R3/R4, where R1, R2, R3, and R4 are the resistances of the four arms of the bridge.
A meter bridge is a practical device used for measuring unknown resistances, based on the principle of the Wheatstone bridge.
Null deflection in a Wheatstone bridge occurs when the galvanometer shows zero current, indicating that the bridge is balanced.
The current is defined as the net charge passing per unit time through a given area.

concepts.concept_title: Closed circuit for a steady current
concepts.description: A closed circuit is necessary for maintaining a steady current, requiring an external agency to move electric charge from lower to higher potential energy.
Electric current is proportional to the voltage across a substance, given by V = RI (V proportional to I) with resistance R.
The resistance of a conductor is given by R = ρ (l/A), where ρ is the resistivity, dependent on material, temperature, and pressure.
Metals have low resistivity, insulators have high resistivity, and semiconductors have resistivity in the middle range.
In most substances, electrons are the current carriers, but in some cases, positive and negative ions carry the electric current.
Current density is given by j = nq v (amount of charge flowing per second per unit area normal to the flow).

concepts.concept_title: Relationship between force, electric field, and drift velocity
concepts.description: The force on the electrons in a metal is proportional to the drift velocity due to the external electric field and the collisions with ions.
The average time interval between collisions is given by t = e^2t / (m * v), where a is the acceleration of the electron.
The temperature coefficient of resistivity is defined as the fractional increase in resistivity per unit increase in temperature.

Ohm's law is obeyed by many substances, but it fails when current depends non-linearly on voltage, or when the relation between voltage and current depends on the sign of voltage, or when the relation between voltage and current is non-unique.
At any junction of circuit elements, the sum of currents entering the junction must equal the sum of currents leaving it.
The algebraic sum of changes in potential around any closed loop must be zero.
The null-point condition is given by R1 / R2 = R3 / R4, using which the value of one resistance can be determined, knowing the other three resistances.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Current: Scalar vs Vector Nature
concepts.description: Current is a scalar although we represent current with an arrow, and currents do not obey the law of vector addition.

concepts.concept_title: Definition of Resistance and Resistance Formula
concepts.description: Resistance is defined as R = V/I, and it may be applied to all conducting devices whether they obey Ohm's law or not.
A conducting material obeys Ohm's law when the resistivity is independent of the electric field.

concepts.concept_title: No current density relation application to total current
concepts.description: The relation j = ρ v does not apply to the total current charge density.

concepts.concept_title: Conductivity and resistivity in homogeneous and inhomogeneous conductors
concepts.description: Conductivity and resistivity properties of homogeneous and inhomogeneous conductors like silver, semiconductors, and impurity-doped semiconductors.

concepts.concept_title: Ohm's law deviation due to strong electric field
concepts.description: Departures from Ohm's law for homogeneous conductors and semiconductors under strong electric fields.

concepts.concept_title: Electric current due to charge carriers in a conductor
concepts.description: Motion of conduction electrons in an electric field E and the contribution of charge carriers to the current.

concepts.concept_title: Kirchhoff's junction rule for conservation of charge
concepts.description: Conservation of charge at a junction based on Kirchhoff's junction rule and application to circuit analysis.
Properties and operation of a storage battery, including emf, internal resistance, and maximum current.

concepts.concept_title: Terminal voltage and internal resistance in a circuit
concepts.description: Relationship between terminal voltage, internal resistance, and current in a circuit with a battery and a resistor.

concepts.concept_title: Temperature coefficient of resistivity and resistivity
concepts.description: Temperature dependence of resistivity and its application to resistors, heating elements, and nichrome.
Relationship between heat transfer and resistivity of a material, including nichrome and silver.
Division of current in a multi-branch circuit using Kirchhoff's junction rule and circuit analysis.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic field due to a straight long current-carrying wire
concepts.description: The magnetic field due to a straight current-carrying wire is tangential to an imaginary circle with the wire as its centre, perpendicular to the plane of the wire.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of reversing the direction of current on the magnetic field orientation
concepts.description: Reversing the direction of the current in a wire reverses the orientation of the magnetic field, with the north pole becoming south and vice versa.

concepts.concept_title: Influence of current strength and distance on magnetic field deflection
concepts.description: Increasing the current strength or decreasing the distance between the wire and the compass needle increases the deflection of the magnetic field.

concepts.concept_title: Arrangement of iron filings around a current-carrying wire
concepts.description: Iron filings around a current-carrying wire arrange themselves in concentric circles with the wire as the centre.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic field produced by a moving charge
concepts.description: A moving charge or current produces a magnetic field in the surrounding space.
The unification of the laws of electricity and magnetism was formulated by James Maxwell in 1864.
Electromagnetic waves were discovered by Hertz, and produced by J.C.Bose and G. Marconi in the 19th century.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic field exerting forces on moving charged particles
concepts.description: A magnetic field exerts forces on moving charged particles, like electrons, protons, and current-carrying wires.

concepts.concept_title: Electric Field due to a Point Charge
concepts.description: The electric field E due to a point charge Q is given by E = Q rˆ / (4πε0)r2, where rˆ is a unit vector along r.

concepts.concept_title: Force on a Charge due to an Electric Field
concepts.description: The force F on a charge q due to an electric field E is given by F = qE = q Q rˆ / (4πε0)r2.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field due to a Current-Carrying Wire
concepts.description: A current-carrying wire produces a magnetic field that deflects a compass needle.

concepts.concept_title: Principle of Superposition of Electric Fields
concepts.description: The electric field at a point due to multiple charges is the vector sum of the electric fields due to each charge.
The Lorentz force F on a charge q due to both electric and magnetic fields is given by F = q [ E (r) + v × B (r)].

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Force Depends On Charge, Velocity, and Magnetic Field
concepts.description: The magnetic force depends on the charge of the particle, its velocity, and the magnetic field. The force on a negative charge is opposite to that on a positive charge.

concepts.concept_title: Magnitude of Magnetic Force Depends on Vector Product of Velocity and Magnetic Field
concepts.description: The magnitude of the magnetic force depends on the vector product of the velocity and the magnetic field, resulting in a force perpendicular to both.
key_terms: perpendicular to both velocity and magnetic field

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Force Zero When Velocity or Magnetic Field is Zero or Parallel/Anti-Parallel
concepts.description: The magnetic force is zero if the velocity or magnetic field is zero, or if they are parallel/anti-parallel, resulting in no force or force cancellation.

concepts.concept_title: Screw Rule and Right Hand Rule for Determining Force Direction
concepts.description: The screw rule or right hand rule is used to determine the direction of the magnetic force, which is perpendicular to both the velocity and magnetic field.

concepts.concept_title: Unit of Magnetic Field Defined Using Magnetic Force
concepts.description: The unit of the magnetic field, tesla (T), is defined using the magnetic force on a unit charge, and is dimensionally equivalent to newton second per coulomb meter.

concepts.concept_title: Magnitude of Magnetic Field Determined by Force, Charge, and Velocity
concepts.description: The magnitude of the magnetic field can be determined from the magnetic force, charge, and velocity, using the equation [B] = [F/qv].
The average drift velocity of mobile carriers in a conducting rod is assumed to be v.

concepts.concept_title: Lorentz Force on a Moving Charge
concepts.description: The force on a moving charge in a magnetic field is given by F = q v × B, where q is the charge, v is the velocity, and B is the magnetic field.
The current density j can be expressed as j = nq v, where n is the number density of carriers, q is the charge, and v is the drift velocity.
The force on a current-carrying wire in a magnetic field is given by F = I l × B, where I is the current, l is the length of the wire, and B is the magnetic field.

The direction of the Lorentz force on a moving charge can be determined using the right-hand thumb rule, which states that the thumb points in the direction of the velocity and the fingers point in the direction of the magnetic field.

concepts.concept_title: Equation of Motion of a Charge in a Magnetic Field
concepts.description: The equation of motion of a charge in a magnetic field can be derived using the Lorentz force equation, and it shows that no work is done by the magnetic field on the charge.

concepts.concept_title: Relation Between Perpendicular Force and Circular Motion
concepts.description: A force parallel to the velocity can change the magnitude of the velocity but not its direction. When a charged particle moves perpendicular to a magnetic field, the magnetic force acts as a centripetal force.

concepts.concept_title: Expression for Radius of a Circular Orbit
concepts.description: The radius of a circular orbit of a charged particle in a magnetic field is given by r = mv / (qB), where m is the mass, v is the velocity, q is the charge, and B is the magnetic field strength.

concepts.concept_title: Expression for Angular Frequency and Frequency
concepts.description: The angular frequency of a charged particle in a magnetic field is ω = qB / m, and the frequency is n = ω / (2π) = qB / (2πm), which is independent of the particle's velocity or energy.

concepts.concept_title: Expression for Time of One Revolution
concepts.description: The time taken for one revolution of a charged particle in a magnetic field is T = 1 / n = 2π / ω = 2πm / (qB).

concepts.concept_title: Expression for Pitch of a Helical Orbit
concepts.description: The distance moved along the magnetic field in one rotation is called the pitch p, and is given by p = 2πmv / (qB).

concepts.concept_title: Kinetic Energy of a Charged Particle
concepts.description: The kinetic energy of a charged particle in a magnetic field is given by E = (½)mv^2, where m is the mass and v is the velocity.
Biot-Savart's law relates the magnetic field produced by a current-carrying conductor to the current, element length, and distance.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field due to Infinitesimal Element
concepts.description: The magnetic field dB at a point P due to an infinitesimal element of the conductor is perpendicular to the plane containing the element and displacement vector r.
The vector notation of Biot-Savart's law is given by μ Idl × r = d B, where μ is a constant of proportionality.
The magnitude of the magnetic field dB is proportional to the current I, element length |dl|, and inversely proportional to the square of the distance r.

concepts.concept_title: Constant of Proportionality (permeability of free space)
concepts.description: The constant of proportionality in Biot-Savart's law is μ₀, the permeability of free space, which is equal to 10⁻⁷ T m/A.

concepts.concept_title: Similarities and Differences between Biot-Savart and Coulomb's Laws
concepts.description: Both Biot-Savart and Coulomb's laws are long-range and follow the principle of superposition, but differ in the nature of their sources and the geometry of the resulting fields.
The magnetic field dB due to the current element I dl varies with the angle θ between dl and r.
The Biot-Savart law relates the magnetic field dB due to an infinitesimal current element (I dl) to the displacement vector r and the magnetic permeability of free space (µ).

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field Due to a Circular Loop
concepts.description: The magnetic field at a point on the axis of a circular loop is determined by integrating the contributions of infinitesimal current elements (I dl).
The product of vacuum permittivity (ε0) and vacuum permeability (µ0) is equal to the square of the speed of light in vacuum (c), i.e., ε0 µ0 = c^2.
The cross product of two vectors returns a vector perpendicular to both, and the magnitude of the cross product is equal to the product of the magnitudes and the sine of the angle between them.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic field due to a circular loop
concepts.description: The magnetic field at a point due to a circular loop of current is given by the Biot-Savart law.

concepts.concept_title: Cancellation of perpendicular components in dB
concepts.description: When components of dB perpendicular to the x-axis are summed over, they cancel out, leaving only the x-component.

concepts.concept_title: Integration of dB to find net magnetic field
concepts.description: The net contribution of dB along the x-direction can be obtained by integrating dB cos θ over the loop.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic field lines due to a circular wire
concepts.description: The magnetic field lines due to a circular wire form closed loops and are given by the right-hand thumb rule.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic field due to a semicircular arc
concepts.description: The contribution to the magnetic field from a semicircular arc is similar to that of a circular loop, but with a magnitude that is half of the circular loop.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic field due to a tightly wound coil
concepts.description: The magnetic field at the center of a tightly wound coil is given by the sum of the magnetic fields due to each circular element.

The Biot-Savart law describes the magnetic field produced by a current-carrying wire. It states that the magnetic field dB is proportional to the current I, length element dl, and the sine of the angle between the wire and the radial vector.

Ampere's circuital law relates the magnetic field to the total current passing through a closed loop. It states that the line integral of the magnetic field around a closed loop is proportional to the current passing through the loop.
An alternative form of Ampere's law uses the line integral of the magnetic field over a closed loop, which simplifies to BL = μI for a loop where B is tangential.

The sign convention for Ampere's law states that the direction of the current is determined by the right-hand rule when curling the fingers of the right hand in the sense the boundary is traversed in the loop integral.
The equation BL = μI gives the magnetic field at a point on a loop where the magnetic field is tangential to the loop, and relates it to the current enclosed by the loop.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field of a Straight Infinite Wire
concepts.description: The magnetic field of a straight infinite wire is given by B = µ I / (2πr) at a distance r outside the wire.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field Due to a Straight Wire
concepts.description: The magnetic field at a distance from an infinitely long straight wire is directly proportional to the current and inversely proportional to the distance.
The magnetic field around a straight wire has cylindrical symmetry, depending only on the distance from the wire.
The direction of the magnetic field due to a straight wire can be determined using the right-hand rule.

concepts.concept_title: Cylindrical Symmetry and Magnetic Field Lines
concepts.description: The magnetic field lines around a straight wire form concentric circles, showing cylindrical symmetry.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field Lines and Closed Loops
concepts.description: Magnetic field lines form closed loops, unlike electrostatic field lines which originate from positive charges and end at negative charges.

concepts.concept_title: Ampere's Circuital Law and Biot-Savart Law
concepts.description: Ampere's circuital law and Biot-Savart law relate the magnetic field and current, expressing the same physical consequences of a steady electrical current.
Enclosed current is a measure of the total current passing through a closed loop, often used to simplify calculations of magnetic fields.
An Amperian loop is a closed loop used to apply Ampere's circuital law to calculate the magnetic field of a current-carrying wire or coil.

concepts.concept_title: Ampere's Circuital Law for Long Straight Wire
concepts.description: Ampere's circuital law gives the magnetic field (B) around a long straight wire: B = μ₀I / 2πr.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field inside a Circular Wire
concepts.description: The magnetic field inside a circular wire (r < a) varies as B ∝ r, due to the current distribution being uniform.

concepts.concept_title: Ampere's Circuital Law for Circular Loop
concepts.description: Ampere's circuital law can be used to calculate the magnetic field of a circular loop, but it may not always facilitate an evaluation of the field.
The net magnetic field of a long solenoid is the vector sum of the fields due to all its turns, and is strong in the interior.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field of a Long Solenoid
concepts.description: The magnetic field of a very long solenoid is uniform and strong along its axis and becomes zero at the exterior points.
The magnetic field outside a long solenoid is weak and has no perpendicular or normal component.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field of a Long Circular Amperian Loop
concepts.description: A long circular Amperian loop has a magnetic field that is uniform along its axis and becomes zero at the points where the loop is perpendicular to the field.

concepts.concept_title: Ampere's Circuital Law for a Long Solenoid
concepts.description: Ampere's circuital law states that the magnetic field of a long solenoid is given by the formula B = μnI.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field inside a Long Solenoid
concepts.description: The magnetic field inside a long solenoid is uniform and given by the formula B = μnI.

concepts.concept_title: Force between Two Parallel Current-Carrying Conductors
concepts.description: Two parallel current-carrying conductors exert a magnetic force on each other, which depends on the magnitude of the currents, shape and size of the conductors, and the distance between them.
The force between two parallel conductors is given by F = (μ₀ I₁ I₂) / (2πd) and acts in the direction determined by the right-hand rule.
Oppositely directed currents repel each other, as per Newton's third law.

concepts.concept_title: Magnitude of force per unit length
concepts.description: The magnitude of the force per unit length is given by f = (μ₀ I₁ I₂) / (2πd), where μ₀ is the magnetic constant.
The ampere is the value of one coulomb per second, defined as a current that produces a force of 2 × 10–7 N/m on each of two parallel conductors.

concepts.concept_title: Newton's third law in relation to electromagnetism
concepts.description: Newton's third law may not hold for forces between charges and/or conductors in time-dependent situations, but momentum conservation still holds.

concepts.concept_title: Force on a Current Carrying Conductor
concepts.description: The force per unit length on a conductor is given by F = IlB sinθ, where I is the current, l is the length, B is the magnetic field, and θ is the angle between the current and the magnetic field.

concepts.concept_title: Direction of Force on a Current Carrying Conductor
concepts.description: The direction of force on a conductor is downward when the current flows from east to west and is zero when the current flows from south to north in a horizontal table.

concepts.concept_title: Torque on a Rectangular Current Loop
concepts.description: A rectangular current loop experiences a torque when placed in a uniform magnetic field, and the torque is given by τ = IAB, where I is the current, A is the area, and B is the magnetic field.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Moment of a Rectangular Current Loop
concepts.description: The magnetic moment of a rectangular current loop is given by m = IAB, where I is the current, A is the area, and B is the magnetic field.

concepts.concept_title: Torque on a Current Loop in a Uniform Magnetic Field
concepts.description: A current loop in a uniform magnetic field experiences a torque, and the direction of the torque is anticlockwise.

concepts.concept_title: Direction of Torque on a Current Loop
concepts.description: The direction of torque on a current loop is along the axis and tends to rotate the coil anticlockwise.

concepts.concept_title: Equal and Opposite Forces on Coil Arms
concepts.description: Forces on coil arms BC and DA are equal, opposite, and collinear along the axis, resulting in no net force or torque.
Forces on coil arms AB and CD form a couple, resulting in torque τ = F sinθ.
Magnetic moment m = I A, where I is current and A is area of the coil.
Torque τ = m × B, where m is magnetic moment and B is magnetic field.

concepts.concept_title: Dimensions and Units of Magnetic Moment
concepts.description: Magnetic moment has dimensions [A][L^2] and unit Am^2.
A magnetic dipole (coil) equilibrium occurs when its moment m is parallel or antiparallel to the external magnetic field B.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Number of Turns on Magnetic Moment
concepts.description: Magnetic moment of a coil with N closely wound turns is m = N I A.
The magnetic moment of a coil is given by m = N I A, where N is the number of turns, I is the current, and A is the area of the coil.

The torque on a coil in a magnetic field is given by τ = m B sin θ, where m is the magnetic moment, B is the magnetic field strength, and θ is the angle between the magnetic moment and the magnetic field.

concepts.concept_title: Right-Hand Thumb Rule for Magnetic Field
concepts.description: The direction of the magnetic field is given by the right-hand thumb rule, where the fingers point in the direction of the current and the palm points in the direction of the magnetic field.

concepts.concept_title: Angular Speed of a Rotating Coil
concepts.description: The angular speed of a rotating coil is given by I ωdω=m Bsinθdθ, where I is the moment of inertia, ω is the angular speed, m is the magnetic moment, B is the magnetic field strength, and θ is the angle between the magnetic moment and the magnetic field.

concepts.concept_title: Stable Equilibrium of a Current-Carrying Loop
concepts.description: The orientation of stable equilibrium of a current-carrying loop in a uniform external magnetic field is when the area vector of the loop is in the direction of the external magnetic field.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Flux Maximum for a Current-Carrying Loop
concepts.description: The flux of the total field (external field + field produced by the loop) is maximum when the area vector of the loop is in the direction of the external magnetic field.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field due to a Circular Current Loop
concepts.description: The magnetic field produced by a circular loop is similar to that of an electric dipole. It can be calculated using the formula B = 0 / 2(x2 +R2)3/2 or simplified as B = 0 / 2x3 for distances much larger than the loop radius.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Moment of a Current Loop
concepts.description: The magnetic moment of a current loop is defined as m = IA, where A is the area of the loop.

The magnetic field of a current loop can be described using an electric dipole analogy. Substituting µ with 1/ε₀ and p with m, the magnetic field equation can be transformed into the equation for an electric dipole field.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field of a Magnetic Dipole
concepts.description: The magnetic field of a magnetic dipole is given by B ≃ 0 / 4πx3 at large distances from the dipole.

concepts.concept_title: Equivalence of a Planar Loop and a Magnetic Dipole
concepts.description: A planar loop can be considered equivalent to a magnetic dipole with a dipole moment m = I A.
Elementary particles such as electrons and protons have an intrinsic magnetic moment not accounted for by circulating currents.
A moving coil galvanometer is a device that measures electric current or voltage, consisting of a coil in a uniform radial magnetic field.
The magnetic torque acting on a coil is given by the equation NIBA, where N is the number of turns, I is the current, B is the magnetic field strength, and A is the area of the coil.

In a galvanometer, the magnetic torque is balanced by the spring torque to result in a steady angular deflection, which is given by the equation AB / k, where k is the torsional constant of the spring.
The current sensitivity of a galvanometer is defined as the ratio of the current to the deflection, and is given by the equation NAB / k.
An ammeter can be made by attaching a shunt resistance in parallel with the galvanometer coil, allowing most of the current to pass through the shunt and providing a low resistance path.
The resistance of an ammeter with a shunt resistance is given approximately by R_shunt / (R + R_shunt), where R is the resistance of the circuit and R_shunt is the shunt resistance.
The current sensitivity of a galvanometer is the deflection per unit current.
A galvanometer can be converted into an ammeter by attaching a shunt resistance in parallel.
A galvanometer can be used as a voltmeter by connecting a large resistance in series with it. Voltage sensitivity is the deflection per unit voltage.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship between Current and Voltage Sensitivity
concepts.description: The voltage sensitivity remains unchanged when the galvanometer is converted into an ammeter or a voltmeter.

concepts.concept_title: Measurement of Current Using an Ammeter
concepts.description: The current measured using an ammeter is the current that flows through it. The ammeter should have a very small resistance.
The total force on a moving charge is the sum of the magnetic and electric forces.

concepts.concept_title: Force on a Straight Conductor in a Uniform Magnetic Field
concepts.description: The force on a straight conductor is proportional to the current and the length, and is perpendicular to both.
The frequency of uniform circular motion of a charge in a uniform magnetic field, given by n = qB/(2πm).
The magnetic field dB due to a current element dl at a point P is given by dB = (μ x dl x r) / (4πr^3 I).

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field Due to a Circular Coil
concepts.description: The magnitude of the magnetic field B at a distance x from the center of a circular coil is given by B = (μ I) / (2(x^2 + R^2)^(3/2))
The line integral of the magnetic field B around a closed loop is proportional to the net current I enclosed by the loop, ∫Bdl = (μ x I) where μ is the magnetic permeability.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field Due to a Long Straight Wire
concepts.description: The magnitude of the magnetic field B at a distance R from a long straight wire carrying a current I is given by B = (μ I) / (2πR).
The magnitude of the magnetic field B inside a long solenoid carrying a current I is given by B = μnI.
Parallel currents attract, while anti-parallel currents repel.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Moment of a Planar Loop
concepts.description: The magnetic moment of a planar loop carrying a current I is given by m = N I A.
The torque on a loop carrying a current I in a magnetic field B is given by τ = m × B.

concepts.concept_title: Equilibrium Deflection in a Moving Coil Galvanometer
concepts.description: The equilibrium deflection φ in a moving coil galvanometer is given by kφ = NIAB.

concepts.concept_title: Conversion of a Moving Coil Galvanometer to an Ammeter
concepts.description: A moving coil galvanometer can be converted to an ammeter by introducing a shunt resistance r.
Magnetic field is a vector quantity that surrounds charged particles and exerts a force on them. It is created by the motion of charges.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field due to a Long Straight Wire
concepts.description: The magnetic field at a point due to a long straight wire is inversely proportional to the distance from the wire and is perpendicular to the direction of the current.
The direction of the magnetic field due to a current-carrying wire can be determined using the right-hand rule.
Magnetic moment is a vector quantity that describes the strength and orientation of a magnet.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field due to a Circular Coil
concepts.description: The magnetic field at the center of a circular coil is proportional to the number of turns and the current passing through it.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field due to a Solenoid
concepts.description: The magnetic field inside a solenoid is proportional to the current passing through it.
The Biot-Savart law describes the magnetic field due to a small element of a current-carrying wire.
Ampere's circuital law relates the magnetic field to the current passing through the loop.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Force on a Current-Carrying Wire
concepts.description: The magnetic force on a current-carrying wire is proportional to the current, the length of the wire, and the magnetic field.
The Lorentz force is the force exerted on a charged particle due to the magnetic and electric fields.

The force on a current-carrying wire is estimated using the formula F = (B * I * L) / g, where F is the force, B is the magnetic field strength, I is the current, L is the length of the wire, and g is the magnetic permeability.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field Strength of a Solenoid
concepts.description: The magnetic field strength inside a solenoid is estimated using the formula B = μ * n * I, where B is the magnetic field strength, μ is the magnetic permeability, n is the number of turns per unit length, and I is the current.

The torque on a current-carrying coil in a magnetic field is calculated using the formula τ = n * N * I * B * cos(θ), where τ is the torque, n is the number of turns, N is the number of turns, I is the current, B is the magnetic field strength, and θ is the angle between the normal of the coil and the magnetic field.

concepts.concept_title: Current and Voltage Sensitivity of Moving Coil Meters
concepts.description: The current and voltage sensitivity of moving coil meters are calculated using the formulas Is = N * B * A / R and Vs = N * B * A / (R * N), where Is is the current sensitivity, Vs is the voltage sensitivity, N is the number of turns, B is the magnetic field strength, A is the area of the coil, and R is the spring constant.

concepts.concept_title: Motion of an Electron in a Magnetic Field
concepts.description: When an electron is shot into a magnetic field, it experiences a force perpendicular to its velocity and the magnetic field, causing it to move in a circular orbit with a radius of r = m * v / (e * B).

concepts.concept_title: Frequency of Revolution of an Electron
concepts.description: The frequency of revolution of an electron in a circular orbit is given by f = v / (2 * π * r), where f is the frequency, v is the velocity, and r is the radius of the orbit.

concepts.concept_title: Counter Torque on a Coil in a Magnetic Field
concepts.description: The counter torque required to prevent a coil from turning in a magnetic field is given by τ = N * I * B * cos(θ) * sin(θ), where τ is the counter torque, N is the number of turns, I is the current, B is the magnetic field strength, and θ is the angle between the normal of the coil and the magnetic field.
Magnetic phenomena are universal in nature, and magnetic fields exist in various sources, including the earth.
The earth behaves as a magnet with its magnetic field pointing approximately from the geographic south to the north.
A bar magnet has a north pole and a south pole, and like poles repel while opposite poles attract.
Magnetic monopoles, or isolated north or south poles, do not exist, and each pole has an opposite pole.
Magnets can be made from iron and its alloys, and iron filings around a magnet exhibit a pattern consistent with magnetic poles.

concepts.concept_title: Classification of materials based on magnetic properties
concepts.description: Materials can be classified as paramagnetic, diamagnetic, or ferromagnetic based on their magnetic properties.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic field lines of a magnet
concepts.description: Magnetic field lines form continuous closed loops around a magnet. They are a visual representation of the magnetic field and its properties.

Magnetic field lines have several properties, including forming continuous closed loops, tangents representing the direction of the magnetic field, and the strength of the magnetic field indicated by the number of lines per unit area.
The direction of the magnetic field is represented by the tangent to the magnetic field line at a given point.
The strength of the magnetic field is indicated by the number of magnetic field lines per unit area. More lines per unit area indicate a stronger magnetic field.
key_terms: number of lines per unit area

concepts.description: Magnetic field lines do not intersect as it would result in a non-unique magnetic field direction at the point of intersection.
A bar magnet can be thought of as a large number of circulating currents in analogy with a solenoid, and its resemblance to a magnetic dipole.

concepts.concept_title: Analogous behavior of bar magnets and solenoids
concepts.description: When a bar magnet is cut in half, it behaves similarly to a solenoid, having continuous field lines emerging from one face and entering into the other face.
key_terms: analogous behavior of bar magnets and solenoids

concepts.concept_title: Calculation with bar magnet and solenoid
concepts.description: The axial field of a finite solenoid can be used to demonstrate its similarity to that of a bar magnet, with the field lines remaining continuous and emerging from one face and entering into the other face.
key_terms: calculation with bar magnet and solenoid

concepts.concept_title: Analogy between bar magnet and solenoid
concepts.description: Bar magnet and solenoid produce similar magnetic fields, both in terms of magnitude and direction.

concepts.concept_title: Axial field of a finite solenoid
concepts.description: At large distances, the axial field of a finite solenoid resembles that of a bar magnet.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic moment of a bar magnet
concepts.description: The magnetic moment of a bar magnet is equal to the magnetic moment of an equivalent solenoid.

concepts.concept_title: Torque on a small compass needle
concepts.description: The torque on a small compass needle in a magnetic field is given by τ = m × B.
The magnetic potential energy of a small compass needle in a magnetic field is given by U = –mB cos θ.
The zero of potential energy can be fixed at θ = 90°, i.e., when the needle is perpendicular to the field.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of cutting a bar magnet
concepts.description: If a bar magnet is cut into two pieces, each piece will act as a new magnet with north and south poles.

concepts.concept_title: Net force on a magnetized needle in a uniform field
concepts.description: A magnetized needle in a uniform magnetic field experiences no net force, but a torque.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of a non-uniform magnetic field on a magnetized needle
concepts.description: A magnetized needle in a non-uniform magnetic field experiences both force and torque.
A toroid, unlike other magnetic configurations, may have no north and south poles.
Magnetization can be detected by observing the force between two identical iron bars.
The magnetic field at large distances due to a bar magnet can be obtained from the electric field due to an electric dipole by certain replacements.

concepts.concept_title: Equatorial Field of a Bar Magnet
concepts.description: The equatorial field of a bar magnet at a distance r is given by m / (4πr^3), where m is the magnetic moment.

concepts.concept_title: Axial Field of a Bar Magnet
concepts.description: The axial field of a bar magnet at a distance r is given by 2m / (4πr^3), where m is the magnetic moment.

concepts.concept_title: Dipole Analogy in Electricity and Magnetism
concepts.description: The dipole analogy suggests that the magnetic field due to a magnetic dipole is similar to the electric field due to an electric dipole.

concepts.concept_title: Potential Energy of a Magnetic Dipole in an External Field
concepts.description: The potential energy of a magnetic dipole in an external field is given by -m.B, where m is the magnetic moment and B is the external field.

concepts.concept_title: Equilibrium of a Magnetic Dipole in an External Field
concepts.description: A magnetic dipole is in stable equilibrium when its magnetic moment is parallel to the external field, and in unstable equilibrium when its magnetic moment is antiparallel to the external field.
Magnetic field lines do not start or end at any point, similar to electric field lines.
Magnetic field lines form closed loops, and their density indicates the field strength.
The net magnetic flux through any closed surface is zero due to the absence of isolated magnetic poles.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Flux through a Closed Surface
concepts.description: Magnetic flux through a closed surface is defined as the dot product of the magnetic field and area vector.

concepts.concept_title: Net Magnetic Flux through a Closed Surface
concepts.description: The net magnetic flux through any closed surface is zero due to the absence of isolated magnetic poles.

concepts.concept_title: Arrangement of Dipoles and Current Loops
concepts.description: All magnetic phenomena can be explained in terms of an arrangement of dipoles and/or current loops.
Electrostatic field lines can never form closed loops, either in empty space or when the loop encloses charges.
Magnetic field lines can never emanate from a point and always form closed loops around a region across which a current is passing.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field Lines and Gauss's Law
concepts.description: The net flux of magnetic field through any closed surface must always be zero, i.e., as many field lines seem to enter the surface as the number of lines leaving it.
Magnetic field lines (like electric field lines) can never cross each other because otherwise the direction of field at the point of intersection is ambiguous.
Magnetic field lines form closed loops around a region across which a current is passing, in contrast to electrostatic field lines.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field Lines Outside a Solenoid
concepts.description: Magnetic field lines due to a solenoid, at its ends and outside, cannot be completely straight and confined, and should curve out at both ends to form closed loops.

concepts.concept_title: Direction of Magnetic Field Lines Inside a Bar Magnet
concepts.description: Not all magnetic field lines emanate out of a north pole or converge into a south pole, and the net flux of the field is zero around both the N-pole and the S-pole.
Magnetic field lines can never be precisely straight at the ends between two pole pieces, and some fringing of lines is inevitable.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field Lines and Moving Charged Particles
concepts.description: Magnetic field lines represent the direction along which a small magnetised needle aligns at every point, but they do not necessarily represent the lines of force on a moving charged particle.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Force Always Normal to B
concepts.description: Magnetic force exerted by magnetic field on a moving charge is always normal to the magnetic field direction.
Magnetic flux through any closed surface is always zero due to the absence of magnetic monopoles.

concepts.concept_title: Self-Force and Self-Torque Due to Magnetic Field
concepts.description: An element of a bar magnet or a current-carrying wire cannot exert a force or torque on itself due to its own magnetic field.

concepts.concept_title: Force and Torque Between Two Elements of a Wire
concepts.description: An element of a current-carrying wire can exert a force and torque on another element of the same wire due to the magnetic field generated by each element.

concepts.concept_title: Zero Net Charge Does Not Imply Zero Magnetic Moment
concepts.description: A system can have a non-zero net magnetic moment even if its net charge is zero, due to the alignment of magnetic moments of individual particles.
Magnetisation of a sample is the net magnetic moment per unit volume of the sample.
Magnetic field within a material core is proportional to the magnetisation of the material, given by the relation B = μM.
Magnetic intensity H is a vector field defined as the magnetising field that would produce a unit magnetic moment in the material.
The total magnetic field B is the sum of the external magnetic field H and the magnetization M.
The magnetic susceptibility χ is a dimensionless quantity that measures the response of a magnetic material to an external field.
Paramagnetic materials have a positive and small magnetic susceptibility, resulting in magnetization in the same direction as the external field.
Diamagnetic materials have a negative magnetic susceptibility, resulting in magnetization in the opposite direction of the external field.
The relative magnetic permeability µ is a dimensionless quantity that relates the magnetic field H and the total magnetic field B.
The magnetic permeability µ0 is a physical constant that represents the permeability of free space.
The magnetizing current I is the additional current required in the absence of the core material to produce the same magnetic field B.
Ferromagnetic materials have a large and positive magnetic susceptibility, resulting in strong magnetization and permanent alignment of magnetic moments.
Diamagnetic substances tend to move from a stronger to a weaker external magnetic field, repelling magnetic field lines.
Bismuth, copper, lead, silicon, nitrogen (at STP), water, and sodium chloride are examples of diamagnetic materials.
Electrons in an atom orbiting the nucleus possess orbital angular momentum, contributing to diamagnetism.
Lenz's law explains the induced current in electrons orbiting an atom, leading to diamagnetic effects in materials.
Superconductors exhibit perfect diamagnetism when cooled to low temperatures, repelling magnetic field lines completely.
Paramagnetic substances get weakly magnetised when placed in an external magnetic field and tend to move from weak to strong fields.
Individual atoms of paramagnetic materials possess a permanent magnetic dipole moment.
At low temperatures and in strong external fields, paramagnetic materials exhibit net magnetisation due to aligned atomic dipole moments.

concepts.concept_title: Concentration of Magnetic Field Lines Inside Paramagnetic Material
concepts.description: In a paramagnetic material, external magnetic field lines get concentrated inside the material, enhancing the field.

concepts.concept_title: Movement of Paramagnetic Material in Non-Uniform Magnetic Field
concepts.description: Paramagnetic material tends to move from weak to strong magnetic field areas in a non-uniform magnetic field.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetisation and Temperature Relationship in Paramagnetic Materials
concepts.description: Magnetisation of paramagnetic material increases as temperature decreases or magnetic field increases.

concepts.concept_title: Saturation of Magnetisation in Paramagnetic Materials
concepts.description: Magnetisation reaches saturation when all dipoles in paramagnetic material are perfectly aligned with the external field.
Individual atoms in ferromagnetic materials possess a dipole moment similar to paramagnetic materials.
Ferromagnetic materials spontaneously align their dipole moments in domains over a macroscopic volume.

concepts.concept_title: Motion of Domains in Ferromagnetic Materials
concepts.description: When a magnetic field is applied, ferromagnetic domains orient and grow, leading to bulk magnetisation.

concepts.concept_title: Field Lines Concentration in Ferromagnetic Materials
concepts.description: Ferromagnetic materials have highly concentrated field lines, resulting in magnetic attraction towards strong fields.

concepts.concept_title: PERSISTENCE OF MAGNETISATION IN FERROMAGNETIC MATERIALS
concepts.description: In some ferromagnetic materials, magnetisation persists even after the external magnetic field is removed.
Ferromagnetic materials exhibit magnetization under an external field which disappears upon removal of the field.
The relative magnetic permeability is a dimensionless value greater than 1000 for ferromagnetic materials.
Ferromagnetism disappears with an increase in temperature, eventually becoming a paramagnet.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Poles and Magnetic Field Intensity
concepts.description: Magnetic poles repel or attract due to the interaction of magnetic field intensity.
The torque on a bar magnet is given by m × B, where m is its dipole moment and B is the magnetic field.

concepts.concept_title: Potential Energy of a Bar Magnet
concepts.description: The potential energy of a bar magnet in a uniform magnetic field is given by –m.B.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field at a Distance from a Bar Magnet
concepts.description: The magnetic field at a distance r from a bar magnet is given by two equations: B = 0/2πr3 along the axis and B = –0/4πr3 along the equator.
The net magnetic flux through any closed surface is zero.
The magnetic intensity is defined as B/H and the magnetisation is its dipole moment per unit volume.
For linear materials, M = cH, and the magnetic susceptibility c is related to the relative magnetic permeability.
Magnetic materials are classified into three categories: diamagnetic, paramagnetic, and ferromagnetic.
The following physical quantities and units are defined: permeability, magnetic field, magnetic moment, magnetic flux density, magnetisation, magnetic intensity, and magnetic susceptibility.
Magnetic permeability is a scalar quantity that represents the ratio of magnetic flux density to magnetic field strength.
Magnetic susceptibility is a measurement of how much a material is susceptible to a magnetic field, and is represented by the symbol χ.
The permeability of free space (μ0) is a constant that relates the magnetic flux density to the magnetic field strength.
Paramagnetic materials have positive magnetic susceptibility, while diamagnetic materials have negative magnetic susceptibility.
Diamagnetism is the phenomenon where a material is weakly repelled by a magnetic field.
Magnetic field lines are continuous and form closed loops, and the Lorentz force equation describes the force experienced by a charged particle in a magnetic field.
The torque on a magnetic dipole is given by the equation τ = m × B, where τ is the torque, m is the magnetic moment, and B is the magnetic field strength.

concepts.concept_title: Stable and Unstable Equilibrium of a Bar Magnet
concepts.description: A bar magnet can experience stable or unstable equilibrium in a magnetic field, depending on its orientation.
Classical theories are insufficient to explain certain phenomena, such as the behavior of superconductors, which are explained by quantum-mechanical theories like the BCS theory.

concepts.concept_title: Equilibrium and Potential Energy in a Magnetic System
concepts.description: A system in equilibrium has zero net force or torque, and its potential energy is a measure of its stored energy.
The magnetic moment of a solenoid is dependent on the number of turns, area of cross-section, and current flowing through it.

concepts.concept_title: Torque on a Solenoid in a Uniform Magnetic Field
concepts.description: The torque experienced by a solenoid in a uniform magnetic field is given by the product of its magnetic moment and the component of the magnetic field perpendicular to its axis.

concepts.concept_title: Work Done in Rotating a Bar Magnet in a Magnetic Field
concepts.description: The work done in rotating a bar magnet in a magnetic field is given by the product of its magnetic moment and the angle between its axis and the magnetic field.
key_terms: angle between axis and magnetic field

concepts.concept_title: Torque on a Bar Magnet in a Uniform Magnetic Field
concepts.description: The torque on a bar magnet in a uniform magnetic field is given by the product of its magnetic moment and the sine of the angle between its axis and the magnetic field.

concepts.concept_title: Magnetic Field Due to a Bar Magnet
concepts.description: The magnetic field due to a bar magnet has both radial and tangential components, with the radial component decreasing with distance from the magnet.
The phenomenon where an electric current is generated in a conductor due to changing magnetic fields.
When a bar magnet moves relative to a wire loop, an electric current is induced in the loop.
Electromagnetic induction has practical applications in the development of generators, transformers, and other electrical devices.
Faraday demonstrated electromagnetic induction by moving a bar magnet relative to a wire loop and observing the induced electric current.
Joseph Henry made important improvements in electromagnets and invented an electromagnetic motor and a new telegraph.
Self-induction is the property of a coil to oppose changes in electric current.
A galvanometer is a device used to measure electric current, which can detect the presence of an electric current in a coil.
An electromotive force is induced in a coil due to motion relative to a magnetic field, causing a current to flow.
The direction of the induced current is such that it opposes the change in magnetic flux that caused it.
When a coil is moving in a magnetic field or is stationary in a changing magnetic field, an electromotive force is induced in it due to the changing magnetic flux.
A change in the magnetic flux through a coil induces an electromotive force, which causes a current to flow.
An electromotive force is induced in a moving conductor by the changing magnetic flux through it.

concepts.concept_title: The Effect of a Changing Magnetic Field
concepts.description: A changing magnetic field induces an electromotive force in a stationary conductor, causing a current to flow.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Iron Rod on Electromagnetic Induction
concepts.description: The presence of an iron rod increases the magnetic flux through the coils, resulting in a larger electromotive force and induced current.
Magnetic flux is defined as F = B . A = BA cos q, where B is the magnetic field, A is the plane area, q is the angle between them.
Magnetic flux through a curved surface or non-uniform field can be calculated using the formula F = (cid:229) B . dA.
The SI unit of magnetic flux is weber (Wb) or tesla meter squared (T m2).
Faraday stated that an electromotive force (emf) is induced in a coil when the magnetic flux through the coil changes with time.
A change in magnetic flux through a circuit induces an electromotive force (emf) in it.
The magnitude of the induced emf in a circuit is equal to the time rate of change of magnetic flux through the circuit.

concepts.concept_title: Induced Emf due to Change in Magnetic Flux
concepts.description: The induced emf is given by e = – dΦ/dt, where Φ is the magnetic flux and t is time.
The total induced emf in a coil with N turns is given by e = -N (dB/dt) A, where N is the number of turns, B is the magnetic field, and A is the area of the coil.
The induced emf can be increased by increasing the number of turns N of a coil, or by changing the area A or magnetic field B of the coil.

concepts.concept_title: Induced Emf due to Change in Angle
concepts.description: An emf is induced in a coil when the angle between the magnetic field B and the area A of the coil changes.
An induced current can be demonstrated using a small bulb, which will glow when the relative motion between two coils causes an induced emf.
Faraday's law states that the magnitude of the induced emf in a coil is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux through the coil.

concepts.concept_title: Laws Governing the Change in Magnetic Flux
concepts.description: The change in magnetic flux through a coil is governed by Faraday's law, where the magnitude of the induced emf is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux.
Lenz's law states that the polarity of the induced emf is such that it tends to produce a current which opposes the change in magnetic flux that produced it.
The magnitude of the induced voltage is given by the equation ΔΦ_B / Δt, and the magnitude of the induced current is given by Ohm's law, I = V/R.

Lenz's law can be applied to understand the direction of the induced current in a coil when the magnetic flux through it changes, and to understand the effect of the change in the magnetic flux on the current.
Lenz's law states that the direction of the induced emf opposes the change in magnetic flux. This law is crucial in understanding the behavior of induced currents in various scenarios.
The direction of the induced current can be determined using Lenz's law, which states that the current will flow to oppose the change in magnetic flux.

concepts.concept_title: Dissipation of Energy by Joule Heating
concepts.description: The energy spent by a person moving a magnet is dissipated by Joule heating produced by the induced current in the coil.

concepts.concept_title: Determining the Direction of Induced Current in Loops of Different Shapes
concepts.description: The direction of the induced current in loops of different shapes can be determined using Lenz's law, considering the change in magnetic flux.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Motion of Loops on Magnetic Flux
concepts.description: When a loop moves into or out of a magnetic field, its magnetic flux changes, resulting in the induction of current in the loop.

concepts.concept_title: No Induced Current for Stationsary Loops
concepts.description: If a closed loop is held stationary in a magnetic field, no current is induced in the loop.

concepts.concept_title: Only a changing magnetic flux induces current
concepts.description: A current is induced in a closed loop only if the magnetic flux through the loop changes with time, regardless of the strength of the magnet.

concepts.concept_title: No current is induced by changing electric flux
concepts.description: A changing electric flux does not induce a current in a closed loop, in contrast to changing magnetic flux.

concepts.concept_title: Motional emf induced by changing magnetic flux
concepts.description: When a conductor moves at a constant velocity in a uniform magnetic field, the change in magnetic flux through the conductor induces an electromotive force (emf), known as motional emf.
The motional emf induced in a conductor moving at a constant velocity v in a uniform magnetic field B is given by the expression emf = Blv, where l is the length of the conductor.

concepts.concept_title: Current induced in a conductor moving in a magnetic field
concepts.description: When a conductor with free charge carriers is moved at a constant velocity in a uniform magnetic field, the Lorentz force on the charge carriers induces a current in the conductor.

concepts.concept_title: Constant speed of induced emf in a moving rectangular loop
concepts.description: In a rectangular loop moving out of a uniform magnetic field region, the induced emf remains constant if the area of the loop is changing at a constant rate, which is the case for a rectangular loop.

concepts.concept_title: Polarity of a capacitor in relation to magnetic field
concepts.description: When a capacitor is placed in a magnetic field, the polarity of the capacitor can be determined by considering the direction of the magnetic field and the orientation of the capacitor plates.
The motional emf induced in a conductor moving in a magnetic field can be explained by considering the Lorentz force acting on the free charge carriers of the conductor.
The work done in moving a charge from one point to another is equal to the electric field multiplied by the charge, and given by W = qvBl.

concepts.concept_title: Electromotive force (emf) due to work done
concepts.description: The emf induced across a conductor is the work done per unit charge.
Free electrons in a conductor move towards the outer end due to the Lorentz force and get distributed over the ring, producing a separation of charges and an emf.

concepts.concept_title: Induced emf due to changing magnetic field
concepts.description: An electromotive force (emf) is induced in a stationary conductor when the magnetic field around it changes.

concepts.concept_title: Force on a charge in a magnetic field
concepts.description: The force on a charge in a magnetic field is given by F = q (E + v ´ B), which reduces to F = qE for a stationary conductor.

concepts.concept_title: Electromagnetic induction and relationship between electricity and magnetism
concepts.description: Electricity and magnetism are related, and a changing magnetic field can produce an electric field, leading to electromagnetic induction.

concepts.concept_title: Calculation of induced emf using rate of change of area of loop
concepts.description: The induced emf can be calculated by using the rate of change of area of the loop as given by B × (rate of change of area of loop).

concepts.concept_title: Expression for induced emf due to rotation of a conductor
concepts.description: The expression for induced emf due to the rotation of a conductor is given by ε = BωR^2 / 2.

concepts.concept_title: Induced Emf in a Rotating Conducting Wire
concepts.description: The induced emf in a rotating conducting wire is given by e = (1/2) ω B R2, where ω is the angular velocity, B is the magnetic field strength, and R is the radius of the wire.

concepts.concept_title: Induced Emf in a Sector of a Circle
concepts.description: The induced emf in a sector of a circle is given by e = B R2 dθ / dt, where B is the magnetic field strength, R is the radius of the circle, and dθ/dt is the angular velocity.
The flux through a coil is proportional to the current, i.e., Φ α I, and the induced emf in a coil is given by e = -dΦ / dt.
