Flux linkage is the total magnetic flux linked with all the turns of a coil, and is given by NΦ, where N is the number of turns and Φ is the flux through the coil.
Inductance is a scalar quantity that depends only on the geometry of the coil and intrinsic material properties, and is given by L = Φ / I.
The SI unit of inductance is the henry (H), which is named in honour of Joseph Henry.

Mutual inductance is the property of one coil to induce an emf in another coil, and is given by M = NΦ / I, where M is the mutual inductance, N is the number of turns, Φ is the flux through the coil, and I is the current.
The magnetic field within a solenoid is given by B = µn I, where I is the current flowing through the solenoid, n is the number of turns per unit length, and µ is the magnetic constant.
The flux linkage of a solenoid with a current I is given by N F = (n l) (π r^2) (µn I), where n l is the total number of turns, π is constant, r is the radius, and µ is the magnetic constant.

The mutual inductance of a solenoid is given by M = (n n) (π r^2) l / (n l) (π r^2) (µ I), where n and n l are the total number of turns of the coils, π is a constant, r is the radius, and µ is the magnetic constant.
The mutual inductance of two solenoids is given by M = M, where M and M are the mutual inductances of the two solenoids.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of medium on mutual inductance
concepts.description: The mutual inductance of a solenoid is affected by the presence of a medium with relative permeability µ, which is given by M = µ M.

concepts.concept_title: Mutual inductance dependence on separation and orientation
concepts.description: The mutual inductance of two coils, solenoids, etc., depends on their separation and relative orientation.
Mutual inductance is the phenomenon where a change in current in one coil induces an emf in another coil. It depends on the magnetic field and the number of turns.
The mutual inductance between two coils is given by M = dΦ/dI, where Φ is the flux and I is the current.
The mutual inductance can be derived using the equation Φ = πr2B, where Φ is the flux, r is the radius of the coil, and B is the magnetic field.
Self-inductance is a phenomenon where a change in current in a single coil induces an emf in the coil itself.
The self-inductance of a coil is given by L = NΦ/I, where L is the self-inductance, N is the number of turns, Φ is the flux linkage, and I is the current.
The self-inductance can be derived using the equation Φ = nIA, where Φ is the flux, n is the number of turns per unit length, I is the current, and A is the cross-sectional area.
key_terms: number of turns per unit length

concepts.description: The self-inductance of a coil depends on the permeability of the medium, which can be affected by filling the inside of the solenoid with a material of relative permeability.
Electromagnetic induction has an analogy with mass in mechanics, showing electrical inertia.

concepts.concept_title: Rate of Work Done in Establishing Current
concepts.description: The rate of work done in establishing current in a circuit is εI.
Inductive effect stores energy as magnetic potential energy, given by LI2 / 2.
Self-inductance L and mutual inductance M are related to the coils and their flux.
The magnetic energy stored in a solenoid is given by U = (1/2)LI2.

concepts.concept_title: Comparison of Magnetic and Electrostatic Energies
concepts.description: Magnetic and electrostatic energies are proportional to the square of the field strength in different regions.

concepts.concept_title: Energy per Unit Volume in Magnetic and Electric Fields
concepts.description: The energy per unit volume in magnetic and electric fields is B^2 / (2μ0) and D^2 / (2ε0), respectively.
The modern ac generator uses electromagnetic induction to produce alternating currents.

concepts.concept_title: Electromagnetic Induction in a Rotating Coil
concepts.description: A changing magnetic flux induces an emf in a rotating coil due to a changing effective area.
The induced emf in a conductor is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux through it.

concepts.concept_title: Maxwell's Equation and EMF Induced in a Coil
concepts.description: EMF induced in a coil due to change in flux is given by the rate of change of flux.
AC is generated when a coil rotates in a magnetic field at a constant angular speed, causing the induced emf to change periodically.

concepts.concept_title: Instantaneous Value of EMF in AC Generation
concepts.description: The instantaneous value of the emf in an AC generation is given by e = e sin wt.

concepts.concept_title: Periodicity of EMF in AC Generation
concepts.description: The periodicity of the emf in AC generation is directly related to the frequency of revolution of the coil.
AC has a periodically changing voltage and current, whereas DC has a constant voltage and current.
AC generators are classified based on their power source, including hydro-electric, thermal, and nuclear generators.
Electric power is generated by converting mechanical energy into electrical energy using electromagnetic induction.
Magnetic flux through a surface is defined as the product of the magnetic field and the area of the surface, with the angle between the field and the area taken into account.
The induced emf in a coil is directly proportional to the rate of change of flux through it.
The polarity of the induced emf opposes the change in magnetic flux that produces it.
The motional emf induced across a conductive rod moving through a magnetic field is given by the product of the magnetic field strength, length of the rod, and its velocity.
Inductance is the ratio of the flux-linkage to current in a coil.
Mutual inductance is the ratio of the emf induced in a coil to the rate of change of current in another nearby coil.
Self-inductance is a measure of the inertia of a coil against the change of current through it.

The self-inductance of a long solenoid with a magnetic core is given by the product of the relative permeability of the core, the area of the solenoid's cross-section, and the square of its length, divided by the magnetic constant.
AC generators convert mechanical energy into electrical energy using electromagnetic induction.
Magnetic flux is the force exerted by a magnetic field on a current-carrying loop. It is measured in webers.

concepts.concept_title: EMF induced in a closed circuit
concepts.description: In a closed circuit, electric currents are induced so as to oppose the changing magnetic flux as per the law of conservation of energy.

Motional emf and Faraday's law describe how an emf is induced in a loop due to a changing magnetic flux. Motional emf can be argued independently from Faraday's law using the Lorentz force on moving charges.
The motional emf is produced when a coil is turned at a certain rate in a uniform magnetic field and a velocity is applied.

Lorentz force and symmetry give a hint on the relevance of the principle of relativity for Faraday's law. This indicates that moving charges in static fields and static charges in time-varying fields are symmetric situations.
Lenz's law describes that the direction of induced current is opposite to the direction of the magnetic field that induced it.
Mutual inductance is the ratio of the change in emf to the change in the current through one coil, when the current in the other coil is changing.
Self-inductance is the ratio of the change in emf to the change in the current through a coil, when the current in the coil is changing.
The equation of motional emf is E = NBA(2πn) sin(2πnt), where E is the emf, N is the number of turns, B is the magnetic field, A is the area, n is the revolutions per second, and t is time.

concepts.concept_title: EMF in a Rod due to Changing Magnetic Field
concepts.description: A constant and uniform magnetic field induces emf in a rod when its length is changing.

concepts.concept_title: EMF Induced in a Falling Wire
concepts.description: A changing magnetic flux induces emf in a conductor when it is moving in a uniform magnetic field with a velocity perpendicular to the field direction.

concepts.concept_title: Instantaneous Value of EMF in a Falling Wire
concepts.description: The emf induced in a conductor moving in a magnetic field is proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux through the conductor.

concepts.concept_title: Direction of EMF in a Falling Wire
concepts.description: The direction of the emf induced in a conductor moving in a magnetic field is determined by Fleming's left-hand rule.

concepts.concept_title: Determination of Potential Difference in a Falling Wire
concepts.description: The potential difference across a conductor is determined by the difference of emf induced at the two ends.

concepts.concept_title: Estimation of Self-Inductance of a Circuit
concepts.description: The self-inductance of an inductor can be estimated using the rate of change of current in the circuit.

concepts.concept_title: Change of Flux Linkage due to Change in Current
concepts.description: The change of flux linkage through a coil is proportional to the change in current flowing through the coil.

concepts.concept_title: Mutual Inductance and Change in Flux Linkage
concepts.description: Mutual inductance is a measure of the change in flux linkage through a coil due to a change in current through a nearby coil.
Alternating current is a type of current that varies with time, commonly found in household electrical devices.
Alternating voltage is a type of voltage that varies with time, commonly found in household electrical devices.
Electrical energy can be transmitted as direct current (DC) or alternating current (AC), with AC being more commonly used.
Alternating current has several advantages, including efficient conversion from one voltage to another using transformers and economic transmission over long distances.
Alternating current exhibits characteristics that are exploited in various devices, such as radios and television sets.

concepts.concept_title: Ac Voltage Applied to a Resistor
concepts.description: When ac voltage is applied to a resistor, the current through the resistor is sinusoidally varying.

concepts.concept_title: Kirchhoff's Loop Rule for AC Circuits
concepts.description: Kirchhoff's loop rule can be applied to AC circuits to find the value of current through a resistor.

concepts.concept_title: Current through a Resistor in an AC Circuit
concepts.description: The current through a resistor in an AC circuit is directly proportional to the resistance and the amplitude of the applied voltage.

concepts.concept_title: Instantaneous Current and Voltage in a Pure Resistor
concepts.description: The current and voltage in a pure resistor are sinusoidally varying and in phase with each other.
The average value of instantaneous current over a cycle is zero.

concepts.concept_title: Joule Heating and Energy Dissipation in a Resistor
concepts.description: Electric energy is dissipated in a resistor when an alternating current passes through it due to Joule heating.

concepts.concept_title: Instantaneous Power Dissipated in a Resistor
concepts.description: The instantaneous power dissipated in a resistor is given by p = i^2 R.

concepts.concept_title: Average Power Dissipation in a Resistor
concepts.description: The average power dissipation in a resistor is given by p_avg = I^2 R.
RMS current is defined as the effective value of current, which is related to the peak current by I = i / 2.

Alternating current (AC) is characterized by its root mean square (RMS) values, similar to DC current. RMS values are used to derive equations for power and relation between current and voltage in AC circuits.
Average power (P) in AC circuits is given by P = I^2R, where I is the RMS current and R is the resistance.
RMS voltage (V) is given by V = 0.707 v, where v is the peak voltage.

concepts.concept_title: Relation between AC Current and Voltage
concepts.description: The relation between AC current and voltage is given by V = IR, where V is the RMS voltage and I is the RMS current.
The peak voltage is given by v = sqrt(2)V, where V is the RMS voltage.

Phasors are vectors that represent AC quantities and are used to analyze AC circuits. Phasors facilitate the use of phasor diagrams to show the phase relationship between voltage and current in AC circuits.
Phasor diagrams show the phase relationship between voltage and current in AC circuits using phasors.
A circuit containing an inductor with negligible resistance is known as a purely inductive AC circuit.

concepts.concept_title: Kirchhoff's Loop Rule for AC Circuit
concepts.description: Kirchhoff's loop rule is used to analyze AC circuits and can be expressed mathematically as ∑ε(t) = 0.
Self-induced emf in an inductor is given by the equation: v - L = 0.
Lenz's law states that the negative sign follows from Lenz's law, indicating that the direction of self-induced emf is opposite to the change in current.
The phase of voltage and current in an AC circuit is related by the equation: v and i are in phase.

concepts.concept_title: Amplitude of Current in a Purely Inductive AC Circuit
concepts.description: The amplitude of current in a purely inductive AC circuit is given by: i = v / L.
Inductive reactance is the ratio of inductive reactance to the amplitude of current and is given by: X = ωL.

concepts.concept_title: Limitation of Current by Inductive Reactance
concepts.description: Inductive reactance limits the current in a purely inductive circuit in the same way as resistance limits the current in a purely resistive circuit.

concepts.concept_title: Proportionality between Inductive Reactance and Frequency
concepts.description: Inductive reactance is directly proportional to the frequency of the current.

concepts.concept_title: Phase Difference between Voltage and Current
concepts.description: The current in a purely inductive AC circuit lags the voltage.
An inductor opposes changes in current by generating a back electromotive force.
An inductor has reactance that limits current similar to resistance in a dc circuit.
The instantaneous power supplied to an inductor is given by the product of instantaneous current and voltage.
The average power supplied to an inductor over a complete cycle is zero.
The inductive reactance of an inductor is given by the product of angular frequency and inductance.

concepts.concept_title: rms Current in a Pure Inductive Circuit
concepts.description: The rms current in a pure inductive circuit is given by the division of peak voltage by inductive reactance.

concepts.concept_title: Capacitor as a Purely Capacitive AC Circuit
concepts.description: A capacitor in an ac circuit will limit or regulate the current, but will not completely prevent the flow of charge.
The voltage across a capacitor in an ac circuit is given by the division of charge on the capacitor by capacitance.

concepts.concept_title: Current in a Capacitive AC Circuit
concepts.description: The current in a capacitive ac circuit is given by the product of angular frequency, capacitance, and the derivative of charge on the capacitor.
key_terms: current in a capacitive ac circuit

concepts.description: Capacitive reactance is defined as the opposition to the change in current in an AC circuit due to the presence of a capacitor.
The formula for capacitive reactance is X = 1 / (ωC), where ω is the angular frequency and C is the capacitance.
The capacitive reactance limits the amplitude of the current in a purely capacitive circuit and is inversely proportional to the frequency and capacitance.
The current phasor is π/2 ahead of the voltage phasor in a purely capacitive circuit.

concepts.concept_title: Instantaneous Power Supplied to a Capacitor
concepts.description: The instantaneous power supplied to a capacitor is given by pi = iv cos(ωt) sin(ωt), which averages to zero over a complete cycle.

concepts.concept_title: Observations for dc and ac Connections
concepts.description: A capacitor allows current to flow in an AC circuit but blocks DC current. The lamp connected in series with the capacitor will shine if the source is AC and will not glow if the source is DC.
Reducing the capacitance of a capacitor increases the capacitive reactance and reduces the current in the circuit.

concepts.concept_title: Capacitive Reactance at Twice the Frequency
concepts.description: If the frequency is doubled, the capacitive reactance is also doubled.

concepts.concept_title: RMS Current and Peak Current in a Capacitor
concepts.description: The RMS current and peak current in a capacitor can be calculated using the formulas Irms = I/R and Ip = 2 I/R.

concepts.concept_title: Capacitive Reactance in Purely Capacitive Circuit
concepts.description: The capacitive reactance in a purely capacitive circuit is inversely proportional to the capacitance and the angular frequency of the ac source.

concepts.concept_title: Phase Relationship in Purely Capacitive Circuit
concepts.description: In a purely capacitive circuit, the current is ahead of the voltage by pi/2 radians.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Doubling Frequency on Capacitive Reactance
concepts.description: When the frequency is doubled, the capacitive reactance is halved.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship Between Current and Frequency in Series LCR Circuit
concepts.description: When the frequency is doubled in a series LCR circuit, the current is also doubled, if the capacitive reactance is proportional to 1/frequency.

concepts.concept_title: Phase Difference in Series LCR Circuit
concepts.description: In a series LCR circuit, the current is phase-shifted with respect to the applied voltage by a phase angle f.
A phasor diagram can be used to represent the phase relationships between the voltages across the resistor, inductor, and capacitor in a series LCR circuit.

concepts.concept_title: Voltage Equation for Series LCR Circuit
concepts.description: The voltage equation for a series LCR circuit is v = v + v + v, where v is the voltage across the resistor, v is the voltage across the inductor, and v is the voltage across the capacitor.

concepts.concept_title: Phasor Representation of Voltages and Currents
concepts.description: In an LRC circuit, voltages and currents can be represented as phasors. The magnitude and direction of these phasors can be used to determine the amplitude and phase of the currents.
Impedance (Z) is defined as the ratio of voltage to current in an AC circuit and is represented by the equation Z = R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2.

concepts.concept_title: Phase Angle in an AC Circuit
concepts.description: The phase angle (θ) is the angle between the voltage and current phasors and can be determined using the equation tan(θ) = (X_L - X_C) / R.

Inductive reactance (X_L) is the opposition to the change in current due to the presence of an inductor and is given by X_L = ωL, while capacitive reactance (X_C) is the opposition to the change in voltage due to the presence of a capacitor and is given by X_C = 1/ωC.
Resonance occurs in an LCR circuit when the frequency of the applied voltage is equal to the natural frequency of the circuit, resulting in maximum current and maximum impedance.
A phenomenon where the amplitude of oscillation is large when a system is driven at a frequency near its natural frequency, resulting in maximum current amplitude.
The opposition to the flow of current in an RLC circuit, given by the equation Z = R2 + (XL - XC)^2/C.
The frequency at which the impedance of an RLC circuit is minimum, given by ω = 1/√(LC), resulting in maximum current amplitude.
The current amplitude in an RLC circuit at resonant frequency is given by i = v/R, where R is the resistance.
RLC circuits have practical applications in tuning mechanisms of radios and TVs, where the resonant frequency is adjusted to match the frequency of the received signal.
For a circuit to exhibit resonance, both inductance (L) and capacitance (C) must be present, resulting in cancellation of voltages across L and C.

concepts.concept_title: Paradox in Algebraic Sum of Voltages
concepts.description: The algebraic sum of voltages across the resistor and capacitor in an RLC circuit can be more than the source voltage, resolving a paradox.

concepts.concept_title: Impedance in a Parallel RLC Circuit
concepts.description: Impedance in a parallel RLC circuit is calculated using the formula Z = R2 + X2 = R2 + (2πfC)-2.

concepts.concept_title: Current in a Parallel RLC Circuit
concepts.description: The current in a parallel RLC circuit is calculated using the formula I = V/Z.

concepts.concept_title: Voltage Across Resistor and Capacitor in a Parallel RLC Circuit
concepts.description: The voltage across the resistor and capacitor in a parallel RLC circuit is calculated using the Pythagorean theorem.

concepts.concept_title: Instantaneous Power in a Series RLC Circuit
concepts.description: The instantaneous power in a series RLC circuit is given by p = vi = v sin(ωt) · i sin(ωt + φ).

concepts.concept_title: Average Power in a Series RLC Circuit
concepts.description: The average power in a series RLC circuit is given by P = VIcosφ.

concepts.concept_title: Power Factor in a Series RLC Circuit
concepts.description: The power factor in a series RLC circuit is given by cosφ.

concepts.concept_title: Power Dissipation in an LCR Series Circuit
concepts.description: Power is dissipated only in the resistor in an LCR series circuit.

concepts.concept_title: Power at Resonance in an LCR Circuit
concepts.description: The power at resonance in an LCR circuit is maximum.

concepts.concept_title: Power Factor and its Effect on Power Loss
concepts.description: When the power factor is low, it implies a large power loss in transmission as more current is required to supply the same power, resulting in increased power loss (I^2R).

concepts.concept_title: Improving Power Factor by Neutralizing Wattless Current
concepts.description: The power factor can be improved by neutralizing the lagging wattless current with a leading wattless current, which can be achieved by adding a capacitor of appropriate value in parallel.
The impedance of a circuit is given by Z = sqrt(R^2 + (X - X')^2) and the power factor is given by cos(phi) = R/Z.
The phase difference between the voltage and current can be calculated using f = tan^(-1)((X' - X)/R) and the power factor is given by cos(phi) = R/Z.

concepts.concept_title: Power Dissipation in an LCR Circuit
concepts.description: The power dissipated in an LCR circuit is given by P = I^2 * R, where I is the current and R is the resistance.
LCR circuits can be solved by using the formulas for impedance, phase difference, and power factor, and by applying the concept of resonance.
The frequency at which resonance occurs in an LC circuit is determined by the values of the inductor and capacitor.
At resonant condition, the impedance of an LC circuit is equal to the resistance.
The RMS current at resonance in an LC circuit is given by the formula I = V/Z, where V is the voltage and Z is the impedance.
The power dissipated at resonance in an LC circuit is given by the formula P = I^2*R, where I is the current and R is the resistance.
A transformer consists of two coils, primary and secondary, wound on a soft-iron core, and works on the principle of mutual induction.
The induced EMF in a transformer's secondary coil is given by the formula e = -N(df/dt), where N is the number of turns in the secondary and f is the flux in each turn.
Ideal transformer equations e = v, e = - N (dφ/dt), v = - N (dφ/dt), where v is voltage and φ is flux.

concepts.concept_title: Power Conservation in an Ideal Transformer
concepts.description: In an ideal transformer, power input equals power output, i.e., i v = i v.
For an ideal transformer, V s = Ł (N s /N p) ł V p and I s = Ł (N p /N s) ł I p.
Step-up transformer: voltage is stepped up, current is stepped down. Step-down transformer: voltage is stepped down, current is stepped up.
Some flux leakage occurs due to poor design of the core or air gaps in the core.
Energy is lost due to resistance of windings, I 2R.
Hysteresis loss is the energy dissipated due to the reversal of magnetisation in a magnetic material, resulting in heating. It is minimised by using a magnetic material with low hysteresis loss.
Eddy currents are induced in the iron core due to alternating magnetic flux, causing heating. The effect is reduced by using a laminated core.
An alternating voltage applied to a resistor drives a current that is in phase with the applied voltage. The average power loss is given by (1/2)i^2 R.
RMS values of alternating voltage and current are defined as 0.707 times the maximum values, allowing the calculation of average power loss and impedance.
An alternating voltage applied to a pure inductor drives a current that lags the voltage by π/2. The average power supplied to an inductor over one complete cycle is zero.
An alternating voltage applied to a pure capacitor drives a current that leads the voltage by π/2. The average power supplied to a capacitor over one complete cycle is zero.
In a series RLC circuit, the current is given by i = (V / Z) sin (ωt + φ), where V is the voltage, Z is the impedance, and φ is the phase angle.
The power factor is the cosine of the phase angle between voltage and current in an ac circuit, given by cos(φ).
In a purely inductive or capacitive circuit, the current is referred to as wattless, as no power is dissipated despite the flow of current.
Phasors are rotating vectors used to represent the phase relationships between voltage and current in an ac circuit.
Phasors are used to represent AC quantities like voltage and current, with magnitude representing amplitude and direction representing phase.
RMS (root mean square) value of AC voltage or current is related to its amplitude, with RMS voltage being half of the amplitude in magnitude.
Reactance is the opposition to change in current due to inductive or capacitive components in an AC circuit.
Impedance is the total opposition to the flow of current in an AC circuit, depending on elements present.
Resonant frequency is the frequency at which an LC circuit or series RLC circuit has maximum current, given by w = 0 for a series RLC circuit.
Quality factor is a measure of the sharpness of resonance in a series RLC circuit, given by Q = 0 = for a series RLC circuit.
Power factor is the ratio of real power to apparent power in an AC circuit, given by cosf, where f is the phase difference between voltage and current.
Step-up transformers increase voltage, while step-down transformers decrease voltage, by ratio of turns in primary and secondary coils.

concepts.concept_title: Definition of Ampere for Alternating Current
concepts.description: Alternating current ampere is defined as the current producing the same average heating effect as one ampere of direct current.
When adding voltages across different elements in an AC circuit, take care of their phases properly.
In a series RLC circuit, voltage combination is V1 + V2 for V1 and V2 out of phase by 90 degrees.
AC quantities are represented as vectors in a phasor diagram, with phases and amplitudes combining mathematically like rotating vectors.
Pure capacitances and inductances do not dissipate energy in an AC circuit, while resistive elements do.
Resonance occurs in an RLC circuit when the inductive reactance equals the capacitive reactance, and is not possible with only one of these elements.
The power factor in an RLC circuit measures how close the circuit is to expending maximum power.
Transformers change voltage and current in a circuit, while generators and motors transform energy from one form to another.
The rms value of current in an AC circuit is related to the peak current and the frequency of the supply.

concepts.concept_title: Net Power Consumed in AC Circuits
concepts.description: The net power consumed in an AC circuit over a full cycle depends on the resistance and reactance of the circuit.
The angular frequency of free oscillations in an LCR circuit is related to the inductance and capacitance of the circuit.

concepts.concept_title: Average Power Transferred in RLC Circuits
concepts.description: The average power transferred to an RLC circuit in one complete cycle depends on the resistance and reactance of the circuit.
A circuit resonates when the frequency of the driving source matches the natural frequency of the circuit, leading to maximum current amplitude and special impedance.

concepts.concept_title: Impedance in a tuned LC circuit
concepts.description: At resonance, the impedance of an LC circuit is at its minimum, resulting in maximum current amplitude through the circuit.

concepts.concept_title: LC circuit potential drops at resonance
concepts.description: At resonance frequency, the potential drops across inductor and capacitor in an LC circuit are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, resulting in a net potential drop of zero.

concepts.concept_title: Calculated rms potential drops across circuit elements
concepts.description: Using the given circuit parameters, the rms potential drops across inductor, capacitor and resistor can be calculated for the resonating frequency.
A concept introduced by Maxwell to explain the existence of electromagnetic waves. It is the current associated with the changing electric field.

concepts.concept_title: Ampere's Circuital Law and Its Application
concepts.description: A law that relates the magnetic field around a closed loop and the current passing through it. It can be used to find the magnetic field at a point outside a capacitor.
According to Maxwell's equations, time-varying electric and magnetic fields propagate in space as waves, which is confirmed experimentally by Hertz.
The speed of electromagnetic waves is very close to the speed of light and is a fundamental prediction of Maxwell's equations.
Electromagnetic waves span a broad spectrum, ranging from gamma rays to long radio waves, each with characteristic frequencies and wavelengths.

concepts.concept_title: Ampere's Circuital Law and Its Application
concepts.description: Ampere's Circuital Law states that the magnetic field generated by a current-carrying wire is directly proportional to the current and inversely proportional to the distance from the wire. It can be used to find the magnetic field at a point outside a current-carrying wire.

Ampere's circuital law has a limitation that it does not account for the magnetic field generated by changing electric fields. This is evident when using the law on different surfaces such as a parallel plate capacitor, where a calculated magnetic field is contradictory.

concepts.concept_title: Role of Electric Field in Generating Magnetic Field
concepts.description: The electric field passing through a surface between the plates of a capacitor plays a crucial role in generating a magnetic field. The electric field is perpendicular to the surface and has the same magnitude over the area of the capacitor plates.
Electric flux is the measure of the amount of electric field passing through a given surface area. In this context, it is used to find the missing term in Ampere's circuital law.

concepts.concept_title: Guessing the Missing Term in Ampere's Circuital Law
concepts.description: By carefully analyzing the surface S between the plates of a capacitor, it can be inferred that the missing term in Ampere's circuital law is the electric field. This field passes through the surface, generating a magnetic field.
The electric field between capacitor plates is given by E = (Q/ε₀A), where Q is the charge on the plates, ε₀ is the permittivity of free space and A is the area of the plates.
Displacement current is the time rate of change of electric field, given by i_d = ε₀ dΦ/dt, where Φ is the electric flux through a surface.
Conduction current is the current flowing through conductors, given by i_c = dQ/dt, where Q is the charge flowing through the conductor.
The total current passing through a surface is the sum of conduction current and displacement current, given by i = i_c + i_d.
The generalized Ampere's law includes the displacement current, and is given by ∇×B = μ₀(i_c + i_d) + μ₀ε₀(dΦ/dt), where Φ is the electric flux through a surface.
The change of electric flux over time in a closed loop is known as the displacement current, which is a source of magnetic field.

Ampere-Maxwell law relates the total current, including conduction and displacement current, with the magnetic field and gives a quantitative expression for the symmetry of time-dependent electric and magnetic fields.

concepts.concept_title: Symmetry of Electric and Magnetic Fields
concepts.description: Electric and magnetic fields are symmetrical, with time-dependent electric fields giving rise to magnetic fields and time-dependent magnetic fields giving rise to electric fields.
key_terms: Symmetry of Electric and Magnetic Fields

concepts.concept_title: Electric and Magnetic Fields giving rise to each other
concepts.description: Time-dependent electric fields give rise to magnetic fields, and time-dependent magnetic fields give rise to electric fields.

concepts.concept_title: Electromagnetic waves are produced by accelerating charges
concepts.description: Electromagnetic waves are produced by charges that are accelerating, not by stationary charges or charges in uniform motion.

concepts.concept_title: Energy of electromagnetic wave comes from the accelerated charge
concepts.description: The energy associated with the propagating electromagnetic wave comes at the expense of the energy of the source, which is the accelerated charge.

concepts.concept_title: Frequency of electromagnetic wave equals the frequency of oscillating charge
concepts.description: The frequency of the electromagnetic wave is equal to the frequency of oscillation of the accelerated charge.

concepts.concept_title: Electromagnetic waves have oscillating electric and magnetic fields
concepts.description: Electromagnetic waves propagate through space with oscillating electric and magnetic fields.

concepts.concept_title: Electric and magnetic fields in electromagnetic waves are perpendicular
concepts.description: Electric and magnetic fields in an electromagnetic wave are perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation.
Electromagnetic waves propagate in a medium without the involvement of material vibrations.
In electromagnetic waves, the electric and magnetic fields are perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation.
An electromagnetic wave with electric and magnetic fields varying sinusoidally along the direction of propagation.
The wave vector describes the direction of propagation of the electromagnetic wave.
The speed of propagation of an electromagnetic wave is given by ω/k.
ν = c/λ or λν = c.

concepts.concept_title: Relation Between Electric and Magnetic Fields
concepts.description: B = (E/c), where E is the electric field and B is the magnetic field.
Electromagnetic waves are self-sustaining oscillations of electric and magnetic fields.
The velocity of light in a medium depends on the electric and magnetic properties of the medium. It is given by v = c / √(με), where c is the velocity of light in vacuum.
The velocity of light in vacuum is a fundamental constant, equal to 3 × 10^8 m/s, and is used to define a standard of length.
Electromagnetic waves have the capability to carry energy from one place to another, making them crucial for technologies like radio and TV broadcasting.

concepts.concept_title: Amplitude and Magnitude of Electric Field
concepts.description: The magnitude of the electric field is given by E = |E| = E0 / √(με), where E0 is the amplitude of the electric field.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship between Electric and Magnetic Fields
concepts.description: The electric and magnetic fields in an electromagnetic wave are perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation.
The direction of the electric field in an electromagnetic wave is perpendicular to the direction of propagation and the direction of the magnetic field.
The magnetic field in an electromagnetic wave is given by B = B0 sin(kx-ωt), where B0 is the amplitude of the magnetic field and k and ω are constants.

concepts.concept_title: Frequency and Wavelength of Electromagnetic Wave
concepts.description: The frequency and wavelength of an electromagnetic wave are related by the equation λ = c / f, where c is the velocity of light in vacuum and f is the frequency.

concepts.concept_title: Expression for Electric Field in Electromagnetic Wave
concepts.description: The electric field in an electromagnetic wave is given by E = E0 sin(kx-ωt), where E0 is the amplitude of the electric field and k and ω are constants.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship between Electric and Magnetic Field Components
concepts.description: The electric and magnetic field components in an electromagnetic wave are perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation.
Electromagnetic waves include visible light, X-rays, gamma rays, radio waves, microwaves, ultraviolet and infrared waves.
Radio waves are produced by the accelerated motion of charges in conducting wires.

concepts.concept_title: Types of FM and AM Radio Waves
concepts.description: The FM (frequency modulated) radio band extends from 88 MHz to 108 MHz, while the AM (amplitude modulated) band is from 530 kHz to 1710 kHz.
Microwaves are produced by special vacuum tubes (called klystrons, magnetrons and Gunn diodes).
Infrared waves are produced by hot bodies and molecules. They are absorbed by water molecules, leading to an increase in thermal motion.
The energy emitted or received in the form of electromagnetic waves with different frequencies and wavelengths.
Different parts of the electromagnetic spectrum including radio waves, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays, each with distinct wavelengths and properties.
The limited range of wavelengths that the human eye can detect, and the existence of other animals that can perceive different ranges of electromagnetic radiation.
Infrared radiation emitted by objects and its detection using infrared detectors and electronic devices such as thermometers and light emitting diodes.
Production, detection, and properties of ultraviolet radiation, including its harmful effects on humans and its absorption by ordinary glass.
X-rays and their use in medical diagnostics and treatment for cancer.
Properties of gamma rays, their production, and their high-frequency radiation.

concepts.concept_title: Production and detection of electromagnetic waves
concepts.description: Electromagnetic waves are produced in nuclear reactions, radioactivity, and other sources, and can be detected using various methods.

concepts.concept_title: Types of electromagnetic waves and their characteristics
concepts.description: There are several types of electromagnetic waves, including radio waves, microwaves, infrared, light, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays, each with their own unique characteristics and applications.
Displacement current is an additional current that is introduced by Maxwell to remove inconsistency in Ampere's law, and is given by the equation i = eE/∂t.

concepts.concept_title: Production of electromagnetic waves by accelerating charge
concepts.description: An accelerating charge produces electromagnetic waves, and an electric charge oscillating harmonically with frequency n produces electromagnetic waves of the same frequency n.

Electric and magnetic fields oscillate sinusoidally in space and time in an electromagnetic wave, and the oscillating electric and magnetic fields are perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation.

concepts.concept_title: Relation between electric and magnetic fields in electromagnetic waves
concepts.description: The electric and magnetic fields in an electromagnetic wave are related by the equation E/B = c, where c is the speed of light in a vacuum.

concepts.concept_title: Speed of electromagnetic waves in vacuum
concepts.description: The speed of electromagnetic waves in vacuum is related to the free space permeability and permittivity constants as c = 1/√(με), where c is the speed of light in a vacuum.
The speed of electromagnetic waves in free space is the same as the speed of light, denoted by c.
The speed of electromagnetic waves in a material medium is given by v = c / sqrt(με), where μ is the permeability and ε is the permittivity of the medium.
The electromagnetic spectrum consists of various types of waves with different wavelengths, ranging from gamma rays to radio waves.
Electromagnetic waves interact with matter through their electric and magnetic fields, which can set charges in oscillation.
The wavelength of electromagnetic waves is often correlated with the characteristic size of the system that radiates, such as atomic nuclei or electrons.
Infrared waves can increase the internal energy and temperature of a substance by vibrating atoms or molecules.
The displacement current across the plates of a capacitor is given by I_d = ε ∂φ/∂t, where φ is the electric flux.
Kirchhoff's first rule (junction rule) states that the current entering a junction is equal to the current leaving the junction.
The rms value of the conduction current is related to the square root of the average value of the current squared.

concepts.concept_title: Equality of Displacement and Conduction Currents
concepts.description: Displacement and conduction currents are equal in an ideal capacitor.

concepts.concept_title: Amplitude of B-Field at a Point in a Capacitor
concepts.description: The amplitude of the B-field at a point between the plates of a capacitor can be calculated using the given formula.

concepts.concept_title: Common Physical Quantity for Different Waves
concepts.description: The speed of a wave, which is the common physical quantity for X-rays, red light, and radio waves, is the same as the speed of light in a vacuum.

concepts.concept_title: Direction of E and B Field Vectors
concepts.description: In an electromagnetic wave traveling in a vacuum, the E and B field vectors are perpendicular to each other and perpendicular to the direction of propagation.
The wavelength of an electromagnetic wave can be calculated using the speed of light in a vacuum and the frequency of the wave.
The wavelength band corresponding to a given frequency range can be determined using the formula: wavelength = c / frequency.
The frequency of electromagnetic waves produced by an oscillating charged particle is equal to the frequency of the particle's oscillation.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship Between E and B Field Amplitudes
concepts.description: The amplitude of the electric field is related to the amplitude of the magnetic field in a harmonic electromagnetic wave by the speed of light.

concepts.concept_title: Photon Energies in Different Parts of the Electromagnetic Spectrum
concepts.description: The photon energies in different parts of the electromagnetic spectrum can be calculated using the formula: energy = h * frequency.

concepts.concept_title: Average Energy Density of E and B Fields
concepts.description: The average energy density of the E field equals the average energy density of the B field in a plane electromagnetic wave.
The speed of light in vacuum is a measure of how fast light travels in a vacuum and is the highest speed attainable in nature.
A ray of light is a straight line path traveled by a light wave, assuming its wavelength is very small compared to the size of ordinary objects.

concepts.concept_title: Reflection of Light by a Plane Mirror
concepts.description: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence, and the incident ray, reflected ray, and normal to the reflecting surface lie in the same plane.

concepts.concept_title: Reflection of Light by a Curved Mirror
concepts.description: The normal to a curved mirror is along the radius of the mirror, and the incident ray, reflected ray, and normal lie in the same plane.
The geometric centre of a spherical mirror is called its pole.

concepts.concept_title: Optical centre of a spherical lens
concepts.description: The geometric centre of a spherical lens is called its optical centre.

concepts.concept_title: Principal axis of a spherical mirror
concepts.description: The line joining the pole and the centre of curvature of the spherical mirror is known as the principal axis.

concepts.concept_title: Principal axis of a spherical lens
concepts.description: The line joining the optical centre with its principal focus of a spherical lens is known as the principal axis.
All distances are measured from the pole of the mirror or the optical centre of the lens.
The heights measured upwards are positive and those measured downwards are negative.

concepts.concept_title: Focal length of a spherical mirror
concepts.description: The distance between the focus and the pole of the mirror is called the focal length.

concepts.concept_title: Radius of curvature and focal length
concepts.description: The focal length is half of the radius of curvature of the mirror.
The angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection.
Paraxial rays are rays that pass close to the principal axis of a spherical mirror. The path of a paraxial ray from an object to a mirror and its reflection is described.

Image formation at a spherical mirror involves the rays emanating from a point on an object and converging to or appearing to diverge from a point called the image. The image can be real or virtual based on whether the rays converge or appear to diverge.

The geometry of reflection involves the path of rays emanating from a point on an object, their reflection at a spherical mirror, and the formation of an image based on the intersection of these rays.
The mirror equation relates the object distance (u), image distance (v), and the focal length (f) of a spherical mirror. The equation is derived using similar triangles and the sign convention.
A sign convention is applied to assign positive or negative values to distances based on the direction of light travel from an object to a mirror.
For ray optics, the positive direction is taken as the direction of object movement. All distances and angles are measured from the pole (P) of the mirror.

The mirror equation describes the relationship between the object and image distances from a mirror. It is given by 1/u + 1/v - 1/f = 0, where u is the object distance, v is the image distance, and f is the focal length.
The magnification of an image formed by a mirror is given by m = -v/u, where m is the magnification, v is the image distance, and u is the object distance.
The linear magnification is defined as the ratio of the height of the image to the height of the object. It is given by h¢/h = -v/u.
Spherical mirrors can produce real or virtual images, and the mirror equation and magnification formula are valid for all cases.
Reducing the area of the reflecting surface of a mirror will reduce the intensity of the image but not its size.
Convex mirrors can form virtual images, and the mirror equation and magnification formula are valid for these cases as well.
The mirror formula relates the object distance u, image distance v, and focal length f of a mirror to the equation 1/f = 1/u + 1/v.
In a concave mirror, when the object distance is less than the focal length, the image formed is virtual, erect, and smaller than the object.
In a convex mirror, the image formed is always virtual, erect, and smaller than the object.
The shift in position of the image of an object moving towards a convex mirror increases as the object moves closer to the mirror.

concepts.concept_title: Angle of Incidence vs Angle of Refraction
concepts.description: The ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction is constant and depends on the refractive indices of the two media.
Snell's law relates the angle of incidence to the angle of refraction using the formula sin(i) / sin(r) = n, where n is the refractive index of the second medium.

Snell's law states that the ratio of the sines of the angles of incidence and refraction is equal to the ratio of the velocities of the two media, which is also equal to the inverse of the ratio of the refractive indices.
When light travels from a denser medium to a rarer medium, the refracted ray bends away from the normal.
The refractive index is a characteristic of the pair of media and depends on the wavelength of light, but is independent of the angle of incidence.
Optical density is a measure of the ratio of the speed of light in two media, which is similar to mass density.
When light passes from one medium to another, it is refracted according to the laws of refraction, which includes Snell's law.
When light travels from a denser medium to a rarer medium, a part of it is reflected back into the denser medium.

concepts.concept_title: Angle of Refraction vs Angle of Incidence
concepts.description: The angle of refraction increases as the angle of incidence increases, till the critical angle is reached.
Refraction of light is not possible beyond a certain angle of incidence when light passes from a denser to a rarer medium.
The minimum angle of incidence required for total internal reflection to occur when light passes from a denser to a rarer medium.
Using Snell's law to determine the maximum angle of incidence for refraction to occur when light passes from a denser to a rarer medium.
Relationship between the refractive index of a denser medium and its critical angle with respect to a rarer medium.
Measuring critical angle using a laser pointer and a glass beaker with clear water and adding a few drops of milk or other suspension.
Total internal reflection occurs when a light ray hits a medium boundary at an angle greater than the critical angle.
Prisms designed to bend rays by 90° or 180° make use of total internal reflection.

concepts.concept_title: Use of Prisms to Invert Images
concepts.description: Prisms can be used to invert images without changing their size using total internal reflection.
Optical fibres use total internal reflection to transmit light signals through long distances.
An optical fibre consists of a core and cladding with a higher refractive index in the core.
An optical fibre works by allowing a signal to undergo repeated total internal reflections along its length.
Optical fibres can be used to transport light signals without appreciable loss in intensity.
The critical angle for an optical fibre must be greater than the angle of incidence for total internal reflection to occur.

concepts.concept_title: Total Internal Reflection in Optical Fibres
concepts.description: Total internal reflection occurs in optical fibres when light travels through them, resulting in very little absorption of light.
Refraction at spherical interfaces follows the same laws as refraction at plane interfaces. The normal at the point of incidence is perpendicular to the tangent plane to the spherical surface.

concepts.concept_title: Snell's Law for Refraction at Spherical Surfaces
concepts.description: Snell's law can be applied at spherical surfaces, relating the angle of incidence to the angle of refraction.

concepts.concept_title: Geometry of Refraction at a Spherical Surface
concepts.description: The geometry of refraction at a spherical surface involves the use of small angle approximation and the formation of an image on the principal axis.
The lens maker's formula is derived from the application of the formula for image formation by a single spherical surface to the two surfaces of a lens.

concepts.concept_title: Refraction by a Curved Spherical Surface
concepts.description: A relation between object and image distance in terms of refractive index of the medium and radius of curvature of a curved spherical surface.
Formation of an image by a double convex lens through two refracting surfaces.
A lens is treated as a single refracting surface with equal distances from the two refracting surfaces.
The relation between the object distance, image distance, and focal length of a lens.

concepts.concept_title: Focus and Focal Length of a Lens
concepts.description: A lens has two foci, and the distance between the object and the focus is called the focal length.
The thin lens formula is a relationship between the object distance, image distance, and focal length of a lens. It is given by 1/v + 1/u = 1/f.
The focal points of a lens are the points where parallel rays of light converge after refraction. The focal length is the distance between the focal points and the optical center of the lens.
To draw a ray diagram, two rays are typically chosen: a ray parallel to the principal axis and a ray passing through the optical center, and a ray passing through the principal focus.

The sign convention for lenses is used to determine the sign of the image distance and magnification. A positive image distance corresponds to a real image, while a negative image distance corresponds to a virtual image.

The magnification of a lens is defined as the ratio of the image height to the object height. The sign of the magnification is positive for erect and virtual images and negative for inverted and real images.

The power of a lens is a measure of its ability to converge or diverge light. It is defined as the tangent of the angle by which it converges or diverges a beam of light parallel to the principal axis.

concepts.concept_title: Sign Convention for Power of a Lens
concepts.description: The power of a lens is positive for a converging lens and negative for a diverging lens.

concepts.concept_title: SI Unit for Power of a Lens
concepts.description: The SI unit for power of a lens is dioptre (D), where 1D = 1m–1.
The lens formula is given by 1/f = (n-1)/(R1-R2), where n is the refractive index and R1 and R2 are the radii of curvature.

concepts.concept_title: Combination of Thin Lenses in Contact
concepts.description: When two lenses are placed in contact, the final image is formed assuming the optical centres of the lenses to be coincident.

concepts.concept_title: Focal Length and Power of a Lens
concepts.description: The power of a lens is related to its focal length by the equation P = 1/f.

concepts.concept_title: Refraction Index and Lens Focal Length
concepts.description: The refraction index of a substance affects the focal length of a lens. The relationship between refraction index and focal length is given by 1/f = (n-1)/(R1-R2).
The image equation is given by 1/u + 1/v = 1/f, where u is the object distance, v is the image distance, and f is the focal length of the lens.
The effective focal length of a combination of thin lenses is given by 1/F = 1/f1 + 1/f2 + 1/f3 + ..., where F is the effective focal length and fj is the focal length of the jth lens.
The power of a lens is given by P = 1/f, where P is the power and f is the focal length of the lens.
The total magnification of a combination of lenses is given by M = M1 * M2 * M3 * ..., where Mj is the magnification of the jth lens.

The angle of deviation is given by d = i - r + (180 - e), where d is the angle of deviation, i is the angle of incidence, r is the angle of refraction at the first face, and e is the angle of emergence.

concepts.concept_title: Angle of total deviation and prism equation
concepts.description: Angle of total deviation depends on the angle of incidence, given by the equation d = i + e - A.
Minimum deviation occurs when the angle of incidence equals the angle of emergence, giving refractive index n = sin[(A+D)/2]/sin[A/2].

concepts.concept_title: Refractive index of a prism for small angle prism
concepts.description: For a small angle prism, the refractive index is given by n = (A + D)/2A and can be related to (n - 1)A.
The magnifying power of a microscope depends on the position of the object and the focal length of the lens.

A sign convention is used to indicate whether an object or image is real or virtual, and its distance from the lens. The object distance is defined as positive, the image distance is negative for virtual images, and the focal length is positive for convex lenses.
The magnification of a simple microscope is given by the formula m = (D/f) where D is the near point (25 cm) and f is the focal length of the lens.
The maximum magnification of a simple microscope is obtained when the image is at the near point, and it is given by m = 6 when the focal length is 5 cm.
Angular magnification is the ratio of the angle subtended by the image to the angle subtended by the object. For an image at infinity, the angular magnification is m = (D/f)
The angular magnification of a simple microscope is given by m = (D/f) when the image is at infinity.
A compound microscope is a microscope that uses two or more lenses to achieve higher magnification than a simple microscope.

A compound microscope consists of two lenses: the objective and the eyepiece. The objective lens forms a real, inverted, magnified image of the object, while the eyepiece lens functions as a simple magnifier, producing the final virtual image.

concepts.concept_title: Linear Magnification by the Objective Lens
concepts.description: The linear magnification due to the objective lens is given by the ratio of the size of the first image to the object size, and can be expressed using the formula m = (h¢/h) = (L/f)o, where h is the object size, h¢ is the size of the first image, f is the focal length of the objective, and L is the tube length.

concepts.concept_title: Angular Magnification by the Eyepiece Lens
concepts.description: The angular magnification due to the eyepiece lens is given by the formula m = D/(f + D), where D is the distance between the eyepiece and the final image, and f is the focal length of the eyepiece.

concepts.concept_title: Total Magnification of a Compound Microscope
concepts.description: The total magnification of a compound microscope is given by the product of the linear magnification due to the objective lens and the angular magnification due to the eyepiece lens, and can be expressed using the formula m = mL.
To achieve a large magnification with a compound microscope, the objective and eyepiece should have small focal lengths, and the tube length should be large.
The ratio of the angle subtended at the eye by the final image to the angle the object subtends at the eye or lens.
The ability to observe two objects distinctly, which are in very nearly the same direction, depends on the diameter of the objective.

concepts.concept_title: Focal Length and Aperture of Objective
concepts.description: The objective has a large focal length and a much larger aperture than the eyepiece in a telescope.
A telescope that uses a lens for the objective, used for both terrestrial and astronomical observations.
A telescope that uses a concave mirror for the objective, used to avoid chromatic aberration and mechanical support issues.
Large lenses tend to have chromatic aberration and distortions, making them difficult and expensive to make.
The ability to observe fainter objects depends on the area of the objective.
Refraction of light occurring through a medium with a critical angle of incidence greater than 90°, resulting in total reflection.
The relationship between the angles of incidence and refraction in a refracting medium, given by the equation n1*sin(θ1) = n2*sin(θ2).
A set of rules for assigning signs to measurements in ray optics, based on the direction of the incident light and the position of the object relative to the mirror or lens.

concepts.concept_title: Reflection by Concave and Convex Mirrors
concepts.description: The laws of reflection for concave and convex mirrors, with equations for object and image distances, and focal length.
The refraction of light through a prism with a given refractive index and angle of deviation, with an equation for the angle of minimum deviation.
The relationship between the object and image distances, and the focal length of a lens, given by the equation 1/u + 1/v = 1/f.
The refraction of light through a spherical interface between two media with different refractive indices, with an equation for the object and image distances.

concepts.concept_title: Maker's Formula for Lens Focal Length
concepts.description: The focal length of a lens is related to its radii of curvature. A positive focal length indicates a converging lens, while a negative focal length indicates a diverging lens.
The power of a lens is defined as the reciprocal of its focal length. The SI unit for power is the dioptre (D).

concepts.concept_title: Effective Focal Length of a Combination of Thin Lenses
concepts.description: The effective focal length of a combination of thin lenses is given by the sum of their individual focal lengths.

concepts.concept_title: Total Power of a Combination of Lenses
concepts.description: The total power of a combination of lenses is the sum of their individual powers.

concepts.concept_title: Dispersion and the Splitting of Light into its Constituent Colors
concepts.description: Dispersion occurs when light is split into its constituent colors due to differences in refractive indices of the medium.

concepts.concept_title: Magnifying Power of a Simple Microscope
concepts.description: The magnifying power of a simple microscope is given by the formula m = 1 + (D/f), where D is the least distance of distinct vision and f is the focal length of the lens.

concepts.concept_title: Magnifying Power of a Compound Microscope
concepts.description: The magnifying power of a compound microscope is given by the formula m = m × m, where m is the magnification due to the eyepiece and m is the magnification produced by the objective.
The magnifying power of a telescope is the ratio of the angle subtended at the eye by the image to the angle subtended at the eye by the object.
Study of the behavior of light as it passes through various media and devices.
When light hits a surface at an angle, it bounces back at the same angle of incidence.
Devices that refract (bend) light to form an image.
The distance between the lens and the point where parallel light rays converge.
Study of how light rays from an object are refracted and focused by a lens to form an image.
Devices that refract light in different directions to form images.
A formula to calculate the focal length of a lens in terms of its radii of curvature.
Devices that use lenses or prisms to magnify or project images.
A measure of how much a medium bends light.
Phenomenon where light is completely reflected back into a medium from an interface with a medium of higher refractive index.
Study of the behavior of light when it hits a mirror and is reflected back.
Study of how light rays from an object are reflected by a mirror to form an image.
Rules to assign signs to distances and heights in ray optics problems.

concepts.concept_title: Formation of Image by a Compound Microscope
concepts.description: A compound microscope consists of an objective lens and an eyepiece separated by a distance. Object is placed at a certain distance from the objective to obtain the final image at the least distance of distinct vision or at infinity.

concepts.concept_title: Magnifying Power of a Compound Microscope
concepts.description: The magnifying power of a compound microscope is calculated when the image is obtained at the least distance of distinct vision or at infinity. It depends on the focal length of the objective lens and the eyepiece.
The magnifying power of a telescope is calculated using the focal length of the objective lens and the eyepiece. It is used to observe distant objects.
The angular magnification of a telescope is calculated using the focal length of the objective lens and the eyepiece when viewing distant objects.

concepts.concept_title: Mirror Equation for Concave and Convex Mirrors
concepts.description: The mirror equation is used to deduce the properties of images formed by concave and convex mirrors. It helps in understanding the nature and location of images formed by these mirrors.
When observing an object through a glass slab, it appears raised due to refraction. The shift depends on the thickness of the slab and the refractive index of the glass.

concepts.concept_title: Total Internal Reflection in a Light Pipe
concepts.description: Total internal reflection occurs when light is incident from a denser medium to a rarer medium at a critical angle. It is used in the design of light pipes with total internal reflection.

concepts.concept_title: Minimum Focal Length of a Convex Lens
concepts.description: The minimum focal length of a convex lens required to obtain the image of an object on the opposite wall is half the distance between the object and the image.

concepts.concept_title: Convex Lens - Focal Length from Object Distance
concepts.description: The focal length of a convex lens can be determined using the object distance and the image distance separated by 20cm.

concepts.concept_title: Combination of Thin Lenses - Effective Focal Length
concepts.description: The effective focal length of a combination of thin lenses is determined by the individual focal lengths and their distances apart.
The magnification produced by a convex lens is determined by the object distance and its size compared to the image distance and size.

concepts.concept_title: Total Internal Reflection in a Prism
concepts.description: The angle of incidence required for total internal reflection in a prism is determined by the refractive index and the angle of the prism.

concepts.concept_title: Magnification and Angular Magnification in a Converging Lens
concepts.description: The magnification and angular magnification in a converging lens can be different, depending on the object distance and the position of the eye.

concepts.concept_title: Distance for Magnifying Power in a Convex Lens
concepts.description: The distance at which a convex lens should be held from an object to view it distinctly with the maximum possible magnifying power is determined by the focal length.

concepts.concept_title: Magnifying Power in a Simple Microscope
concepts.description: The magnifying power of a simple microscope is inversely proportional to the focal length of the lens.

concepts.concept_title: Objective and Eyepiece Focal Lengths in a Compound Microscope
concepts.description: Both the objective and eyepiece lenses in a compound microscope must have short focal lengths for clear viewing.
Angular magnification of a compound microscope is the ratio of the angle subtended by the final image at the eye to the angle subtended by the object at the eye.
Magnifying power of a compound microscope is the product of the two magnifications of the objective and eyepiece.
In a telescope, normal adjustment is achieved when the final image is formed at infinity.
Magnifying power of a telescope is the ratio of the angle subtended by the final image at the eye to the angle subtended by the object at the eye.
The separation between the objectives and eyepiece in a telescope is given by the difference between the focal length of the objective and the eyepiece.
Angular magnification of a telescope is the ratio of the angle subtended by the final image at the eye to the angle subtended by the object at the eye.
The height of the image formed by the objective lens in a telescope is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the objective lens and the eyepiece.
A Cassegrain telescope uses two mirrors to form an image at infinity.
The deflection of a mirror is related to the angle of deviation of a light beam passing through it.
The refractive index of a liquid can be determined by measuring the distance of a needle from a lens before and after immersion in the liquid.
Descartes' corpuscular model of light predicts that if a ray of light bends towards the normal on refraction, the speed of light is greater in the second medium.
Isaac Newton developed Descartes' corpuscular model, which predicted phenomena of reflection and refraction, and explained that light requires a medium for its propagation
Huygens proposed the wave theory of light, which explained phenomena of reflection and refraction, but predicted that the speed of light is less in the second medium on refraction
Foucault's experiment showed that the speed of light in water is less than in air, confirming the wave model's prediction
Young's interference experiment firmly established that light is a wave phenomenon, and the wavelength of visible light was measured to be extremely small

Geometrical optics is the branch of optics where light is assumed to travel in straight lines due to its small wavelength, and a ray is defined as the path of energy propagation in the limit of wavelength tending to zero
Maxwell's electromagnetic theory of light explained how light waves propagate through a vacuum, resolving the major difficulty of the wave theory
Maxwell predicted the existence of electromagnetic waves and derived the wave equation. He calculated the speed of electromagnetic waves in free space, close to the speed of light.

concepts.concept_title: Speed of light is a characteristic of electromagnetic waves
concepts.description: Maxwell showed that the speed of electromagnetic waves in free space is close to the speed of light, suggesting that light is an electromagnetic wave.

concepts.concept_title: Changing electric field produces a time and space varying magnetic field
concepts.description: According to Maxwell, a changing electric field produces a time and space varying magnetic field, which results in the propagation of electromagnetic waves.

concepts.concept_title: Changing magnetic field produces a time and space varying electric field
concepts.description: Maxwell showed that a changing magnetic field produces a time and space varying electric field, resulting in the propagation of electromagnetic waves.
Maxwell demonstrated that electromagnetic waves are transverse waves, as they vibrate in directions perpendicular to the direction of propagation.
Huygens principle states that wavefronts propagate outwards from a point source at a constant speed, and that all points on a wavefront oscillate in phase.

concepts.concept_title: Waves can be spherical or plane
concepts.description: Waves can be spherical, emanating from a point source, or plane, at a large distance from the source.
A wavefront is a surface of constant phase, where all points oscillate in phase.
Huygens principle is a geometrical construction to determine the shape of a wavefront at a later time.
Secondary wavelets are the wavelets emanating from each point of the wavefront, spreading out in all directions with the speed of the wave.
A common tangent to the spheres of radius vt drawn from each point on the wavefront gives the new position of the wavefront at a later time.
According to Huygens principle, the amplitude of secondary wavelets is maximum in the forward direction and zero in the backward direction.
Huygens principle is used to derive the laws of refraction, considering a plane wave propagating through a medium at an angle.

concepts.concept_title: Speed of Light in Different Media
concepts.description: The speed of light in medium 1 and medium 2 is denoted by v and v', respectively.

concepts.concept_title: Time Taken by Wavefront to Travel Distance
concepts.description: The time taken by the wavefront to travel a distance BC is denoted by t.

concepts.concept_title: Speed of light in a medium
concepts.description: The speed of light in a medium is given by BC = vt, where BC is the distance traveled and t is the time taken.
When a light ray passes from one medium to another, it bends due to change in speed.
Snell's law is given by n1 sin i = n2 sin r, where n1 and n2 are refractive indices of the two media and i and r are the angles of incidence and refraction, respectively.

concepts.concept_title: Wavelength of light in a medium
concepts.description: The wavelength of light in a medium is directly proportional to the distance traveled, i.e., λ = l / v, where λ is the wavelength and v is the speed of light.

concepts.concept_title: Refractive indices and their relation to speed and wavelength
concepts.description: The refractive indices of two media are related to the speed and wavelength of light in those media, n1 = c / v1 and n2 = c / v2.

concepts.concept_title: Refraction of a plane wave at a rarer medium
concepts.description: When a plane wave passes from one medium to a rarer medium, it speeds up and bends away from the normal.

concepts.concept_title: Frequency of light remains constant in refraction
concepts.description: The frequency of light remains the same while passing from one medium to another.
The law of refraction is expressed by the equation n sin i = n sin r.
The critical angle is the angle of incidence when the angle of refraction is 90 degrees.
Total internal reflection occurs when the angle of incidence is greater than the critical angle.
The angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection.
A plane wave incident at an angle i on a reflecting surface MN will have a reflected wavefront with equal angle of incidence and reflection.

concepts.concept_title: Faster and Slower Speeds Affecting Time in Refraction
concepts.description: Time taken by light in different paths in a medium is the same, despite differences in speed and path length.
Two sources of waves are coherent if the phase difference between their displacements does not change with time.
Reflected and refracted light have the same frequency as the incident frequency due to atomic interactions, and the energy carried by a wave depends on its amplitude, not speed.
Intensity of light in the photon picture is determined by the number of photons crossing an unit area per unit time.

The principle of superposition states that when two or more waves overlap in the same medium, the resultant displacement at any point in the medium is the algebraic sum of the displacements produced by the individual waves.
Constructive interference occurs when two or more waves overlap in the same medium and the resultant displacement is the maximum possible, resulting in a greater intensity.
Constructive interference occurs when the path difference between the two waves is equal to an integral multiple of the wavelength, resulting in a phase difference of 0, 2π, 4π, etc.
Destructive interference occurs when two or more waves overlap in the same medium and the resultant displacement is the minimum possible, resulting in zero intensity.
Destructive interference occurs when the path difference between the two waves is equal to an odd multiple of one half the wavelength, resulting in a phase difference of π, 3π, 5π, etc.
The intensity in an interference pattern is proportional to the square of the amplitude, and can be calculated using the formula I = 4I0, where I0 is the intensity produced by each individual source.
Constructive interference occurs when path difference is given by n l, resulting in maximum intensity. Maximum intensity occurs when f = 0, ± 2 p, ± 4 p, ...
Destructive interference occurs when path difference is given by (n + 1) l, resulting in zero intensity. Destructive interference occurs when f = ± p, ± 3p, ± 5p, ...

concepts.concept_title: Resultant Displacement due to Multiple Waves
concepts.description: Resultant displacement is given by y = 2 a cos (f/2) cos (wt + f/2), where a is the amplitude of the individual waves and f is the phase difference
Intesity is given by I = 4I cos2 (f/2) for coherent sources, resulting in stable interference pattern
Time-averaged intensity is given by I = 2I for incoherent sources resulting in varying interference pattern
Thomas Young's experiment is discussed where two pinholes are illuminated by a bright source to produce interference fringes on a screen.
Coherent sources are defined as two sources whose light waves are derived from the same original source, resulting in synchronized phase changes.
Constructive interference results in a bright region with equation = nl, while destructive interference results in a dark region with equation = (n+1/2)l.
Fringes are patterns of dark and bright bands resulting from interference or diffraction, with equal spacing between them.
Diffraction is a phenomenon exhibited by all types of waves, resulting in alternate dark and bright regions in the shadow of an opaque object.
Diffraction is the phenomenon where light bends around corners and enters regions where a shadow is expected.
When a parallel beam of light falls on a single narrow slit, it produces a broad pattern with a central bright region and alternate dark and bright regions.

concepts.concept_title: Conditions for Maxima in Single Slit Diffraction
concepts.description: The intensity of light at any point on the screen has a central maximum at q = 0 and other secondary maxima at q = (n+1/2)λ/a, where n is an integer.

concepts.concept_title: Conditions for Minima in Single Slit Diffraction
concepts.description: The minima (zero intensity) occur at q = nl/a, where n is an integer.
There is no specific, important physical difference between interference and diffraction; it's just a matter of usage.
The result of superposition of single-slit diffraction from each slit in a double-slit experiment.
The pattern of bright and dark bands observed on a screen when light passes through a narrow slit.
The principle that energy remains conserved in interference and diffraction phenomena, with energy reduced in one region and increased in another.
Not explicitly extracted (text was cutoff)
Wave equation describes the displacement of a point on a wave as a function of its position and time.
Angular frequency is the rate of change of phase of a wave and is related to the frequency by the equation ω = 2πf.
Amplitude is the maximum displacement of a point on a wave from its equilibrium position.
Wavelength is the distance between two consecutive points on a wave that are in phase with each other.
A transverse wave is a wave in which the displacement of a point on the wave is at right angles to the direction of propagation.
A plane polarised wave is a wave whose displacement is confined to a single plane.
An unpolarised wave is a wave whose displacement is randomly changing with time.
A linearly polarised wave is a wave whose displacement is at right angles to the direction of propagation.
A polaroid is a device that absorbs electric vectors parallel to the direction of aligned molecules.
The pass-axis of a polaroid is the direction perpendicular to the aligned molecules.

Malus' law states that the intensity of transmitted light after passing through two polaroids is I = I cos^2q, where I is the intensity after passing through the first polaroid and q is the angle between the pass-axes of the two polaroids.

concepts.concept_title: Intensity of Transmitted Light in Crossed Polaroids
concepts.description: The intensity of transmitted light when a polaroid sheet is rotated between two crossed polaroids is given by I = (I/4) sin^2(2q), where I is the intensity of polarised light after passing through the first polaroid and q is the angle between the pass-axes of the two polaroids.
Huygens' principle states that each point on a wavefront is a source of secondary waves, which add up to give the wavefront at a later time.
Huygens' construction is a method used to draw the wavefront at a later time by considering each point on the original wavefront as a source of secondary waves.
